                     ▼
Stage 1: groupBy + write parquet
  Tasks: 16    Duration: 9.7 s
  Shuffle Read: 120 MB   Output: 45 MB

The shuffle boundary between stages is where
data moves between executors.
blocks.caption: A job with two stages separated by a shuffle. Stage 0 reads and filters the data. Stage 1 aggregates and writes the result. The shuffle between them moves data so rows with the same groupBy key land on the same executor.

blocks.content: The SQL / DataFrame Tab

The SQL tab (also labeled SQL / DataFrame in newer Spark versions) shows the execution plans for your DataFrame operations. This is the most useful tab for understanding how Catalyst optimized your code. Each query appears with a visual DAG that shows the sequence of operations: scan, filter, project, exchange (shuffle), aggregate, and write. You can see which filters were pushed into the reader, which columns were pruned, and whether a broadcast join was used. You will learn to read these plans in detail in Module 3, but starting to explore this tab now builds familiarity.

blocks.title: Build the Spark UI habit early
blocks.content: Many junior engineers write PySpark code for months before opening the Spark UI. By that point, they have developed habits that produce slow pipelines without understanding why. If you start checking the Spark UI from your very first pipeline, you build an intuition for how your code translates to execution. When a senior engineer says 'check the DAG' or 'look at the stage duration,' you will know exactly where to look.
Other Tabs

rows: Cached DataFrames and their memory usage
When you use .cache() or .persist() and want to verify data is in memory
All Spark configuration properties, JVM settings, classpath
When debugging configuration issues or verifying which settings are active
Each executor's memory usage, task count, shuffle read/write, and GC time
When diagnosing performance problems or checking if executors are evenly loaded

blocks.content: At the junior level, the Jobs and Stages tabs are the ones you will use most. They answer the two most common questions: 'Did my code run successfully?' and 'Which part took the longest?' The SQL / DataFrame tab becomes essential when you start optimizing pipelines. The Storage, Environment, and Executors tabs are tools you will reach for as specific situations arise. For now, knowing they exist and where to find them is sufficient.

If you read older PySpark tutorials, Stack Overflow answers from before 2017, or legacy code at a company that has used Spark for a long time, you will encounter a different starting object: SparkContext, often stored in a variable called sc. The SparkContext was Spark's original entry point, and it is still running behind the scenes inside every SparkSession. Understanding the relationship between SparkContext and SparkSession helps you read older code, understand error messages that mention SparkContext, and appreciate why SparkSession was created.
The Historical Timeline

When Spark first launched, the only entry point was SparkContext. It could distribute data and run computations, but it had no concept of SQL, DataFrames, or structured schemas. When Spark added the DataFrame API in Spark 1.3, a new object called SQLContext was required to use it. When Spark added Hive integration, a third object called HiveContext appeared. By Spark 1.6, engineers had to juggle three separate context objects, each with its own configuration and lifecycle. Spark 2.0 solved this by introducing SparkSession as a unified wrapper around all three.

blocks.title: Evolution of Spark entry points
blocks.content:
Spark 1.0–1.2         Spark 1.3–1.6            Spark 2.0+
───────────         ────────────            ─────────

SparkContext         SparkContext             SparkSession
  (only option)      SQLContext                  (unified)
                     HiveContext                    │
                                                   │ wraps
   1 object           3 objects                    │
   no SQL             confusing              SparkContext
   no DataFrames      hard to configure      SQLContext
                                             HiveContext
                                             (all internal)
blocks.caption: Spark's entry point evolved from one limited object (SparkContext) to three confusing objects, then back to one unified object (SparkSession) that wraps all the functionality internally.

blocks.content: SparkSession Wraps SparkContext

The SparkSession does not replace SparkContext — it wraps it. When you create a SparkSession, a SparkContext is created automatically inside it. You can access it through spark.sparkContext if you need it, but for DataFrame and SQL operations — which is virtually everything in modern PySpark — you never touch SparkContext directly. The wrapping means that all the low-level functionality of SparkContext is still available, but you interact with it through the higher-level, more convenient SparkSession interface.

from pyspark.sql import SparkSession

spark = SparkSession.builder.appName('nordgrid').getOrCreate()

# SparkContext is inside the SparkSession
sc = spark.sparkContext

print(f"SparkSession type: {type(spark)}")
print(f"SparkContext type: {type(sc)}")
print(f"App name via session: {spark.conf.get('spark.app.name')}")
print(f"App name via context: {sc.appName}")
print(f"Same app? {spark.conf.get('spark.app.name') == sc.appName}")

SparkSession type: <class 'pyspark.sql.session.SparkSession'>
SparkContext type: <class 'pyspark.context.SparkContext'>
App name via session: nordgrid
App name via context: nordgrid
Same app? True
When You See SparkContext in the Wild

You will encounter SparkContext in three situations. First, in legacy code written before Spark 2.0 that starts with SparkContext and SparkConf instead of SparkSession.builder. Second, in low-level operations like creating RDDs (Spark's original data abstraction, which predates DataFrames) or setting broadcast variables and accumulators. Third, in error messages — Spark's internal errors sometimes reference SparkContext even when you only used SparkSession, because SparkContext is doing the actual work underneath.

left.label: Legacy Pattern (pre-2.0)
points: from pyspark import SparkContext, SparkConf
conf = SparkConf().setAppName('nordgrid')
sc = SparkContext(conf=conf)
sqlContext = SQLContext(sc)
df = sqlContext.read.csv(...)

right.label: Modern Pattern (2.0+)
points: from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
spark = SparkSession.builder \
.appName('nordgrid') \
.getOrCreate()
df = spark.read.csv(...)

blocks.title: Always use SparkSession
blocks.content: Never create a SparkContext directly in new code. Always use SparkSession.builder. If you need SparkContext for a low-level operation, access it through spark.sparkContext. Creating a standalone SparkContext alongside a SparkSession causes configuration conflicts and can crash your application. One entry point, one session, one context inside it.

Spark can run in two fundamentally different modes: local mode, where everything happens on a single machine, and cluster mode, where work is distributed across multiple machines. The mode is determined by the master setting. In Module 1, you learned that Spark uses a driver and executors. In local mode, the driver and all executors run as threads within a single process on your laptop. In cluster mode, the driver runs on one machine and executors run on separate machines managed by a cluster manager. The same PySpark code works in both modes — only the master setting changes.
Local Mode

Local mode is what you use during development and testing. When you set master('local[*]'), Spark runs the driver and executors in the same process on your machine. The [*] means Spark should use all available CPU cores. You can also specify a number: local[4] uses 4 cores, local[1] uses 1 core (completely sequential, useful for debugging). Local mode is fast to start, requires no cluster infrastructure, and behaves identically to cluster mode for DataFrame operations. NordGrid's engineers develop and test their pipelines in local mode on their laptops before deploying to the production cluster.

from pyspark.sql import SparkSession

# Local mode: all processing on your machine
# local[*] = use all available CPU cores
spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .appName('nordgrid-dev') \
    .master('local[*]') \
    .getOrCreate()

print(f"Master: {spark.conf.get('spark.master')}")
print(f"Cores:  {spark.sparkContext.defaultParallelism}")

Master: local[*]
Cores:  8
Cluster Mode

In cluster mode, the master setting points to a cluster manager that allocates resources across multiple machines. The three common cluster managers each have their own master URL format. On NordGrid's Kubernetes cluster, the master URL points to the Kubernetes API server. When the pipeline is submitted, the cluster manager creates executor pods on available nodes, and Spark distributes the work across them. The same pipeline code that ran on a laptop in local mode now processes data in parallel across eight machines.
Local vs Cluster — What Changes, What Stays

The beauty of Spark's architecture is that your PySpark code does not change between local and cluster mode. The DataFrame operations, the filter conditions, the column transformations, the joins — all of it is identical. What changes is where the work physically runs and how many resources are available. A filter that processes 500,000 meter readings in 2 seconds on your laptop processes the same readings in 0.3 seconds on an 8-machine cluster because the work is parallelized across more cores and the data is read from multiple disks simultaneously.

left.label: Local Mode
points: Driver and executors in one process
Uses your laptop's cores and RAM
No network overhead between tasks
Fast to start, no infrastructure needed
Limited by one machine's resources
Used for development and testing

right.label: Cluster Mode
points: Driver on one machine, executors on many
Uses cluster's combined cores and RAM
Executors communicate over the network
Requires cluster manager and multiple nodes
Scales horizontally with more nodes
Used for production workloads

blocks.title: Develop locally, deploy to cluster
blocks.content: NordGrid's standard workflow is: write and test in local mode on a laptop with a small sample of data, then deploy to the cluster for the full dataset. The code is identical — only the master and the data paths change. This workflow catches logic errors cheaply on the laptop and reserves the cluster for production-scale processing.

Spark has hundreds of configuration properties that control how it behaves: how much memory executors receive, how many partitions a shuffle creates, which compression codec to use for output files, and much more. As a junior engineer, you do not need to know all of them. You need to know that configuration exists, how to read and set properties, and which three or four properties you are most likely to encounter in your first weeks on the job. This lesson covers the configuration system and the properties that matter most at the junior level.
Configuration as Key-Value Pairs

Every Spark configuration property is a key-value pair where the key is a dotted string and the value is a string. The key follows a naming convention: it starts with spark, then a category (sql, executor, driver, shuffle, etc.), then the specific setting. For example, spark.sql.shuffle.partitions controls how many partitions a shuffle operation creates, and its default value is the string '200'. You set properties during session creation with config() or at runtime with spark.conf.set().

from pyspark.sql import SparkSession

# Setting configuration during session creation
spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .appName('nordgrid-daily-pipeline') \
    .master('local[*]') \
    .config('spark.sql.shuffle.partitions', '16') \
    .config('spark.driver.memory', '4g') \
    .getOrCreate()

# Reading configuration values
print(f"Shuffle partitions: {spark.conf.get('spark.sql.shuffle.partitions')}")
print(f"Driver memory:      {spark.conf.get('spark.driver.memory')}")

# Setting configuration at runtime (only works for some properties)
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.shuffle.partitions', '32')
print(f"Updated shuffle:    {spark.conf.get('spark.sql.shuffle.partitions')}")

Shuffle partitions: 16
Driver memory:      4g
Updated shuffle:    32
The Properties You Need to Know

Out of Spark's hundreds of configuration properties, four appear in almost every junior engineer's first week. These are the properties you will see in code reviews, in cluster configuration files, and in conversations with your team. Each one controls a fundamental aspect of how Spark allocates resources and processes data. Learning these four gives you enough configuration literacy to understand your team's existing pipelines and to make sensible choices in your own code.

rows: Number of partitions created during shuffle operations (groupBy, join)
Default of 200 is too high for small datasets (creates many tiny tasks) and too low for very large ones. NordGrid uses 16 for local development and 200–400 for production.
Amount of memory each executor receives
1GB is rarely enough for real workloads. NordGrid's production executors use 16g. Too little memory causes out-of-memory errors; too much wastes cluster resources.
Amount of memory the driver process receives
Matters when you call collect() or toPandas() to bring data to the driver. NordGrid uses 4g for most pipelines.
Number of CPU cores each executor uses
More cores per executor means more tasks run in parallel on each machine. NordGrid uses 4 cores per executor.

blocks.content: Mutable vs Immutable Properties

Not all properties can be changed after the session starts. Memory settings (spark.executor.memory, spark.driver.memory) are immutable — they must be set before the session is created because they determine how much memory the JVM allocates at startup. SQL settings (spark.sql.shuffle.partitions) are mutable — they can be changed at any time using spark.conf.set(). If you try to set an immutable property at runtime, Spark either ignores the change or throws a warning.

blocks.title: Common mistake
blocks.content: Setting spark.executor.memory inside spark.conf.set() after the session is already running. The memory was already allocated when the session started — changing the property string does nothing. Memory properties must be set in the builder chain or in spark-submit arguments before the session is created.

Spark configuration can be set in four different places: Spark's built-in defaults, a configuration file called spark-defaults.conf, the spark-submit command line, and the SparkSession.builder in your code. When the same property is set in more than one place, Spark follows a strict precedence order to decide which value wins. Understanding this order prevents the frustrating situation where you change a property in your code but Spark seems to ignore it — because a higher-precedence source is overriding it.
The Four Layers

The four configuration sources form a stack where each layer overrides the one below it. The built-in defaults sit at the bottom — they are always present but have the lowest priority. The spark-defaults.conf file overrides the defaults. The spark-submit command-line flags override the file. And the SparkSession.builder.config() calls in your code override everything. This means your code always has the final word, but it also means that if you do not set a property in code, the value comes from whichever lower layer set it most recently.

blocks.title: Configuration precedence stack
blocks.content:
Highest priority (wins)
───────────────────────
┌───────────────────────┐
│  SparkSession.builder  │  ← Your code (.config())
│  .config(key, value)   │
├───────────────────────┤
│  spark-submit flags    │  ← Command line (--conf)
│  --conf key=value      │
├───────────────────────┤
│  spark-defaults.conf   │  ← Cluster config file
│  key  value            │
├───────────────────────┤
│  Built-in defaults     │  ← Spark source code
│  (e.g. shuffle = 200)  │
└───────────────────────┘

Lowest priority (overridden)
blocks.caption: Each layer overrides the one below it. Your code has the highest priority. Built-in defaults have the lowest. If a property is set in multiple places, the highest layer wins.

blocks.content: A NordGrid Precedence Scenario

Here is a concrete example that illustrates how the layers interact. Spark's built-in default for spark.sql.shuffle.partitions is 200. NordGrid's cluster administrator set it to 400 in spark-defaults.conf because the production data is large enough to benefit from more partitions. A developer working locally submits the same script with --conf spark.sql.shuffle.partitions=8 to keep local testing fast. The script itself does not set the property in code. What value does Spark use?

# The script does NOT set shuffle.partitions in code
spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .appName('nordgrid-daily-pipeline') \
    .getOrCreate()

# What value is active?
print(spark.conf.get('spark.sql.shuffle.partitions'))

# On the cluster (submitted without --conf flag):
#   -> 400 (from spark-defaults.conf, overriding the default of 200)
#
# On the developer's laptop (submitted with --conf flag):
#   -> 8 (from spark-submit --conf, overriding spark-defaults.conf)
#
# If the code had .config('spark.sql.shuffle.partitions', '16'):
#   -> 16 (code overrides everything)
Checking Active Configuration

When you are unsure which value is active for a property, you can check it at runtime using spark.conf.get(). You can also see all active configuration in the Spark UI's Environment tab, which lists every property and its current value. This is especially useful when debugging configuration issues on a shared cluster where spark-defaults.conf may contain settings you did not expect and where spark-submit flags may have been added by an orchestrator.

# Check specific properties
print(f"Shuffle:     {spark.conf.get('spark.sql.shuffle.partitions')}")
print(f"Exec memory: {spark.conf.get('spark.executor.memory')}")
print(f"Driver mem:  {spark.conf.get('spark.driver.memory')}")

# Get all SQL-related properties
for item in spark.conf.getAll:
    if 'sql' in item[0].lower():
        print(f"{item[0]} = {item[1]}")

blocks.title: Use the right layer for the right purpose
blocks.content: Built-in defaults are Spark's safe starting point. spark-defaults.conf is for cluster-wide policies that apply to all applications (set by your platform team). spark-submit flags are for per-job overrides that vary between runs (like memory for a large backfill vs a daily batch). Code-level config() is for properties that are intrinsic to the pipeline's logic (like shuffle partitions tuned for a specific data size). Putting the right setting in the right layer keeps your configuration maintainable.

During development, you run PySpark code directly on your laptop in a notebook or IDE. But in production, pipelines run on a cluster, submitted by an orchestrator like Apache Airflow. The bridge between your Python script and the cluster is a command-line tool called spark-submit. It takes your script, sends it to the cluster manager, requests resources for the driver and executors, and starts the Spark application. Understanding spark-submit is essential because it is how every NordGrid pipeline reaches the production cluster.
The Basic Command

The simplest spark-submit command takes just a master URL and a script path. The master tells spark-submit where the cluster manager is running. The script path is your PySpark Python file. Everything else is optional — but in practice, you almost always specify at least the application name, executor count, executor memory, and driver memory, because the defaults are rarely appropriate for production workloads.

# Minimal spark-submit
spark-submit \
  --master yarn \
  pipeline.py

# Production spark-submit with common options
spark-submit \
  --master yarn \
  --deploy-mode cluster \
  --name nordgrid-daily-meters-bronze-to-silver \
  --num-executors 8 \
  --executor-cores 4 \
  --executor-memory 16g \
  --driver-memory 4g \
  --conf spark.sql.shuffle.partitions=200 \
  --conf spark.sql.parquet.compression.codec=snappy \
  pipeline.py
Key Arguments

rows: Cluster manager URL (yarn, k8s, spark, local)
Where the driver runs: client (your machine) or cluster (a cluster node)
Application name (same as appName in code)
How many executor processes to request
Additional Python files or .zip archives to distribute

blocks.content: Client Mode vs Cluster Mode

The deploy-mode argument determines where the driver runs. In client mode (the default), the driver runs on the machine where you typed the spark-submit command. The driver's output prints to your terminal, and if you close the terminal, the job dies. In cluster mode, the driver runs on one of the cluster's nodes, managed by the cluster manager. The job continues even if you disconnect. NordGrid uses cluster mode for all production pipelines because Airflow submits the command and then monitors it — the driver does not need to stay on Airflow's machine.

left.label: Client Mode (--deploy-mode client)
points: Driver runs on the submitting machine
Output prints to your terminal
Job dies if you disconnect
Good for interactive debugging
Default mode

right.label: Cluster Mode (--deploy-mode cluster)
points: Driver runs on a cluster node
Output goes to cluster logs
Job continues after you disconnect
Required for production pipelines
Must be explicitly requested

blocks.content: NordGrid's Production Submit

In production, NordGrid's Airflow DAG constructs the spark-submit command and executes it. The command includes all resource settings, configuration overrides, and the path to the pipeline script stored in the team's artifact repository. The pipeline script itself does not set master or memory — those come from spark-submit, which is controlled by the Airflow task. This separation means the same script can run locally with different resources or on the cluster with production resources, without changing a line of code.

# NordGrid production submit (executed by Airflow)
spark-submit \
  --master k8s://https://cluster-api.nordgrid.internal:443 \
  --deploy-mode cluster \
  --name nordgrid-daily-meters-bronze-to-silver-2026-03-22 \
  --num-executors 8 \
  --executor-cores 4 \
  --executor-memory 16g \
  --driver-memory 4g \
  --conf spark.sql.shuffle.partitions=200 \
  --conf spark.sql.parquet.compression.codec=snappy \
  --conf spark.kubernetes.container.image=nordgrid/spark:3.5.1 \
  s3://nordgrid-pipelines/meters/bronze_to_silver.py

blocks.title: spark-submit is the production deployment tool
blocks.content: If you think of your PySpark script as the recipe, then spark-submit is the kitchen it runs in. The recipe says what to cook. The spark-submit command decides how many chefs (executors), how big the kitchen is (memory), and which restaurant location to use (master). Separating the recipe from the kitchen means you can run the same recipe in a small test kitchen (local mode, 4g memory) or a large production kitchen (cluster mode, 128g total memory) without changing the recipe.

Every SparkSession has a lifecycle: it is created, it is used for all pipeline operations, and it is stopped when the work is done. The start and use phases are intuitive — you have seen them in every code example so far. The stop phase is the one that junior engineers most often forget, and forgetting it has real consequences: an unstopped session holds cluster resources that other applications cannot use. This lesson covers the complete lifecycle pattern, explains why stopping matters, and shows how NordGrid structures its pipeline scripts to ensure clean session management.
The Three Phases

blocks.title: SparkSession lifecycle
blocks.content:
Phase 1: START                Phase 2: USE               Phase 3: STOP
──────────────                ──────────────               ───────────

SparkSession.builder    spark.read.csv(...)         spark.stop()
  .appName(...)         df.filter(...)                  │
  .config(...)          df.groupBy(...)                 │
  .getOrCreate()        df.write.parquet(...)           ▼
       │                spark.sql(...)            Resources
       ▼                       │                 released
  Resources                    ▼
  allocated             Pipeline runs              Executors
  Executors             on the cluster             terminated
  created                                          UI shuts down
blocks.caption: The session lifecycle follows a clear pattern: create the session and allocate resources, use the session for all pipeline operations, then stop the session to release resources.

blocks.content: Why Stopping Matters

When a SparkSession is created, the cluster manager allocates executors — real processes running on real machines, consuming real memory and CPU. Those resources are reserved for your application and cannot be used by anyone else. If your pipeline finishes its work but does not call spark.stop(), the executors remain allocated. On a shared cluster, this means other pipelines that need resources cannot get them. On a cloud cluster, it means you are paying for machines that are sitting idle.

At NordGrid, an unreleased session caused a production incident in the team's early days. A developer's debugging script allocated 8 executors with 16GB each — 128GB of cluster memory — and left the session running overnight. The next morning's production batch could not get enough resources to start and missed the 8 AM deadline. After that incident, the team added spark.stop() to every pipeline template and every code review checklist.
The Standard Pipeline Template

NordGrid's pipelines follow a standard template that guarantees the session is stopped even if the pipeline fails. The template wraps the pipeline logic in a try/finally block: the try block contains all the pipeline operations, and the finally block calls spark.stop(). The finally block executes regardless of whether the pipeline succeeds or raises an exception. This ensures resources are always released.

from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, year

def create_session():
    return SparkSession.builder \
        .appName('nordgrid-daily-meters-bronze-to-silver') \
        .config('spark.sql.shuffle.partitions', '16') \
        .getOrCreate()

def run_pipeline(spark):
    """Main pipeline logic: read, transform, write."""
    df = spark.read.parquet('bronze/meters/')
    df = df.filter(col('daily_kw') > 0)
    df = df.withColumn('year', year(col('date')))
    df = df.select('meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'year', 'region')
    df.write.mode('overwrite').parquet('silver/meters_clean/')
    print("Pipeline complete.")

if __name__ == '__main__':
    spark = create_session()
    try:
        run_pipeline(spark)
    finally:
        spark.stop()  # ALWAYS runs, even if run_pipeline() crashes
        print("Session stopped. Resources released.")

Pipeline complete.
Session stopped. Resources released.
Notebooks vs Scripts

The lifecycle pattern differs slightly between scripts and interactive notebooks. In a script, you create the session at the top, use it, and stop it at the bottom. The script runs once and exits. In a Jupyter or Databricks notebook, the session is typically created in the first cell and stays alive for the entire interactive session. You do not stop it between cells because you need it for the next cell. The session is stopped when you close the notebook or explicitly call spark.stop() in a final cell. NordGrid's developers use notebooks for exploration and scripts for production — the lifecycle pattern adapts to each context.

left.label: Script Lifecycle
points: Create session at the top
Use session in the pipeline function
Stop session in a finally block
Runs once, exits, resources released
Used in production

right.label: Notebook Lifecycle
points: Create session in the first cell
Use session across many cells
Stop session when exploration is done
Session stays alive for interactive use
Used for development and exploration

blocks.title: Module 2 complete — the foundation is operational
blocks.content: You now know how to create, configure, diagnose, and manage a SparkSession. Every concept from Module 1 — the driver, executors, partitions, the cluster manager — connects to the SparkSession you just learned to build. The driver is created when your session starts. Executors are allocated based on your configuration. The Spark UI becomes available at localhost:4040. Configuration precedence determines which settings are active. And spark.stop() releases everything when you are done. In Module 3, you will use this session to create and work with DataFrames — the data structure that every PySpark pipeline revolves around.

chapters.title: DataFrames — The Core Abstraction
chapters.description: Understand DataFrames as Spark’s primary distributed data structure, master creation and schema patterns, learn the immutability and lazy evaluation models that make distributed processing safe and fast.

blocks.content: In Module 2 you created a SparkSession — the entry point to everything Spark can do. Now it is time to meet the data structure that every Spark operation revolves around: the DataFrame. A DataFrame is a distributed collection of data organized into named columns, similar to a table in a relational database or a spreadsheet with column headers. It is the central abstraction of PySpark. Every time you read data from a file, every time you filter rows, add columns, join tables, or write results, you are working with DataFrames. Understanding what a DataFrame is, how it differs from a Python list or a Pandas DataFrame, and what properties make it suitable for distributed processing is the foundation for everything that follows in this course.
A Table Distributed Across a Cluster

The simplest way to think about a DataFrame is as a table with rows and named columns. NordGrid's meter readings table has columns called meter_id, daily_kw, date, and region. Each row represents one meter's reading for one day. If you picture a spreadsheet with 500,000 rows and four columns, that is what the DataFrame looks like logically. But physically, the DataFrame is not stored on one machine. As you learned in Module 1, Spark splits data into partitions and distributes them across executors. A DataFrame with 500,000 rows might be split into 8 partitions of roughly 62,500 rows each, with each partition stored on a different executor. When you apply a transformation like filtering to positive daily_kw values, Spark applies that filter to all 8 partitions simultaneously, across all executors.

blocks.title: A DataFrame: logical view vs physical view
blocks.caption: You see one table. Spark sees partitions distributed across executors. The logical view and the physical view are different, but your code works with the logical view and Spark handles the physical distribution.

blocks.content: Three Defining Properties

A PySpark DataFrame has three properties that distinguish it from a simple Python list or dictionary. First, it is distributed — the data is split across partitions on multiple executors, enabling parallel processing. Second, it is typed — every column has a defined data type (string, integer, double, date, etc.), and Spark enforces those types. Third, it is immutable — once a DataFrame is created, its contents cannot be changed. Every transformation creates a new DataFrame rather than modifying the existing one. These three properties — distributed, typed, immutable — are not accidental. Each one solves a specific problem in distributed computing that you will understand fully by the end of this module.

rows: Data is split into partitions across executors
Enables parallel processing — multiple machines work on different pieces simultaneously
Every column has a defined data type enforced by Spark
Prevents type errors at scale — a column declared as DoubleType will never silently contain a string
Transformations create new DataFrames, never modify existing ones
Makes distributed processing safe — Spark can retry failed tasks without worrying about corrupted state

blocks.content: DataFrames in Code

In PySpark code, a DataFrame is an object of type pyspark.sql.dataframe.DataFrame. You get one by reading a file, by creating one from Python data, or by transforming an existing DataFrame. The variable holding a DataFrame is just a reference to a plan — the actual data lives on the executors, not in your Python script's memory. When you assign a DataFrame to a variable like df, you are not copying data into the driver. You are holding a reference to a distributed dataset that Spark manages across the cluster.

from pyspark.sql import SparkSession

spark = SparkSession.builder.appName('nordgrid').master('local[*]').getOrCreate()

# Three ways to get a DataFrame
# 1. Read from a file
df_from_file = spark.read.parquet('bronze/meters/')

# 2. Create from Python data
df_from_list = spark.createDataFrame(
    [('M-001', 1250.5, 'NORTH'), ('M-002', 870.2, 'SOUTH')],
    schema=['meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'region']
)

# 3. Transform an existing DataFrame
df_filtered = df_from_list.filter(df_from_list.daily_kw > 1000)

print(type(df_from_file))    # <class 'pyspark.sql.dataframe.DataFrame'>
print(type(df_from_list))    # <class 'pyspark.sql.dataframe.DataFrame'>
print(type(df_filtered))     # <class 'pyspark.sql.dataframe.DataFrame'>
# All three are the same type — the DataFrame.

<class 'pyspark.sql.dataframe.DataFrame'>
<class 'pyspark.sql.dataframe.DataFrame'>
<class 'pyspark.sql.dataframe.DataFrame'>

Notice that all three operations — reading a file, creating from Python data, and filtering — produce the same type of object. This uniformity is by design. Once you have a DataFrame, every operation you can perform on it is the same regardless of how the DataFrame was created. The filter, select, groupBy, join, and write methods work identically whether the data came from a Parquet file, a CSV file, or a Python list. This consistency makes the DataFrame API learnable: master the operations once, and they apply everywhere.

blocks.title: The DataFrame is the center of everything
blocks.content: Every module from this point forward teaches operations on DataFrames. Module 4 teaches selecting and filtering. Module 5 teaches adding and transforming columns. Module 6 teaches aggregations. Module 7 teaches joins. Module 8 teaches reading and writing. Modules 9 and 10 teach functions and SQL. All of them operate on DataFrames. The DataFrame is not just a data structure — it is the abstraction that ties the entire PySpark API together.

The most common way to create a DataFrame in production is to read it from a file — you will learn that in the next lesson. But during development, testing, and debugging, you frequently need to create a small DataFrame from Python data to test a transformation, verify a schema, or build a lookup table. PySpark's createDataFrame() method takes Python lists and dictionaries and converts them into a distributed DataFrame. This lesson covers the syntax, the schema options, and the NordGrid patterns for creating DataFrames from Python collections.
Creating from a List of Tuples

The simplest way to create a DataFrame is to pass a list of tuples and a list of column names to spark.createDataFrame(). Each tuple becomes one row, and the column names become the header. PySpark infers the data types from the Python values: strings become StringType, integers become LongType, floats become DoubleType. This approach is quick and convenient for small test DataFrames, but the inferred types are not always what you want — a column you intended as IntegerType may be inferred as LongType.

from pyspark.sql import SparkSession

spark = SparkSession.builder.appName('nordgrid').master('local[*]').getOrCreate()

# Create a DataFrame from a list of tuples
data = [
    ('M-001', 1250.5, '2026-03-01', 'NORTH'),
    ('M-002',  870.2, '2026-03-01', 'SOUTH'),
    ('M-003', 2100.0, '2026-03-01', 'EAST'),
    ('M-004',  440.7, '2026-03-01', 'WEST'),
    ('M-005',   -5.0, '2026-03-01', 'NORTH'),  # negative reading (sensor error)
]

columns = ['meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'date', 'region']

df = spark.createDataFrame(data, schema=columns)
df.show()
Explicit Schema with StructType

When you need precise control over data types — which you always do in production — you define the schema explicitly using StructType and StructField. A StructType is a list of StructField objects, where each StructField defines a column's name, data type, and whether it allows null values. Explicit schemas are verbose but safe: the DataFrame has exactly the types you specified, regardless of what the Python values look like. NordGrid's Silver-layer pipelines always use explicit schemas because type consistency across pipeline runs is a requirement.

from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StructField, StringType, DoubleType, DateType
from datetime import date

# Define the schema explicitly
schema = StructType([
    StructField('meter_id',  StringType(), nullable=False),
    StructField('daily_kw',  DoubleType(), nullable=True),
    StructField('date',      DateType(),   nullable=False),
    StructField('region',    StringType(), nullable=False),
])

# Data must use Python date objects for DateType columns
data = [
    ('M-001', 1250.5, date(2026, 3, 1), 'NORTH'),
    ('M-002',  870.2, date(2026, 3, 1), 'SOUTH'),
    ('M-003', 2100.0, date(2026, 3, 1), 'EAST'),
    ('M-004',  None,  date(2026, 3, 1), 'WEST'),   # null reading
]

df = spark.createDataFrame(data, schema=schema)
df.printSchema()
df.show()
Common Spark Data Types

Spark supports a rich set of data types, but at the junior level you will encounter a core set that covers the vast majority of NordGrid's data. String columns hold text like meter IDs and region codes. Numeric columns hold measurements like daily_kw. Date and timestamp columns hold temporal values. Boolean columns hold true/false flags. Each type has a corresponding class in pyspark.sql.types that you import and use in StructField definitions.

blocks.title: Common mistake
blocks.content: Passing string dates like '2026-03-01' to a DateType column in createDataFrame(). Spark expects Python date objects for DateType and Python datetime objects for TimestampType. If you pass strings, Spark raises a TypeError. Either use date(2026, 3, 1) in your data or define the column as StringType and cast it to DateType afterward.

In production, DataFrames almost always come from files: CSV, JSON, or Parquet stored in cloud storage or a distributed file system. PySpark provides a unified reader interface called the DataFrameReader, accessible through spark.read, that knows how to load data from all common file formats. The reader handles splitting files into partitions, parsing the format, and applying schema inference or your explicit schema. This lesson introduces the three formats NordGrid uses and shows how to read each one. Module 8 covers reading and writing in comprehensive detail — this lesson gives you enough to start working with real data.
The spark.read Interface

Every file read starts with spark.read, which returns a DataFrameReader object. From there, you can call format-specific methods — csv(), json(), or parquet() — each of which takes a file path and returns a DataFrame. The reader also supports a generic pattern using format() and load() that works with any file format. NordGrid's Bronze layer uses csv() for meter exports and json() for weather API data. The Silver and Gold layers use parquet() exclusively because Parquet is the most efficient format for analytical workloads.

# Three ways to read files — all produce DataFrames

# 1. CSV: NordGrid's raw meter exports arrive as CSV
df_csv = spark.read.csv('bronze/meters/readings.csv', header=True, inferSchema=True)

# 2. JSON: NordGrid's weather data arrives as JSON from an external API
df_json = spark.read.json('bronze/weather/forecast.json')

# 3. Parquet: NordGrid's Silver and Gold layers use Parquet
df_parquet = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# Generic pattern using format() and load()
df_generic = spark.read.format('parquet').load('silver/meters/')
Reading CSV Files

CSV is the most common raw data format and the one NordGrid's meter readings arrive in. The csv() reader has several important options. The header option tells Spark whether the first row contains column names. The inferSchema option tells Spark to scan the data and guess the data types rather than treating everything as strings. The sep option specifies the delimiter if it is not a comma. For NordGrid's daily exports, the files use commas, have a header row, and contain columns that should be typed — so all three options are typically set.

# Reading a NordGrid meter CSV with common options
df = spark.read.csv(
    'bronze/meters/readings_2026_03_01.csv',
    header=True,           # first row is column names
    inferSchema=True,      # guess types from data (convenient but slow on large files)
    sep=',',               # comma-separated (default)
    nullValue='NA',        # treat 'NA' strings as null
)

df.printSchema()
df.show(3)

root
 |-- meter_id: string (nullable = true)
 |-- daily_kw: double (nullable = true)
 |-- date: string (nullable = true)
 |-- region: string (nullable = true)

+--------+--------+----------+------+
|meter_id|daily_kw|      date|region|
+--------+--------+----------+------+
|   M-001|  1250.5|2026-03-01| NORTH|
|   M-002|   870.2|2026-03-01| SOUTH|
|   M-003|  2100.0|2026-03-01|  EAST|
+--------+--------+----------+------+
only showing top 3 rows
Reading Parquet Files

Parquet is a columnar storage format that is the standard for analytical data in data engineering. Unlike CSV, Parquet files carry their schema inside them — column names, data types, and nullability are stored as metadata alongside the data. This means you never need inferSchema or header options when reading Parquet. Spark reads the schema from the file metadata and applies it automatically. Parquet is also compressed and organized by column, which means Spark can read only the columns your query needs and skip the rest. NordGrid's Silver and Gold layers are stored exclusively in Parquet for these performance advantages.

# Reading Parquet is simple — no options needed because the schema is embedded
df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

df.printSchema()  # schema comes from the Parquet metadata, not from inference
df.show(3)

root
 |-- meter_id: string (nullable = false)
 |-- daily_kw: double (nullable = true)
 |-- date: date (nullable = false)
 |-- region: string (nullable = false)

+--------+--------+----------+------+
|meter_id|daily_kw|      date|region|
+--------+--------+----------+------+
|   M-001|  1250.5|2026-03-01| NORTH|
|   M-002|   870.2|2026-03-01| SOUTH|
|   M-003|  2100.0|2026-03-01|  EAST|
+--------+--------+----------+------+
only showing top 3 rows

Notice the difference in the schema. The CSV reader inferred date as a string because CSV has no type metadata. The Parquet reader knows date is a DateType because the Parquet file stored that information when it was written. This is one of the core reasons data engineering pipelines convert from CSV (Bronze) to Parquet (Silver): the schema becomes self-documenting and type-safe, eliminating an entire category of bugs.

blocks.title: CSV for ingestion, Parquet for everything else
blocks.content: NordGrid's pattern is representative of the industry: raw data arrives in CSV or JSON because that is what source systems produce. The first pipeline (Bronze to Silver) reads the raw format and writes Parquet. From that point on, every pipeline reads and writes Parquet. You will learn this complete pattern in Module 8.

Every DataFrame has a schema — a definition of its columns, their data types, and whether they accept null values. The schema is like a contract between the code that produces data and the code that consumes it. When NordGrid's Bronze pipeline writes meter readings to Parquet, the schema says: meter_id is a non-nullable string, daily_kw is a nullable double, date is a non-nullable date, and region is a non-nullable string. When the Silver pipeline reads that Parquet file, it trusts this contract. If the schema changes unexpectedly — a column is missing, a type changes, a non-nullable column contains nulls — the downstream pipeline breaks. Understanding schemas is essential because schema mismatches are one of the most common sources of production failures in data engineering.
Viewing the Schema

The printSchema() method displays a DataFrame's schema as an indented tree. Each line shows the column name, data type, and nullability. This is the first thing NordGrid engineers check when they read a new dataset or debug a pipeline failure. If the schema does not match expectations, the pipeline will produce wrong results or crash — checking early saves hours of debugging later.

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')
df.printSchema()

root
 |-- meter_id: string (nullable = false)
 |-- daily_kw: double (nullable = true)
 |-- date: date (nullable = false)
 |-- region: string (nullable = false)

The output tells you everything about the DataFrame's structure. meter_id is a string that can never be null — every meter reading must have an ID. daily_kw is a double that can be null — a sensor malfunction might produce a missing reading. date is a non-nullable date — every reading is tied to a specific day. region is a non-nullable string — every meter belongs to one of the four regions. This nullability information is part of the schema and affects how your code handles the data, particularly during filters and aggregations.
Defining a Schema with StructType

You saw StructType briefly in lesson 2 when creating DataFrames from Python collections. In production, StructType becomes a critical tool. Instead of letting Spark infer the schema from the data — which is slow for large files and can produce wrong types — you define the schema yourself and pass it to the reader. The reader then applies your schema without scanning the data for type inference. This is faster, safer, and ensures that the pipeline behaves identically every time, regardless of whether the data has changed.

from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StructField, StringType, DoubleType, DateType

# Define the schema once, use it every time you read this data
meter_schema = StructType([
    StructField('meter_id',  StringType(), nullable=False),
    StructField('daily_kw',  DoubleType(), nullable=True),
    StructField('date',      DateType(),   nullable=False),
    StructField('region',    StringType(), nullable=False),
])

# Apply the schema to a CSV read — no inferSchema needed
df = spark.read.csv(
    'bronze/meters/readings_2026_03_01.csv',
    header=True,
    schema=meter_schema,   # explicit schema overrides inference
)

df.printSchema()  # matches exactly what we defined

root
 |-- meter_id: string (nullable = false)
 |-- daily_kw: double (nullable = true)
 |-- date: date (nullable = false)
 |-- region: string (nullable = false)
Schema Inference vs Explicit Schema

left.label: Schema Inference (inferSchema=True)
points: Spark scans the data to guess types
Slow on large files (must read data twice)
Types may change if data changes
Good for quick exploration
Dates often inferred as strings

right.label: Explicit Schema (schema=StructType)
points: Types are defined in code — no scanning
Fast — schema is applied immediately
Types are locked — pipeline is deterministic
Required for production pipelines
Dates are DateType if you define them that way

blocks.title: Production rule
blocks.content: Never use inferSchema=True in production pipelines. It makes the pipeline non-deterministic: if the source data changes format (a new column appears, a column's values change from integers to decimals), the inferred schema changes, and the downstream pipeline silently produces wrong results or crashes. Always define an explicit schema for production reads. NordGrid's code review checklist flags inferSchema=True as a required fix.

Spark does not execute your transformations when you write them. It records them into a plan — a sequence of steps that describe what you want but do not actually process any data. Only when you call an action — show(), count(), write(), collect() — does Spark look at the entire plan, optimize it, and execute it. This behavior is called lazy evaluation, and it is the mechanism that makes Spark fast. Without laziness, Spark would execute each transformation immediately, with no opportunity to rearrange, combine, or skip unnecessary work. With laziness, the Catalyst optimizer sees the complete picture before any data moves.

To understand why this matters, consider an analogy from the NordGrid control room. A shift supervisor does not send a crew to fix turbine A, wait for them to finish, then send them to inspect turbine B, then wait again, then send them to collect readings from turbine C. The supervisor collects all three work orders, looks at them together, notices that A and B are in the same wind farm, and sends the crew on one trip that handles both before driving to C. The supervisor's laziness — waiting to see all the work before dispatching — results in a faster plan. Spark's Catalyst optimizer does the same thing with your transformations.
Building the Plan

Consider a pipeline that reads NordGrid meter readings from a Parquet file, filters to positive values in the NORTH region, adds a year column extracted from the date, and selects three output columns. Each of these operations is a transformation. When you write them in your code, Spark does not read any files, does not filter any rows, does not compute any columns. It simply records each step as a node in an internal plan graph, waiting for an action to trigger execution.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, year

# Step 1: Define the source — no data is read yet
df = spark.read.parquet('bronze/meters/')

# Step 2: Filter to positive readings — no rows are scanned yet
df = df.filter(col('daily_kw') > 0)

# Step 3: Filter to NORTH region — still no scanning
df = df.filter(col('region') == 'NORTH')

# Step 4: Extract the year from the date column — still no computation
df = df.withColumn('year', year(col('date')))

# Step 5: Select only the columns we need — still nothing executed
df = df.select('meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'year')

# At this point, ZERO data has been read from disk.
# Spark has recorded 5 transformations in a plan.
# The plan exists as a lightweight data structure in the driver's memory.

print("Plan built. No data processed yet.")
print(f"Type of df: {type(df)}")  # pyspark.sql.dataframe.DataFrame — a plan, not results

# THIS triggers execution — Spark optimizes the entire plan and runs it
df.show(5)

Five transformations were recorded, but zero data was processed — until show(5) triggered the entire chain. This is the core of lazy evaluation: writing transformations builds a plan; calling an action executes the plan. Between building and executing, the Catalyst optimizer has a window of opportunity to improve the plan. And it takes that opportunity aggressively.
What Catalyst Optimizes

The Catalyst optimizer does not simply run your five steps in order. It analyzes the entire plan and applies optimizations that a human might not think of — or might think of but find tedious to implement manually. For the pipeline above, Catalyst applies at least three major optimizations that dramatically reduce the amount of data Spark actually reads and processes.

First, it combines the two separate filter conditions (daily_kw > 0 AND region == 'NORTH') into a single compound filter. This means Spark evaluates both conditions on each row in one pass instead of scanning the data twice. Second, it pushes the region filter into the Parquet reader as a partition filter. If the Parquet files are organized with region as a partition column (a common pattern for NordGrid data), Spark skips the SOUTH, EAST, and WEST folders entirely — it never reads those bytes from disk. Third, it applies projection pruning based on the final select() — since the output only needs meter_id, daily_kw, and date (to compute year), Spark tells the Parquet reader to skip the region column data entirely. Parquet is a columnar format, meaning each column is stored in a separate section of the file, so skipping a column means those bytes are literally never read.

blocks.title: Your code vs what Catalyst actually executes
blocks.content:
Your Code (5 transformations)              Catalyst's Optimized Execution
─────────────────────────────              ─────────────────────────────────

1. read parquet (all columns,  ─┐          Read parquet:
   all partitions)              │            • Partition filter: region = NORTH
                                │              (skips 75% of data at filesystem level)
2. filter daily_kw > 0         │            • Column pruning: meter_id, daily_kw, date
                                ├─► Optimize   (skips region column bytes)
3. filter region == 'NORTH'    │            • Pushed filter: daily_kw > 0
                                │              (applied during file scan, not after)
4. withColumn year(date)       │
                                │          Compute year(date)
5. select 3 columns           ─┘          Output: meter_id, daily_kw, year

Naive execution: read 2GB (all data)    Optimized: read ~250MB (NORTH, 3 cols, filtered)
Five separate passes over the data      One pass, three optimizations fused together
blocks.caption: Lazy evaluation lets Catalyst see all five steps before executing any of them. The optimizer combines filters, pushes them into the reader, and skips unnecessary columns — reducing data read by roughly 8x.

blocks.content: The practical impact is dramatic. A naive execution of these five steps would read the entire 2GB file, then discard 75% of rows (non-NORTH regions), then discard more rows (non-positive readings), then compute year on the survivors, then drop columns. The optimized execution reads only the NORTH partition folder (~500MB), reads only three columns from that folder (~300MB effective), and filters during the read (~250MB surviving). The same result, roughly 8x less data processed. This optimization is automatic — you did not need to restructure your code. Catalyst did it because lazy evaluation gave it the opportunity to see the full plan.
Seeing the Plan with explain()

You do not have to trust that Catalyst is optimizing — you can see exactly what it plans to do by calling explain(). This method prints the execution plan without triggering execution, meaning no data is read and no computation happens. It is a diagnostic tool, not an action. The output reads bottom-to-top: the lowest line is where data enters (the file scan), and the topmost line is the final output.

# explain(True) shows all plan phases — parsed, analyzed, optimized, physical
# It does NOT execute anything — no data is read
df.explain(True)

== Parsed Logical Plan ==
Project [meter_id, daily_kw, year(date) AS year]
+- Filter ((daily_kw > 0) AND (region = NORTH))
   +- Relation [meter_id, daily_kw, date, region] parquet

== Optimized Logical Plan ==
Project [meter_id#0, daily_kw#1, year(date#2) AS year#5]
+- Filter (isnotnull(daily_kw#1) AND (daily_kw#1 > 0.0))
   +- FileScan parquet [meter_id#0, daily_kw#1, date#2]
      PartitionFilters: [region#3 = NORTH]
      PushedFilters: [IsNotNull(daily_kw), GreaterThan(daily_kw, 0.0)]
      ReadSchema: struct<meter_id:string,daily_kw:double,date:date>

The optimized plan reveals every optimization Catalyst applied. The line 'PartitionFilters: [region#3 = NORTH]' confirms that Spark will only read the NORTH partition folder, skipping 75% of the data at the file system level before any processing begins. 'PushedFilters: [IsNotNull(daily_kw), GreaterThan(daily_kw, 0.0)]' shows that the kw filter is applied during the file scan itself — rows that do not match are discarded as they are read, never entering the DataFrame. 'ReadSchema: struct<meter_id:string,daily_kw:double,date:date>' shows that only three columns are read from the Parquet files — region is absent from the read schema because its value is encoded in the partition folder name rather than stored in the file data. Notice that Catalyst also added an IsNotNull(daily_kw) filter that you did not write. This is another optimization: since daily_kw > 0.0 cannot be true for null values (any comparison with null returns null, not true or false), Catalyst adds the null check to eliminate null rows early, before they reach the greater-than comparison. This is the kind of detail that the optimizer handles automatically — a small optimization that adds up when processing millions of rows.
Transformations vs Actions

The distinction between transformations and actions is the most important operational concept in PySpark. Transformations are lazy — they build the plan. Actions are eager — they execute the plan. Every PySpark method falls into exactly one of these categories, and knowing which is which tells you when data will actually move across the cluster. If you call a transformation, nothing happens to your data. If you call an action, the entire plan executes from source to result.

rows: filter(), where() — choose which rows to keep
show(n) — display n rows in the console
select(), drop() — choose which columns to keep
count() — count all rows across all partitions
withColumn() — add or replace a column
collect() — return ALL rows to the driver as a Python list
first(), head() — return one row to the driver
write.parquet(), write.csv() — write results to disk
toPandas() — convert entire DataFrame to Pandas on the driver
describe() — compute column statistics (count, mean, stddev, min, max)
foreach() — apply a function to each row

blocks.content: A common source of confusion is that some operations look like they should produce results but are actually transformations. For example, groupBy('region').agg(sum('daily_kw')) looks like it computes a sum — but it is a transformation. It defines the aggregation plan. The plan is not executed until you call an action on the result, like .show() or .write.parquet(). Similarly, orderBy('daily_kw') does not sort anything — it records that a sort should happen when an action triggers execution.
The Cost of Unnecessary Actions

Every action triggers a full execution of the plan from source to result. If you call count() after step 3 to check how many rows survived the filter, then continue building the plan and call show() at the end, Spark executes the plan twice — once for the count and once for the show. The intermediate count triggers a full read, filter, and aggregation. The final show triggers another full read, filter, enrichment, and display. In a development notebook, this is fine — the extra execution costs seconds and helps you debug. In a production pipeline, unnecessary intermediate actions waste compute time and cluster resources.

# DEVELOPMENT PATTERN — use intermediate actions freely for debugging
df = spark.read.parquet('bronze/meters/')
df = df.filter(col('daily_kw') > 0)
print(f"After positive filter: {df.count()} rows")  # action — executes plan from scratch

df = df.filter(col('region') == 'NORTH')
print(f"After NORTH filter: {df.count()} rows")  # action — executes plan from scratch again

df = df.withColumn('year', year(col('date')))
df.show(5)  # action — executes plan from scratch a third time

# Total: the source file is read THREE times. Fine for debugging.

# PRODUCTION PATTERN — one action at the end
df = (spark.read.parquet('bronze/meters/')
    .filter(col('daily_kw') > 0)
    .filter(col('region') == 'NORTH')
    .withColumn('year', year(col('date')))
    .select('meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'year')
)
df.write.parquet('silver/meters_north/')  # ONE action — file read once, plan optimized once

# Total: the source file is read ONCE. Correct for production.

blocks.title: Common mistake
blocks.content: Calling count() inside a loop or after every transformation in a production pipeline. Each count() triggers a full execution of the entire plan, reading and processing the source data from scratch. A pipeline with five count() calls between transformations reads the source file five times instead of once. During development, use show(5) liberally to inspect results — it is cheap because it stops after 5 rows. In production, chain all transformations and trigger one action (write) at the end. If you need to validate row counts in production, do it once after the final write by reading the output back.

There is one more important distinction to understand. Unlike show() and count(), explain() is not an action — it does not trigger execution. It reads the metadata graph that Spark built from your transformations and displays it as text. No data is read from disk, no filters are applied, no computations happen. This makes explain() the ideal debugging tool for production code: you can verify what Spark will do without paying the cost of actually doing it. Get in the habit of calling explain() before triggering expensive actions, especially for joins and aggregations that involve shuffle. If the plan looks wrong — for example, if you expected a broadcast join but see a SortMergeJoin — you can fix the transformations before wasting minutes on an inefficient execution.

In the previous lesson you learned that Spark is lazy — it builds a plan before executing anything. This lesson introduces the second fundamental property of DataFrames: immutability. An immutable object is one that cannot be changed after it is created. When you call df.filter(col('daily_kw') > 0), Spark does not modify df. It creates a brand-new DataFrame that represents the filtered result and leaves the original df completely untouched. Every transformation in PySpark works this way: the input DataFrame is never altered, and the output is always a new DataFrame. This might seem wasteful at first — why create a new object instead of modifying the existing one? The answer is that immutability solves critical problems in distributed computing.
Transformations Create New DataFrames

from pyspark.sql.functions import col

# Start with the full meter readings DataFrame
df_all = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# filter() creates a NEW DataFrame — df_all is unchanged
df_positive = df_all.filter(col('daily_kw') > 0)

# Both DataFrames exist independently
print(f"df_all columns:      {df_all.columns}")
print(f"df_positive columns: {df_positive.columns}")

# df_all still has all rows (including negatives and nulls)
# df_positive has only rows where daily_kw > 0
# They are separate objects referencing separate plans

df_all columns:      ['meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'date', 'region']
df_positive columns: ['meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'date', 'region']

The key insight is that df_all and df_positive are two separate DataFrame objects. df_all still represents the entire dataset. df_positive represents the filtered version. Neither one has been executed yet (because of lazy evaluation) — they are both plans. If you call df_all.count(), you get the total row count. If you call df_positive.count(), you get only the positive rows. The original data is never modified.
The Variable Reassignment Pattern

In practice, PySpark engineers frequently reuse the same variable name for successive transformations. This looks like the DataFrame is being modified in place, but it is not. Python is reassigning the variable to point at a new DataFrame object. The old DataFrame still exists in memory as part of the plan graph until Spark's garbage collector removes it. This pattern is idiomatic PySpark and is the most common style you will see in NordGrid's codebase.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, year

# This LOOKS like mutation but is NOT — it is variable reassignment
df = spark.read.parquet('bronze/meters/')
df = df.filter(col('daily_kw') > 0)           # df now points to a new filtered DataFrame
df = df.filter(col('region') == 'NORTH')       # df now points to another new DataFrame
df = df.withColumn('year', year(col('date')))  # df now points to yet another new DataFrame
df = df.select('meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'year') # df now points to the final new DataFrame

# Each line created a new DataFrame.
# The variable 'df' was reassigned 4 times.
# The original data was never modified.
Why Immutability Matters for Distributed Computing

Immutability solves two critical problems in distributed systems. First, it enables safe retries. When an executor crashes or a task fails, Spark needs to rerun that task on a different executor. If the data were mutable, the failed task might have partially modified it, leaving corrupted state. Because DataFrames are immutable, the original data is untouched — Spark can simply rerun the task from the last known good state. Second, it eliminates coordination between executors. If multiple executors could modify the same DataFrame simultaneously, they would need locks and synchronization to prevent conflicts — exactly the kind of coordination overhead that makes distributed programming difficult. Immutability means each executor works with its own copy of the plan, and no executor can interfere with another.

left.label: Mutable Data (dangerous in distributed systems)
points: Transformations modify the original data
A failed task leaves corrupted state
Executors must coordinate to avoid conflicts
Debugging is hard — state changes over time

right.label: Immutable DataFrames (safe in distributed systems)
points: Transformations create new DataFrames
A failed task can be safely retried
Executors work independently
Debugging is easier — each step is a clean snapshot

blocks.title: Immutability and laziness work together
blocks.content: Laziness means Spark builds a plan without executing it. Immutability means each step in the plan produces a new DataFrame without modifying the previous one. Together, they create a clean, replayable chain of transformations that Spark can optimize, execute in parallel, and retry on failure. These two properties are the reason PySpark code can scale from a laptop to a 100-node cluster without changes.

Before transforming data, you need to understand what you have: what columns exist, what types they are, what the values look like, and whether anything looks unexpected. PySpark provides three essential methods for exploring a DataFrame: show() displays sample rows, printSchema() displays the column structure, and describe() computes summary statistics. These three methods form the exploration workflow that NordGrid engineers use every time they open a new dataset or debug a pipeline. This lesson covers each method in detail and shows you the patterns for using them effectively.
show() — See the Data

The show() method displays rows from the DataFrame in a formatted table. By default, it shows 20 rows with columns truncated to 20 characters. You can control the number of rows by passing an integer argument, and you can disable truncation by passing truncate=False. Remember from lesson 5 that show() is an action — it triggers execution of the entire plan. When you call df.show(5), Spark executes all pending transformations and returns the first 5 rows to the driver for display.

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# Default: 20 rows, truncated columns
df.show()

# Show only 5 rows
df.show(5)

# Show 5 rows without truncating long values
df.show(5, truncate=False)

# Show 5 rows with vertical layout (useful for wide DataFrames)
df.show(5, vertical=True)
printSchema() — See the Structure

The printSchema() method displays the DataFrame's schema as an indented tree showing column names, data types, and nullability. Unlike show(), printSchema() is not an action — it reads the schema metadata that Spark already has in memory and does not trigger any data processing. This makes it free to call as often as you want, even on very large DataFrames. NordGrid's pipeline debugging workflow always starts with printSchema() to verify that the data structure matches expectations before looking at actual values.

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')
df.printSchema()

# You can also access the schema as a Python object
print(df.schema)           # StructType object
print(df.columns)          # list of column names
print(df.dtypes)           # list of (name, type) tuples
print(len(df.columns))     # number of columns

root
 |-- meter_id: string (nullable = false)
 |-- daily_kw: double (nullable = true)
 |-- date: date (nullable = false)
 |-- region: string (nullable = false)

StructType([StructField('meter_id', StringType(), False), ...])
['meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'date', 'region']
[('meter_id', 'string'), ('daily_kw', 'double'), ('date', 'date'), ('region', 'string')]
4
describe() — See the Statistics

The describe() method computes summary statistics for numeric and string columns: count, mean, standard deviation, minimum, and maximum. Like show(), describe() is an action — it triggers a full pass over the data to compute the statistics. For NordGrid's meter readings, describe() immediately reveals whether there are null values (count is lower than expected), whether any readings are negative (min is below zero), and what the typical range looks like (mean and stddev). This is the fastest way to spot data quality issues in a new dataset.

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')
df.describe().show()

The describe output reveals several things about NordGrid's data. The daily_kw count (499,850) is lower than the meter_id count (500,000), which means 150 rows have null values in daily_kw — sensor malfunctions that produced no reading. The minimum daily_kw is -5.0, confirming that there are negative readings that need to be filtered in the Silver pipeline. The mean of 1,247 kW and standard deviation of 623 kW give a sense of the typical consumption range. All of this information is available from a single method call.

blocks.title: The three-method exploration workflow
blocks.content: When you encounter a new DataFrame, use this sequence: printSchema() first to understand the structure (free — no data processing). show(5) second to see sample values (cheap — only 5 rows). describe() third to see summary statistics (expensive — full data scan). This sequence goes from cheapest to most expensive, giving you the most information for the least computation. NordGrid's team calls this the 'schema, sample, stats' workflow.

sections.title: Columns and Rows — The Building Blocks

blocks.content: A DataFrame is made of columns and rows, but PySpark treats them very differently. Columns are the primary unit of work — you reference them by name, apply transformations to them, and use them in expressions. Rows are the data that flows through the pipeline, but you rarely interact with individual rows directly. This column-centric design is fundamental to PySpark's API. If you come from a Python background where you are used to iterating over rows in a list or a Pandas DataFrame, PySpark requires a mental shift: instead of thinking 'for each row, do something,' you think 'for this column, apply this transformation.' This lesson introduces the Column and Row objects and the three ways to reference columns in PySpark.
The Column Object

In PySpark, a column is not a list of values — it is an expression that describes how to compute a value from each row. When you write col('daily_kw') > 0, you are not comparing a number to zero. You are creating a Column expression that Spark will evaluate against every row when an action triggers execution. The col() function from pyspark.sql.functions is the primary way to create a Column reference. Column objects can be combined with arithmetic, comparison, and logical operators to build complex expressions.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col

# col() creates a Column reference — it does NOT access data
kw_column = col('daily_kw')
print(type(kw_column))  # <class 'pyspark.sql.column.Column'>

# Column expressions — building blocks for transformations
positive_filter = col('daily_kw') > 0           # comparison expression
north_filter    = col('region') == 'NORTH'       # equality expression
combined_filter = (col('daily_kw') > 0) & (col('region') == 'NORTH')  # AND

# Arithmetic expressions
kw_to_mw = col('daily_kw') / 1000               # divide every value by 1000
double_kw = col('daily_kw') * 2                  # multiply every value by 2

print(type(positive_filter))  # <class 'pyspark.sql.column.Column'>
print(type(kw_to_mw))         # <class 'pyspark.sql.column.Column'>
# All expressions are Column objects — they describe computation, not results

<class 'pyspark.sql.column.Column'>
<class 'pyspark.sql.column.Column'>
<class 'pyspark.sql.column.Column'>
Three Ways to Reference a Column

PySpark offers three syntaxes for referencing a column. All three create the same Column object, and all three work in the same places. The choice between them is a matter of readability and context. NordGrid's codebase uses col() as the default because it is explicit, consistent, and works outside of any specific DataFrame context — making it safe to use in utility functions that accept any DataFrame.

rows: Default choice. Explicit, works everywhere, imported from pyspark.sql.functions.
Bracket notation. Useful when you need to specify which DataFrame a column comes from (e.g., in joins).
Dot notation. Short and readable, but fails for column names with spaces or special characters.

blocks.content:
from pyspark.sql.functions import col

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# All three produce the same result
df.filter(col('daily_kw') > 0)      # col() — most common
df.filter(df['daily_kw'] > 0)       # bracket notation
df.filter(df.daily_kw > 0)          # dot notation

# col() works without a DataFrame reference — useful in functions
def filter_positive(dataframe, column_name):
    return dataframe.filter(col(column_name) > 0)

result = filter_positive(df, 'daily_kw')
The Row Object

While columns are the primary unit of PySpark's API, rows do exist as objects. When you call collect(), first(), or head(), Spark returns Row objects to the driver. A Row is essentially a named tuple — you can access its fields by name or by position. However, working with individual Row objects is uncommon in PySpark pipelines because it defeats the purpose of distributed processing. If you collect all rows to the driver, you lose parallelism and risk running out of driver memory. Rows are used for inspecting individual records during debugging, not for transforming data at scale.

# first() returns a single Row object
row = df.first()
print(type(row))           # <class 'pyspark.sql.types.Row'>

# Access fields by name
print(row['meter_id'])     # M-001
print(row['daily_kw'])     # 1250.5

# Access fields by position
print(row[0])              # M-001
print(row[1])              # 1250.5

# Convert to a Python dictionary
print(row.asDict())        # {'meter_id': 'M-001', 'daily_kw': 1250.5, ...}

<class 'pyspark.sql.types.Row'>
M-001
1250.5
M-001
1250.5
{'meter_id': 'M-001', 'daily_kw': 1250.5, 'date': datetime.date(2026, 3, 1), 'region': 'NORTH'}

blocks.title: Think in columns, not rows
blocks.content: The most common mistake for engineers coming from Python or Pandas is trying to iterate over rows with a for loop. PySpark is not designed for row-by-row processing — it is designed for column-level transformations that Spark can parallelize across the cluster. Instead of 'for each row, if daily_kw > 0, keep it,' think 'filter the daily_kw column to values greater than zero.' Column-centric thinking is the key to writing efficient PySpark code.

If you have used Python for data analysis, you almost certainly know Pandas DataFrames. PySpark DataFrames share the name and some concepts, but they are fundamentally different in how they work. Understanding these differences prevents you from applying Pandas patterns that do not translate to PySpark, and helps you appreciate why PySpark's API is designed the way it is. This lesson compares the two along five dimensions that matter most to a data engineer transitioning from single-machine Python to distributed PySpark.
The Five Key Differences

left.label: Pandas DataFrame
points: Runs on one machine
Eager: operations execute immediately
Mutable: you can modify in place
Data lives in the Python process's memory
Row-by-row iteration is common and fast

right.label: PySpark DataFrame
points: Distributed across a cluster
Lazy: operations build a plan, actions execute it
Immutable: every transformation creates a new DataFrame
Data lives on executors, not in your Python process
Column-level expressions are the primary pattern

blocks.content: Distribution — One Machine vs Many

A Pandas DataFrame is a single object in a single Python process's memory. It lives entirely on one machine. If the data exceeds that machine's RAM, Pandas crashes with a MemoryError. A PySpark DataFrame is a distributed plan — the actual data is split into partitions across multiple executors on multiple machines. This means PySpark can process datasets that are 10x, 100x, or 1,000x larger than what fits in memory on any single machine. NordGrid's three-year meter archive (500GB) cannot fit in a Pandas DataFrame on any reasonable machine, but it processes comfortably in PySpark across 8 executors.
Eager vs Lazy Execution

When you write df[df['daily_kw'] > 0] in Pandas, the filter executes immediately and produces the result. When you write df.filter(col('daily_kw') > 0) in PySpark, nothing happens — the filter is recorded as part of a plan. This laziness lets Spark's Catalyst optimizer analyze your entire pipeline before executing anything, producing a plan that is often significantly faster than executing each step immediately. The downside is that you cannot inspect intermediate results without triggering an action. This is why PySpark development often uses show(5) liberally during debugging — each show() forces Spark to execute the plan up to that point so you can see what the data looks like.
Bridging with toPandas()

PySpark provides toPandas() to convert a PySpark DataFrame into a Pandas DataFrame. This sends all the data from the executors to the driver and creates a Pandas object in the driver's memory. It is useful when you have reduced a large distributed dataset to a small result that you want to plot, inspect interactively, or pass to a library that expects Pandas. NordGrid's engineers use toPandas() after aggregations — the aggregated Gold table might have only a few thousand rows, small enough for Pandas and for visualization with Matplotlib.

# After aggregation, the result is small — safe to convert to Pandas
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, sum as spark_sum, count

gold_df = (spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')
    .filter(col('daily_kw') > 0)
    .groupBy('region')
    .agg(
        spark_sum('daily_kw').alias('total_kw'),
        count('meter_id').alias('meter_count'),
    )
)

# Convert to Pandas for plotting or inspection
pdf = gold_df.toPandas()
print(type(pdf))  # <class 'pandas.core.frame.DataFrame'>
print(pdf)

<class 'pandas.core.frame.DataFrame'>
  region     total_kw  meter_count
0  NORTH  156,250,000      125,000
1  SOUTH  155,400,000      124,800
2   EAST  157,100,000      125,100
3   WEST  154,500,000      124,900

blocks.title: Never call toPandas() on a large DataFrame
