blocks.content: toPandas() sends ALL data from the executors to the driver. If your PySpark DataFrame has 500 million rows, calling toPandas() tries to load all 500 million rows into the driver's memory — which will crash the driver with an OutOfMemoryError. Only use toPandas() after aggregations, limits, or filters that have reduced the data to a manageable size. A good rule of thumb: if the result is under 100,000 rows, toPandas() is safe.

You now understand the DataFrame's properties — distributed, typed, immutable, lazy — and the tools for creating and exploring them. This final lesson ties everything together by tracing a complete NordGrid pipeline from creation through transformations to a final action. The goal is to show how the concepts from this entire module work together in a realistic pipeline and to establish the read-transform-act pattern that every PySpark pipeline follows. By the end of this lesson, you will have a complete mental model of how DataFrames behave from start to finish.
The Read-Transform-Act Pattern

Every PySpark pipeline follows the same three-phase pattern. The read phase loads data from a source into a DataFrame. The transform phase applies a chain of operations — filtering, adding columns, renaming, joining — each producing a new immutable DataFrame. The act phase triggers execution with an action like write, show, or count. Because of lazy evaluation, no data moves until the act phase. The Catalyst optimizer sees the entire chain during the act phase and optimizes it before execution begins.

blocks.title: The read-transform-act pipeline pattern
blocks.content:
READ                    TRANSFORM                       ACT
────                    ─────────                       ───

spark.read       ─►   filter()    ─►   write.parquet()
  .parquet()            withColumn()        (triggers
                        select()             execution)

Loads data             Each step creates     Catalyst optimizes
into a                 a new immutable       the entire chain,
DataFrame              DataFrame             then executes

│  No data moves  │  No data moves  │  ALL data moves  │
│  (plan only)    │  (plan only)    │  (execution)     │
blocks.caption: The three phases of every PySpark pipeline. The read and transform phases build a plan. The act phase triggers execution of the optimized plan.

blocks.content: A Complete NordGrid Pipeline

The following code shows a complete Bronze-to-Silver pipeline for NordGrid's meter readings. It reads raw CSV data from the Bronze layer, applies an explicit schema, filters out invalid readings, adds a derived year column, selects the output columns, and writes the result to Parquet in the Silver layer. Every concept from this module appears in this pipeline: DataFrame creation (lesson 1), schema enforcement (lessons 2–4), lazy evaluation (lesson 5), immutability through variable reassignment (lesson 6), exploration methods for debugging (lesson 7), and column expressions (lesson 8).

from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, year
from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StructField, StringType, DoubleType, DateType

# Session
spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .appName('nordgrid-daily-meters-bronze-to-silver') \
    .config('spark.sql.shuffle.partitions', '16') \
    .getOrCreate()

# Schema contract — explicit, not inferred
meter_schema = StructType([
    StructField('meter_id',  StringType(), nullable=False),
    StructField('daily_kw',  DoubleType(), nullable=True),
    StructField('date',      DateType(),   nullable=False),
    StructField('region',    StringType(), nullable=False),
])

try:
    # READ — load raw CSV with explicit schema
    df = spark.read.csv('bronze/meters/', header=True, schema=meter_schema)

    # EXPLORE — verify what we have (development only)
    df.printSchema()    # free: check structure
    df.show(5)          # cheap: check sample values

    # TRANSFORM — each line creates a new immutable DataFrame
    df = df.filter(col('daily_kw').isNotNull())       # remove null readings
    df = df.filter(col('daily_kw') > 0)               # remove negative readings
    df = df.withColumn('year', year(col('date')))      # add year column
    df = df.select('meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'date', 'region', 'year')

    # ACT — trigger execution and write output
    df.write.mode('overwrite').parquet('silver/meters_clean/')
    print('Pipeline complete: Silver meters written.')

finally:
    spark.stop()
Tracing the Lifecycle

When this script runs, the following sequence occurs. Lines 14–19 define the schema as a Python object in the driver's memory — no Spark activity. Line 22 creates a DataFrame by pointing Spark at the Bronze CSV files — no data is read yet. Lines 25–26 call printSchema() (reads metadata, not data) and show(5) (triggers a small execution that reads only enough rows to display 5). Lines 29–32 build the transformation plan — four new DataFrames are created, each representing one more transformation step, but no data moves. Line 35 calls write, which triggers the full execution: Catalyst optimizes the entire chain, then executors read the CSV, filter nulls, filter negatives, compute the year, select the columns, and write Parquet. The entire pipeline — from CSV to clean Parquet — executes in one optimized pass.

rows: DataFrame created — plan points to CSV files
Schema metadata displayed from the CSV header
Spark reads just enough data to return 5 rows
New DataFrame added to plan — null filter recorded
New DataFrame added to plan — positive filter recorded
New DataFrame added to plan — year computation recorded
New DataFrame added to plan — column selection recorded
Catalyst optimizes the full chain, executors process all data

blocks.title: Module 3 complete — you have the core abstraction
blocks.content: The DataFrame is the foundation of everything in PySpark. You now understand what it is (a distributed, typed, immutable collection of rows and columns), how to create it (from Python data or from files), how its schema works (the contract that defines column names, types, and nullability), how lazy evaluation defers execution (transformations build a plan, actions execute it), how immutability keeps distributed processing safe (every transformation creates a new DataFrame), and how to explore data (show, printSchema, describe). In Module 4, you will use these DataFrames for the most common pipeline operations: selecting columns and filtering rows.

chapters.title: Selecting and Filtering Data
chapters.description: Master the core data shaping operations: choosing which columns to keep, which rows to keep, and how to chain these operations into clean transformation pipelines.

blocks.content: NordGrid's Bronze meter readings table has dozens of columns: meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, ingestion_timestamp, source_file, batch_id, raw_line_number, and more. The Silver pipeline needs only four of these. The operation that keeps the columns you need and discards the rest is select(). It is the most frequently used DataFrame method in PySpark — virtually every pipeline uses it at least once. In Module 3, you learned that DataFrames are immutable: select() does not remove columns from the original DataFrame. It creates a new DataFrame containing only the columns you specified. The original remains untouched.
Basic Column Selection

The simplest form of select() takes column names as strings. You pass the names of the columns you want, and Spark returns a new DataFrame containing only those columns, in the order you listed them. This is the PySpark equivalent of SQL's SELECT clause. The column order in the output matches the order in your select() call, not the order in the source DataFrame — which gives you control over column ordering.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col

# Bronze table has many columns
df_bronze = spark.read.parquet('bronze/meters/')
print(f"Bronze columns: {df_bronze.columns}")

# select() keeps only the columns you specify
df_silver = df_bronze.select('meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'date', 'region')
print(f"Silver columns: {df_silver.columns}")

df_silver.show(3)
Selection with col() Expressions

You can also pass Column objects to select() using the col() function you learned in Module 3, lesson 8. This is more verbose for simple selection but becomes essential when you want to apply expressions — arithmetic, renaming, or type casting — inside the select call. When you pass col('daily_kw') * 1000, Spark adds a computed column to the output. This is one of select()'s most powerful features: it can both choose columns and transform them in a single operation.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col

# Mixing string names and col() expressions in the same select
df_result = df_bronze.select(
    'meter_id',                          # string: keep as-is
    col('daily_kw'),                     # col(): keep as-is (equivalent to string)
    (col('daily_kw') * 1000).alias('daily_watts'),  # col() expression: computed column
    'region',
)

df_result.show(3)
Why Column Pruning Matters

Selecting only the columns you need is not just about code clarity — it directly reduces the amount of data Spark processes. When you read from a columnar format like Parquet, Spark's Catalyst optimizer uses your select() to perform column pruning: it tells the Parquet reader to skip the columns you did not ask for. Those columns are never read from disk. For NordGrid's Bronze table with 7 columns, selecting 4 means Spark reads roughly 57% of the file data. On a 500GB archive, that saves 215GB of disk I/O. This optimization is automatic and happens because of lazy evaluation — Catalyst sees the select() before executing the read.

blocks.title: select() is both a shaping tool and a performance tool
blocks.content: Use select() to produce the exact columns your downstream pipeline needs. This serves two purposes: it makes the data contract clear (the output DataFrame has precisely the columns the next step expects), and it minimizes the data Spark reads and processes (column pruning). NordGrid's coding standard requires a select() at the end of every Bronze-to-Silver pipeline to enforce the Silver schema contract.

In Module 3, lesson 8 you met the Column object briefly — a reference to a column that describes computation rather than holding data. This lesson expands on that introduction because the Column object is the building block of everything in this module: every select expression, every filter condition, every alias, and every computed column is built from Column objects. Understanding what a Column is and how Column expressions compose is the key to writing expressive, efficient PySpark transformations.
A Column Is an Expression

When you write col('daily_kw'), you do not get a list of values. You get a Column object — a symbolic reference that says 'the column named daily_kw.' This reference is inert until Spark executes a plan. When you write col('daily_kw') > 0, you create a new Column object that represents the expression 'is daily_kw greater than zero?' Spark evaluates this expression for every row when an action triggers execution. The Column is the language you use to tell Spark what to compute, and Spark decides when and how to compute it.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col

# Each of these creates a Column object — no computation happens yet
ref       = col('daily_kw')              # simple reference
gt_zero   = col('daily_kw') > 0          # comparison expression
times_two = col('daily_kw') * 2          # arithmetic expression
renamed   = col('daily_kw').alias('kw')  # renamed reference
cast_int  = col('daily_kw').cast('int')  # type-cast expression

# All are Column objects
for expr in [ref, gt_zero, times_two, renamed, cast_int]:
    print(type(expr).__name__, ':', expr)

Column : Column<'daily_kw'>
Column : Column<'(daily_kw > 0)'>
Column : Column<'(daily_kw * 2)'>
Column : Column<'daily_kw AS kw'>
Column : Column<'CAST(daily_kw AS INT)'>
Composing Expressions

Column expressions can be combined to build more complex expressions. You can chain arithmetic operations, combine comparisons with logical operators, and nest function calls. Each combination produces a new Column object. This composability means you can build arbitrarily complex transformations from simple building blocks, all within the type-safe Column API. Spark compiles these expressions into optimized JVM operations when the plan executes.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, upper, length

# Composing expressions from simple Column building blocks

# Arithmetic chain: convert kW to MW and round
kw_to_mw = (col('daily_kw') / 1000).alias('daily_mw')

# Comparison chain: positive reading in NORTH region
north_positive = (col('daily_kw') > 0) & (col('region') == 'NORTH')

# Function chain: uppercase region and check its length
long_region = length(upper(col('region'))) > 4

# All of these are Column objects that can be used in select() and filter()
df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')
df.select('meter_id', kw_to_mw).show(3)
df.filter(north_positive).show(3)
Column vs String in the API

Many PySpark methods accept both strings and Column objects. When you pass a string like 'daily_kw' to select(), Spark internally converts it to col('daily_kw'). This means select('daily_kw') and select(col('daily_kw')) are identical. The string form is shorter and fine for simple column references. The Column form is required when you need expressions: select(col('daily_kw') * 1000) cannot be written as a string. NordGrid's convention is to use strings for simple references and col() when expressions are involved, keeping the code as readable as possible.

rows: You need to reference a column outside a specific DataFrame context

blocks.title: Columns are the vocabulary of PySpark
blocks.content: Every operation you learn from this point forward — select, filter, withColumn, groupBy, join — accepts Column expressions. The richer your Column vocabulary becomes (arithmetic, comparison, aliasing, casting, function calls), the more expressive your pipelines become. Think of Column objects as the words and operators as the grammar of the PySpark language.

select() keeps the columns you name. drop() does the opposite: it removes the columns you name and keeps everything else. Both produce a new DataFrame (immutability), and both serve the same purpose of shaping the column set. The choice between them depends on which is more convenient: if you want 4 columns out of 7, select() is cleaner. If you want to remove 2 columns out of 20, drop() is cleaner. This lesson covers drop()'s syntax, its behavior with non-existent columns, and the NordGrid patterns where drop() is the natural choice.
Basic Column Dropping

# Bronze table with ingestion metadata columns
df_bronze = spark.read.parquet('bronze/meters/')
print(f"Before: {df_bronze.columns}")

# Drop the columns that are only needed for Bronze-layer auditing
df_clean = df_bronze.drop('ingestion_ts', 'source_file', 'batch_id')
print(f"After:  {df_clean.columns}")

Before: ['meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'date', 'region', 'ingestion_ts', 'source_file', 'batch_id']
After:  ['meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'date', 'region']
drop() Is Safe with Missing Columns

One important difference between select() and drop(): if you try to select() a column that does not exist, Spark raises an AnalysisException. If you try to drop() a column that does not exist, Spark silently ignores it and returns the DataFrame unchanged. This makes drop() safer in pipelines where the input schema might vary — you can drop a column that may or may not be present without risking a crash. NordGrid uses this property when cleaning data from multiple source systems that have slightly different column sets.

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')
print(f"Columns: {df.columns}")

# Dropping a column that does not exist — no error
df_safe = df.drop('nonexistent_column')
print(f"After drop: {df_safe.columns}")  # unchanged, no crash

# Selecting a column that does not exist — raises an error
try:
    df.select('nonexistent_column').show()
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error: {e}")

Columns: ['meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'date', 'region']
After drop: ['meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'date', 'region']
Error: [UNRESOLVED_COLUMN.WITH_SUGGESTION] Column 'nonexistent_column' not found...
When to Use select() vs drop()

left.label: Use select() When
points: You want a specific set of output columns
Column order matters (select controls the order)
You are enforcing a schema contract at the end of a pipeline
You want expressions (computed columns, renames)

right.label: Use drop() When
points: You want to remove a few columns from a wide DataFrame
Column order does not matter
You are removing staging or temporary columns mid-pipeline
You just want to remove columns by name

blocks.title: NordGrid's convention
blocks.content: NordGrid uses drop() in the middle of pipelines to remove temporary columns (staging columns, intermediate flags). At the end of every pipeline, the team uses select() to enforce the output schema — listing exactly the columns the next layer expects, in the correct order. This convention makes the pipeline's output contract explicit and readable.

select() and drop() shape the columns. filter() shapes the rows. It takes a condition — a Column expression that evaluates to true or false for each row — and returns a new DataFrame containing only the rows where the condition is true. Rows where the condition is false or null are discarded. This is the PySpark equivalent of SQL's WHERE clause, and it is the most common way to remove unwanted data from a pipeline. NordGrid's Silver pipeline uses filter() to remove null readings, negative values, and rows from invalid regions.
Basic Filtering

from pyspark.sql.functions import col

df = spark.read.parquet('bronze/meters/')
print(f"Before filter: {df.count()} rows")

# Keep only rows where daily_kw is greater than zero
df_positive = df.filter(col('daily_kw') > 0)
print(f"After filter:  {df_positive.count()} rows")

df_positive.show(3)
Comparison Operators

Filter conditions use Python's standard comparison operators, applied to Column objects. The result is a Column expression of BooleanType that Spark evaluates for every row when an action triggers execution. PySpark supports all six comparison operators plus methods for null checks and string matching. Each operator creates a new Column expression that can be used directly in filter(), combined with other expressions using logical operators, or stored in a variable for reuse.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# Various filter patterns for NordGrid data
df.filter(col('region') == 'NORTH')                 # exact match
df.filter(col('region') != 'NORTH')                 # exclude one value
df.filter(col('region').isin('NORTH', 'SOUTH'))     # include a set of values
df.filter(col('daily_kw').between(100, 2000))       # range filter (inclusive)
df.filter(col('daily_kw') > 0)                      # threshold filter
df.filter(col('meter_id').startswith('M-00'))        # string prefix filter
filter() vs where()

PySpark provides two methods for filtering: filter() and where(). They are exact aliases — the same implementation, the same behavior, the same performance. The choice is purely a matter of style. Some teams prefer filter() because it describes the action (filtering rows). Others prefer where() because it mirrors SQL syntax (WHERE clause). NordGrid uses filter() consistently, but you should use whichever your team prefers and stick with it throughout the codebase.

# These two lines are identical in behavior and performance
df.filter(col('daily_kw') > 0)
df.where(col('daily_kw') > 0)

# NordGrid convention: use filter() everywhere
df_clean = df.filter(col('daily_kw') > 0)  # team standard

blocks.title: filter() is a transformation, not an action
blocks.content: Remember from Module 3, lesson 5: filter() is lazy. It records the filter condition in the plan but does not scan any data. If you chain three filters in a row, Spark combines them into a single compound filter during optimization. The data is scanned only once, when an action triggers execution. This means there is no performance cost to writing multiple readable filter() calls instead of one complex condition.

Real-world filters are rarely a single condition. NordGrid's Silver pipeline checks that daily_kw is positive AND not null AND the region is one of the four valid values AND the date is within the expected range. Combining multiple conditions into a single filter requires logical operators: AND, OR, and NOT. PySpark uses Python's bitwise operators for these — & for AND, | for OR, ~ for NOT — with a critical syntax requirement that catches every new PySpark developer: parentheses around each condition. This lesson covers the syntax, the parentheses rule, and the two patterns for combining conditions.
AND, OR, NOT Operators

from pyspark.sql.functions import col

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# AND: both conditions must be true
df.filter((col('daily_kw') > 0) & (col('region') == 'NORTH'))

# OR: at least one condition must be true
df.filter((col('region') == 'NORTH') | (col('region') == 'SOUTH'))

# NOT: invert the condition
df.filter(~(col('region') == 'NORTH'))  # all regions except NORTH

# Combined: positive readings in NORTH or SOUTH
df.filter(
    (col('daily_kw') > 0) & 
    ((col('region') == 'NORTH') | (col('region') == 'SOUTH'))
)
The Parentheses Rule

In Python, the bitwise operators & and | have higher precedence than comparison operators like > and ==. This means col('daily_kw') > 0 & col('region') == 'NORTH' is interpreted as col('daily_kw') > (0 & col('region')) == 'NORTH', which produces a confusing error. The fix is simple: wrap every individual condition in parentheses. This is not optional — it is required every time you combine conditions in PySpark. After a week of writing PySpark code, adding parentheses becomes automatic.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col

# WRONG — missing parentheses causes an operator precedence error
# df.filter(col('daily_kw') > 0 & col('region') == 'NORTH')  # TypeError!

# CORRECT — each condition wrapped in parentheses
df.filter((col('daily_kw') > 0) & (col('region') == 'NORTH'))
Pattern 1: Single filter() with Combined Conditions

You can combine all conditions into a single filter() call using & and |. This is compact and mirrors SQL WHERE clauses with AND/OR. It works well when conditions are short and the combined expression fits comfortably on a few lines, giving you a complete view of the filtering logic in one place. For complex conditions with many parts, however, this pattern can become hard to read and difficult to modify during code reviews.

# Pattern 1: all conditions in one filter()
df_clean = df.filter(
    (col('daily_kw').isNotNull()) &
    (col('daily_kw') > 0) &
    (col('daily_kw') < 10000) &
    (col('region').isin('NORTH', 'SOUTH', 'EAST', 'WEST'))
)
Pattern 2: Chained filter() Calls

Alternatively, you can chain multiple filter() calls, each with a single condition. Spark combines them into a single compound filter during optimization, so the performance is identical to Pattern 1. The advantage is readability: each line states one clear condition, and each condition can be commented independently. NordGrid's Silver pipelines use this pattern because code reviews found it easier to verify that each condition is correct when they are separated.

# Pattern 2: chained filter() calls (same performance, better readability)
df_clean = (df
    .filter(col('daily_kw').isNotNull())                        # remove nulls
    .filter(col('daily_kw') > 0)                               # remove negatives
    .filter(col('daily_kw') < 10000)                           # remove outliers
    .filter(col('region').isin('NORTH', 'SOUTH', 'EAST', 'WEST'))  # valid regions
)

blocks.title: Chained filters are free
blocks.content: Because of lazy evaluation, Spark combines chained filter() calls into a single optimized filter before executing anything. Writing df.filter(A).filter(B).filter(C) produces the exact same execution plan as df.filter(A & B & C). Choose the pattern that is easier to read and review. For NordGrid, that means chained filters with one condition per line and a comment explaining each condition's purpose.

Null values are the most common source of unexpected behavior in PySpark filters. A null in Spark is not zero, not an empty string, not False — it is the absence of a value. When NordGrid's meter M-004 has a sensor malfunction and produces no reading, the daily_kw column contains null for that row. The critical thing to understand about nulls is that any comparison with null produces null, not true or false. This means col('daily_kw') > 0 evaluates to null for rows where daily_kw is null, and filter() discards null results along with false results. Null rows silently disappear unless you handle them explicitly.
Three-Valued Logic

Most programming languages use two-valued logic: conditions are either true or false. SQL and Spark use three-valued logic: conditions can be true, false, or null. When one operand of a comparison is null, the result is null. This means null > 0 is null, null == null is null, and null != 'NORTH' is null. filter() keeps only rows where the condition is true — rows where the condition is false or null are both discarded. This behavior is correct (you cannot meaningfully compare an absent value), but it surprises developers who expect null comparisons to produce false.
Checking for Nulls

The isNull() and isNotNull() methods are the correct way to check for null values in PySpark. Never use == None or == null — it does not work because null == null evaluates to null, not true, and filter() discards null results along with false results. NordGrid's Silver pipeline always filters null readings explicitly as the very first step, before applying any other conditions, ensuring that all subsequent filters operate on known, non-null values.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col

df = spark.read.parquet('bronze/meters/')

# CORRECT: find null readings
null_readings = df.filter(col('daily_kw').isNull())
print(f"Null readings: {null_readings.count()}")

# CORRECT: find non-null readings
valid_readings = df.filter(col('daily_kw').isNotNull())
print(f"Valid readings: {valid_readings.count()}")

# WRONG: this does NOT find nulls (null == None evaluates to null, not true)
# wrong = df.filter(col('daily_kw') == None)  # always returns 0 rows!

Null readings: 150
Valid readings: 499850
Handling Nulls with dropna() and fillna()

PySpark provides convenience methods on the DataFrame's na accessor for common null-handling patterns. dropna() removes rows that contain null values. fillna() replaces null values with a specified default. Both methods can target specific columns rather than all columns, giving you precise control over which nulls to handle and how. NordGrid uses dropna(subset=['daily_kw']) to remove rows where the meter reading is missing, while keeping rows where other columns might be null.

df = spark.read.parquet('bronze/meters/')

# Drop rows where daily_kw is null (leave other columns untouched)
df_no_nulls = df.dropna(subset=['daily_kw'])

# Fill null daily_kw values with 0.0 instead of dropping them
df_filled = df.fillna({'daily_kw': 0.0})

# Drop rows where ANY column is null
df_all_clean = df.dropna(how='any')

# Drop rows where ALL columns are null
df_non_empty = df.dropna(how='all')

print(f"Original:   {df.count()} rows")
print(f"After drop: {df_no_nulls.count()} rows")
print(f"After fill: {df_filled.count()} rows (same count, nulls replaced)")

Original:   500000 rows
After drop: 499850 rows
After fill: 500000 rows (same count, nulls replaced)

blocks.title: Common mistake
blocks.content: Filtering with col('daily_kw') > 0 and assuming it keeps all valid readings. It does not keep rows where daily_kw is null — those are silently discarded because null > 0 is null, and filter discards nulls. If you need to keep nulls (perhaps to investigate them later), filter for non-null values explicitly first, or use separate filter paths for null and non-null data.

Every filter so far has used Column expressions: col('daily_kw') > 0, col('region') == 'NORTH'. PySpark also accepts SQL expression strings in filter() — you can write filter("daily_kw > 0") instead of filter(col('daily_kw') > 0). Additionally, the expr() function lets you embed SQL syntax inside any Column expression. These SQL alternatives are sometimes more readable for complex conditions, especially for engineers with a strong SQL background. This lesson covers both approaches and the trade-offs between them.
SQL Strings in filter()

When you pass a string to filter(), Spark parses it as a SQL WHERE expression. Column names are used directly without col() or quotes. Operators use SQL syntax: AND, OR, NOT, BETWEEN, IN, IS NULL. This can be more readable for complex conditions because SQL syntax is familiar to many engineers and avoids the parentheses requirement of Python's bitwise operators.

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# Column expression (Python style)
df.filter((col('daily_kw') > 0) & (col('region') == 'NORTH'))

# SQL string expression (SQL style) — same result
df.filter("daily_kw > 0 AND region = 'NORTH'")

# SQL strings shine for complex conditions
df.filter("daily_kw BETWEEN 100 AND 2000 AND region IN ('NORTH', 'SOUTH')")

# NULL checks in SQL style
df.filter("daily_kw IS NOT NULL")
df.filter("daily_kw IS NULL")
The expr() Function

The expr() function from pyspark.sql.functions converts a SQL string into a Column object. This lets you use SQL syntax inside select(), withColumn(), and any other method that accepts Column expressions — not just filter(). expr() is particularly useful for complex calculations that are verbose in the Column API but concise in SQL. NordGrid uses expr() sparingly for conditions that are cleaner in SQL, like CASE WHEN statements.

from pyspark.sql.functions import expr

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# expr() inside filter — same as a SQL string but explicit
df.filter(expr("daily_kw > 0 AND region = 'NORTH'"))

# expr() inside select — SQL syntax for computed columns
df.select(
    'meter_id',
    expr('daily_kw * 1000 AS daily_watts'),
    expr("CASE WHEN daily_kw > 2000 THEN 'high' ELSE 'normal' END AS tier"),
).show(3)
Column Expressions vs SQL Strings

left.label: Column Expressions (col())
points: Type-checked at plan build time
IDE autocompletion works
Composable — variables hold Column objects
Verbose for CASE WHEN and BETWEEN
NordGrid default for most pipelines

right.label: SQL Strings (filter string / expr())
points: Parsed at plan build time — typos caught later
No autocompletion inside strings
String concatenation for dynamic conditions (fragile)
Natural SQL syntax for complex conditions
NordGrid uses for complex expressions that are cleaner in SQL

blocks.title: Both produce the same execution plan
blocks.content: Column expressions and SQL strings produce identical execution plans. The Catalyst optimizer does not care which syntax you used — it sees the same logical plan either way. Choose based on readability: use Column expressions for simple conditions (the parentheses and col() make the code explicit), and SQL strings or expr() for complex conditions where SQL syntax is genuinely clearer.

Column names from source systems are often inconsistent, unclear, or simply different from what your pipeline's output schema requires. NordGrid's raw CSV exports use column names like kwh_reading and rgn_code, but the Silver schema expects daily_kw and region. Renaming columns is a routine step in every Bronze-to-Silver pipeline. PySpark provides two approaches: the alias() method inside select() and the standalone withColumnRenamed() method. Both produce the same result — a new DataFrame with the renamed column — but each has a natural use case.
alias() Inside select()

The alias() method is called on a Column expression to give it a new name in the output DataFrame. It is most commonly used inside select() when you are already choosing and transforming columns. This is the cleanest approach when you want to combine column selection, transformation, and renaming in a single step — which is the typical pattern in NordGrid's Bronze-to-Silver pipelines where raw column names must be standardized to match the Silver schema contract.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col

# Raw CSV has non-standard column names
df_raw = spark.read.csv('bronze/meters/raw_export.csv', header=True, inferSchema=True)
print(f"Raw columns: {df_raw.columns}")

# Rename in select: choose, transform, and rename in one step
df_silver = df_raw.select(
    col('mtr_id').alias('meter_id'),
    col('kwh_reading').alias('daily_kw'),
    col('reading_date').alias('date'),
    col('rgn_code').alias('region'),
)
print(f"Silver columns: {df_silver.columns}")
df_silver.show(3)
withColumnRenamed()

The withColumnRenamed() method renames a single column without affecting any other column in the DataFrame. It takes two arguments: the current name and the new name. This is convenient when you need to rename one or two columns in the middle of a transformation chain without rebuilding the entire select list. Like all DataFrame transformations, it returns a new DataFrame — the original is unchanged and the renamed column retains its data type and nullability.

# Rename one column at a time
df = df_raw.withColumnRenamed('mtr_id', 'meter_id')
df = df.withColumnRenamed('kwh_reading', 'daily_kw')
df = df.withColumnRenamed('reading_date', 'date')
df = df.withColumnRenamed('rgn_code', 'region')

print(f"Columns: {df.columns}")  # still has batch_num — not removed
Columns: ['meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'date', 'region', 'batch_num']

Notice that withColumnRenamed() keeps all columns — including batch_num, which the Silver schema does not need. The select-with-alias approach from the previous section is cleaner because it renames and selects simultaneously, producing exactly the columns you want. Use withColumnRenamed() when you need to rename a column in the middle of a transformation chain without changing the column set, and alias-in-select when you are defining the output schema at the end of a pipeline.

blocks.title: Renaming is part of the schema contract
blocks.content: Column names are part of the data contract between pipeline stages. When NordGrid's Silver layer expects a column called daily_kw, every Bronze-to-Silver pipeline must produce that exact name. Renaming is not cosmetic — it is a compliance step that ensures downstream consumers (the Gold pipeline, dashboards, billing systems) can find the columns they depend on.

Duplicate rows are a common data quality issue in data engineering. NordGrid's Bronze layer occasionally receives duplicate meter readings when ingestion windows overlap — the same meter's reading for the same date appears twice because two batch jobs ran within minutes of each other. If these duplicates reach the Silver layer, every downstream aggregate will be inflated: total consumption will be too high, average consumption will be skewed, and billing calculations will be wrong. PySpark provides two methods for removing duplicates: distinct() for full-row deduplication and dropDuplicates() for column-subset deduplication.
Full-Row Deduplication with distinct()

The distinct() method removes rows that are completely identical across all columns. If two rows have the same meter_id, daily_kw, date, and region, one of them is removed. If two rows share the same meter_id and date but have different daily_kw values (perhaps because the meter was re-read), both rows are kept because they are not identical. distinct() is the simplest deduplication method, but it requires exact matches across every column.

df = spark.read.parquet('bronze/meters/')
print(f"Before distinct: {df.count()} rows")

df_unique = df.distinct()
print(f"After distinct:  {df_unique.count()} rows")
print(f"Duplicates removed: {df.count() - df_unique.count()}")

Before distinct: 500000 rows
After distinct:  499200 rows
Duplicates removed: 800
Column-Subset Deduplication with dropDuplicates()

More often, you need to deduplicate based on a subset of columns. NordGrid's business rule is: one reading per meter per date. If meter M-001 has two readings for March 1st (perhaps with different ingestion timestamps), the pipeline should keep one and discard the other. dropDuplicates() takes a list of column names and keeps the first row encountered for each unique combination of those columns. The remaining columns (like ingestion_ts) may differ, but the row is considered a duplicate based on the specified subset.

df = spark.read.parquet('bronze/meters/')

# Deduplicate by meter_id and date — keep one reading per meter per day
df_deduped = df.dropDuplicates(['meter_id', 'date'])

print(f"Before: {df.count()} rows")
print(f"After:  {df_deduped.count()} rows")

# Verify: each meter_id + date combination appears exactly once
from pyspark.sql.functions import count
df_deduped.groupBy('meter_id', 'date').agg(count('*').alias('cnt')).filter('cnt > 1').show()

Before: 500000 rows
After:  499100 rows

+--------+----+---+
|meter_id|date|cnt|
+--------+----+---+
+--------+----+---+
(empty result — no duplicates remain)
distinct() vs dropDuplicates()

rows: You want exact-match deduplication across every column
You want to keep one row per unique key (e.g., one reading per meter per date)
Identical to distinct() — same behavior, different name

blocks.title: dropDuplicates() keeps an arbitrary row
blocks.content: When dropDuplicates() finds multiple rows with the same key, it keeps one and discards the rest. Which one it keeps is not guaranteed — it depends on partition order, which is non-deterministic. If you need to keep a specific row (for example, the one with the latest ingestion timestamp), you need a different approach: a window function with row_number(), which you will learn in Module 9. For NordGrid's meter readings, the daily_kw values in duplicate rows are typically identical, so keeping an arbitrary row is safe.

You now know how to select columns, drop columns, filter rows, combine filter conditions, handle nulls, use SQL expressions, rename columns, and remove duplicates. In practice, these operations are not used in isolation — they are chained together into multi-step transformation pipelines. PySpark's immutable, lazy DataFrame API is designed for chaining: each method returns a new DataFrame, so you can call the next method directly on the result. This lesson covers the chaining pattern, the readability conventions that NordGrid follows, and how lazy evaluation makes long chains efficient.
The Chaining Pattern

Method chaining means calling one method after another on the result of the previous call, without storing intermediate variables. In PySpark, this looks like a pipeline that reads top-to-bottom: each line is one transformation, and the final line is either another transformation or an action. The backslash at the end of each line continues the statement to the next line, and parentheses around the entire chain serve the same purpose more cleanly.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col

# Method chaining with parentheses — NordGrid's preferred style
df_silver = (
    spark.read.parquet('bronze/meters/')
    .filter(col('daily_kw').isNotNull())          # remove null readings
    .filter(col('daily_kw') > 0)                  # remove negative readings
    .filter(col('daily_kw') < 10000)              # remove outliers
    .dropDuplicates(['meter_id', 'date'])          # one reading per meter per day
    .select(
        col('meter_id'),
        col('daily_kw'),
        col('date'),
        col('region'),
    )
)

df_silver.show(5)
Readability Conventions

NordGrid's coding standard defines three readability rules for chained transformations. First, one transformation per line — each method call gets its own line so that code reviews can evaluate each step independently. Second, inline comments explain why, not what — the code already says what (filter, select, drop), so comments explain the business reason (remove nulls, enforce schema contract). Third, the final select() defines the output contract — the last step is always a select() listing exactly the columns the downstream consumer expects.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col

# NordGrid Bronze-to-Silver pipeline: chained with clear conventions
df_silver = (
    spark.read.csv('bronze/meters/', header=True, schema=meter_schema)

    # Data quality filters
    .filter(col('daily_kw').isNotNull())          # sensor malfunctions produce nulls
    .filter(col('daily_kw') > 0)                  # negative readings are sensor errors
    .filter(col('daily_kw') < 10000)              # readings above 10MW are physically impossible

    # Deduplication
    .dropDuplicates(['meter_id', 'date'])          # overlapping ingestion windows cause dupes

    # Column renaming (raw CSV uses non-standard names)
    .withColumnRenamed('rgn_code', 'region')

    # Output schema contract — exactly the columns Silver expects
    .select('meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'date', 'region')
)

df_silver.write.mode('overwrite').parquet('silver/meters/')
Why Chaining Is Efficient

A chain of eight transformations might look expensive, but remember: all of these are lazy. Spark does not execute each step sequentially. It records the entire chain as a plan, passes the plan to the Catalyst optimizer, and the optimizer fuses the steps into the most efficient execution possible. The three filter() calls become a single compound filter. The select() at the end triggers column pruning at the read step. The entire chain executes in one pass over the data, not eight. This is why NordGrid's pipelines can chain 10–15 transformations without performance concerns — the chain's length affects readability, not execution cost.

blocks.title: What you write vs what Spark executes
blocks.content:
What You Write (8 steps)             What Spark Executes (1 optimized pass)
──────────────────────             ─────────────────────────────

1. read csv                          Read csv:
2. filter isNotNull                    • Read only 4 columns (pruned)
3. filter > 0                          • Apply compound filter:
4. filter < 10000                        NOT NULL AND > 0 AND < 10000
5. dropDuplicates                      • Deduplicate by (meter_id, date)
6. withColumnRenamed                   • Rename rgn_code → region
7. select 4 columns                 Write parquet
8. write parquet

Lazy evaluation lets Catalyst fuse 8 steps into 1 optimized pass.
blocks.caption: Lazy evaluation means the chain's length does not determine the number of data passes. Catalyst optimizes the entire chain into the fewest possible operations.

blocks.title: Module 4 complete — you can shape data
blocks.content: You now have the core data shaping toolkit: select() to choose columns, drop() to remove them, filter() to choose rows, alias() to rename columns, and dropDuplicates() to remove duplicates. You know how to combine filter conditions with &, |, and ~, how to handle null values, and how to use SQL expressions as an alternative syntax. Most importantly, you know how to chain these operations into readable, efficient pipelines. In Module 5, you will learn to add and transform columns — the operations that create new data from existing data.

chapters.title: Adding and Transforming Columns
chapters.description: Learn to create new columns, modify existing values, and apply the full range of column-level transformations that turn raw data into analytical assets.

blocks.content: Module 4 taught you to shape data by choosing which columns and rows to keep. This module teaches the next essential skill: creating new columns and transforming existing values. The primary method for both operations is withColumn(). It takes two arguments — a column name and a Column expression — and returns a new DataFrame with the specified column added or replaced. If the column name does not exist, withColumn() adds it. If it does exist, withColumn() replaces its values with the result of the expression. As with every DataFrame transformation, withColumn() does not modify the original — it creates a new immutable DataFrame.
Adding a New Column

The most common use of withColumn() is adding a derived column — a column whose values are computed from one or more existing columns. NordGrid's Silver pipeline adds a year column extracted from the date column, a daily_mw column computed by dividing daily_kw by 1,000, and an is_valid boolean flag. Each of these is a new column that did not exist in the Bronze data but is needed by downstream consumers.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, year, lit

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')
print(f"Before: {df.columns}")

# Add a 'year' column derived from the 'date' column
df = df.withColumn('year', year(col('date')))

# Add a 'daily_mw' column computed from 'daily_kw'
df = df.withColumn('daily_mw', col('daily_kw') / 1000)

# Add a constant column using lit()
df = df.withColumn('source', lit('bronze_meters'))

print(f"After:  {df.columns}")
df.show(3)
Replacing an Existing Column

If you pass a column name that already exists, withColumn() replaces that column's values with the new expression. The column stays in its original position in the DataFrame. This is the pattern for transforming values in place — for example, converting daily_kw from a raw string to a properly typed double, or rounding a floating-point column to two decimal places. NordGrid uses this pattern during Bronze-to-Silver transformation when the raw CSV column needs to be cleaned but should keep the same name.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, round as spark_round, upper

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# Replace daily_kw with a rounded version
df = df.withColumn('daily_kw', spark_round(col('daily_kw'), 1))

# Replace region with uppercase version (already uppercase, but demonstrating the pattern)
df = df.withColumn('region', upper(col('region')))

df.show(3)
The lit() Function for Constants

The second argument to withColumn() must be a Column expression. If you want to add a column with a constant value — the same value for every row — you cannot pass a plain Python value like 'bronze' or 42. You must wrap it in the lit() function, which creates a Column expression from a literal value. NordGrid uses lit() to tag rows with metadata: a source label, a pipeline version number, or a processing timestamp. Forgetting lit() when adding a constant is one of the most common beginner errors in PySpark.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, lit, current_timestamp

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# CORRECT: use lit() for constant values
df = df.withColumn('pipeline_version', lit(3))
df = df.withColumn('processed_at', current_timestamp())  # built-in, no lit needed

# WRONG: this raises a TypeError
# df = df.withColumn('pipeline_version', 3)  # TypeError: not a Column

blocks.title: Common mistake
blocks.content: Writing df.withColumn('flag', True) instead of df.withColumn('flag', lit(True)). The second argument to withColumn() must always be a Column expression. Plain Python values (strings, numbers, booleans) must be wrapped in lit(). The error message — 'col should be Column' — is the signal that you forgot lit().

In Module 4, lesson 8 you learned to rename columns using alias() inside select() and withColumnRenamed() for single-column renames. This lesson expands on renaming with a third technique — toDF() for renaming all columns at once — and establishes the NordGrid conventions for when each approach is appropriate. Renaming might seem like a minor operation, but in data engineering pipelines it is a critical step: downstream systems expect exact column names, and a single misspelling breaks the data contract. Consistent renaming patterns make pipelines reliable and code reviews efficient.
Three Renaming Techniques

rows: Renaming during column selection and transformation
Renaming one column without changing the column set
Renaming all columns at once when importing from a different schema

blocks.content: Renaming All Columns with toDF()

The toDF() method takes a list of new column names and returns a new DataFrame with those names applied positionally — the first name replaces the first column, the second replaces the second, and so on. This is useful when the source has completely different names and you want to replace them all in one step. NordGrid uses toDF() when reading data from an external partner whose CSV headers use a naming convention different from NordGrid's standard. The risk with toDF() is that it relies on column position, not column name — if the source reorders its columns, the names silently mismatch.

# External partner sends data with their own column names
df_partner = spark.read.csv('bronze/partner_export.csv', header=True, inferSchema=True)
print(f"Partner columns: {df_partner.columns}")

# Rename all columns at once using toDF()
df_nordgrid = df_partner.toDF('meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'date', 'region')
print(f"NordGrid columns: {df_nordgrid.columns}")

Partner columns: ['device_code', 'energy_kwh', 'read_date', 'zone']
NordGrid columns: ['meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'date', 'region']
Programmatic Renaming

When column names follow a pattern that needs standardization — converting from camelCase to snake_case, stripping prefixes, or replacing spaces with underscores — you can rename columns programmatically using a Python loop over the column list. Each iteration calls withColumnRenamed(), building up a chain of renames. This pattern is common when ingesting data from systems that use conventions incompatible with Spark's preferred snake_case naming.
['meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'region_code']

blocks.title: NordGrid convention: alias-in-select at pipeline boundaries
blocks.content: NordGrid uses alias() inside select() at the end of every pipeline to define the output schema. This makes the output contract explicit: anyone reading the last select() sees exactly which columns are produced and what they are named. withColumnRenamed() and toDF() are used mid-pipeline when they are more convenient, but the final select-with-alias is the authoritative definition of the output schema.

When NordGrid reads meter data from CSV files in the Bronze layer, every column arrives as a string — even the daily_kw values that should be numbers and the date values that should be dates. CSV has no type metadata, so Spark treats everything as text unless you provide an explicit schema or use inferSchema. The operation that converts a column from one data type to another is called casting. It is one of the most common transformations in Bronze-to-Silver pipelines because raw data almost never arrives with the correct types. This lesson covers the cast() method, the most frequent casting patterns, and what happens when casting fails.
The cast() Method

The cast() method is called on a Column object and takes a target type as either a string or a Spark type object. It returns a new Column expression that converts the original column's values to the specified type. You use cast() inside withColumn() to replace a column with its properly typed version, or inside select() to cast and rename in one step.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col
from pyspark.sql.types import DoubleType, DateType, IntegerType

# Bronze CSV: all columns are strings
df = spark.read.csv('bronze/meters/', header=True)  # no inferSchema = all strings
df.printSchema()

# Cast string columns to proper types
df = df.withColumn('daily_kw', col('daily_kw').cast(DoubleType()))
df = df.withColumn('date', col('date').cast(DateType()))

# String shorthand works too
df = df.withColumn('daily_kw', col('daily_kw').cast('double'))
df = df.withColumn('date', col('date').cast('date'))

df.printSchema()

root
 |-- meter_id: string (nullable = true)
 |-- daily_kw: string (nullable = true)
 |-- date: string (nullable = true)
 |-- region: string (nullable = true)

root
 |-- meter_id: string (nullable = true)
 |-- daily_kw: double (nullable = true)
 |-- date: date (nullable = true)
 |-- region: string (nullable = true)
Common Casting Patterns
What Happens When Casting Fails

When Spark cannot convert a value to the target type, it does not throw an error — it silently produces null. If a daily_kw string value is 'N/A' instead of a number, casting it to double produces null. This is both convenient (the pipeline does not crash on bad data) and dangerous (you may not realize that valid-looking data has been silently replaced with nulls). NordGrid's Silver pipeline includes a count check after casting: if the number of nulls increased after the cast, the pipeline logs a data quality warning.

# Demonstrate silent null on failed cast
df_raw = spark.createDataFrame(
    [('M-001', '1250.5'), ('M-002', 'N/A'), ('M-003', '2100.0')],
    schema=['meter_id', 'daily_kw']
)

df_cast = df_raw.withColumn('daily_kw', col('daily_kw').cast('double'))
df_cast.show()

# 'N/A' becomes null silently — no error, no warning

blocks.title: Silent nulls after casting
blocks.content: Casting never throws an error on bad data — it produces null instead. Always count nulls before and after a cast operation to detect data quality issues. If the null count increases, some values could not be converted. NordGrid's pipeline template includes this check: null_count_before = df.filter(col('daily_kw').isNull()).count() followed by the cast, then null_count_after to compare.

Meter IDs, region codes, customer names, file paths — many columns in NordGrid's data are strings, and they frequently need cleaning or transformation before they can be used reliably. Strings arrive with inconsistent casing (north vs NORTH vs North), extra whitespace, unexpected prefixes or suffixes, and encoding artifacts. PySpark provides a rich set of string functions in pyspark.sql.functions that handle these problems at scale. These functions operate on Column expressions and are executed by the JVM engine on the executors, making them far faster than equivalent Python string operations.
Case Conversion and Trimming

The most common string operations are case conversion and whitespace trimming. upper() and lower() standardize casing so that 'north', 'North', and 'NORTH' all become the same value. trim(), ltrim(), and rtrim() remove leading and trailing whitespace that causes silent mismatches in filters and joins. NordGrid's Bronze layer sometimes contains region codes with trailing spaces from the source CSV export, which causes join failures when matching against reference tables that do not have the extra space.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, upper, lower, trim, ltrim, rtrim

df = spark.createDataFrame(
    [('M-001', '  north  '), ('M-002', 'South'), ('M-003', 'EAST  ')],
    schema=['meter_id', 'region']
)

# Standardize: trim whitespace, then uppercase
df = df.withColumn('region', upper(trim(col('region'))))
df.show()
Substring, Concatenation, and Length

substring() extracts a portion of a string by position. concat() and concat_ws() join multiple columns or literal values into a single string, with concat_ws() inserting a separator between each value. length() returns the character count of a string. NordGrid uses substring() to extract the region prefix from composite meter IDs where the first two characters indicate the geographic region, and concat_ws() to build composite keys for join operations that require a single unique identifier.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, substring, concat, concat_ws, length, lit

df = spark.createDataFrame(
    [('NO-M-001', 1250.5, '2026-03-01'), ('SO-M-002', 870.2, '2026-03-01')],
    schema=['meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'date']
)

# Extract region prefix from meter_id (first 2 characters)
df = df.withColumn('region_code', substring(col('meter_id'), 1, 2))

# Build a composite key: meter_id + date
df = df.withColumn('reading_key', concat_ws('_', col('meter_id'), col('date')))

# Check meter_id length
df = df.withColumn('id_length', length(col('meter_id')))

df.show(truncate=False)
Pattern Matching with regexp_replace and regexp_extract

When simple string functions are not enough, PySpark provides regexp_replace() and regexp_extract() for regular expression operations. regexp_replace() substitutes all matches of a pattern with a replacement string. regexp_extract() pulls out the first match of a capturing group. NordGrid uses regexp_replace() to strip non-numeric characters from meter IDs when integrating with legacy systems that only accept numeric identifiers, and regexp_extract() to parse structured codes from free-text fields.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, regexp_replace, regexp_extract

df = spark.createDataFrame(
    [('NO-M-001',), ('SO-M-002',), ('EA-M-003',)],
    schema=['meter_id']
)

# Strip everything except digits
df = df.withColumn('numeric_id', regexp_replace(col('meter_id'), '[^0-9]', ''))

# Extract the region prefix (letters before the first dash)
df = df.withColumn('region_prefix', regexp_extract(col('meter_id'), r'^([A-Z]+)', 1))

df.show()

blocks.title: Built-in string functions run on the JVM
blocks.content: Every string function in pyspark.sql.functions executes on the JVM inside the executors, at native speed. This is orders of magnitude faster than applying a Python string method row by row with a UDF. Always check whether a built-in function exists before writing custom Python logic. For most string operations — case conversion, trimming, splitting, replacing, extracting patterns — the built-in functions cover exactly what you need.

NordGrid's data is fundamentally temporal — every meter reading has a date, every SCADA telemetry record has a timestamp, and every dashboard metric is aggregated by day, month, or quarter. Working with dates and timestamps is one of the most frequent operations in NordGrid's pipelines. PySpark provides a comprehensive set of date functions for extracting parts of a date, performing date arithmetic, and formatting dates for output. This lesson covers the functions you will use most often and the NordGrid patterns for temporal transformations.
Extracting Date Parts

The most common temporal operation is extracting a component — year, month, day, day of week — from a date or timestamp column. These extraction functions take a date Column and return an integer Column. NordGrid uses year() and month() to create partition columns for Silver-layer storage, and dayofweek() to identify weekend readings that have different consumption patterns than weekday readings.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, year, month, dayofmonth, dayofweek, quarter

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# Extract date parts into new columns
df = (df
    .withColumn('year', year(col('date')))
    .withColumn('month', month(col('date')))
    .withColumn('day', dayofmonth(col('date')))
    .withColumn('dow', dayofweek(col('date')))    # 1=Sunday, 7=Saturday
    .withColumn('quarter', quarter(col('date')))
)

df.select('date', 'year', 'month', 'day', 'dow', 'quarter').show(3)
Date Arithmetic

Date arithmetic lets you add or subtract days from a date, compute the number of days between two dates, or compute the number of months between two dates. NordGrid uses date_add() to compute the expected next reading date for each meter (today + 1 day), and datediff() to identify meters that have not reported in more than 3 days — a sign of a communication failure that the operations team needs to investigate.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, current_date, date_add, date_sub, datediff, months_between

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# Add 1 day to get the expected next reading date
df = df.withColumn('next_reading', date_add(col('date'), 1))

# Subtract 7 days to get the start of the lookback window
df = df.withColumn('week_ago', date_sub(col('date'), 7))

# Days since this reading (compared to today)
df = df.withColumn('days_since', datediff(current_date(), col('date')))

# Months between the reading and a reference date
df = df.withColumn('months_from_jan', months_between(col('date'), lit('2026-01-01').cast('date')))

df.select('date', 'next_reading', 'week_ago', 'days_since').show(3)
Formatting Dates

The date_format() function converts a date or timestamp column to a string in a specified format. This is useful when your output needs dates in a particular format for a downstream system or report that does not accept the ISO standard. NordGrid's regulatory reports require dates in the European format (DD.MM.YYYY), which differs from Spark's default ISO format (YYYY-MM-DD). The format string follows Java's SimpleDateFormat patterns, where yyyy is the four-digit year, MM is the two-digit month, and dd is the two-digit day.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, date_format

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# ISO format (default): 2026-03-01
df = df.withColumn('date_iso', date_format(col('date'), 'yyyy-MM-dd'))

# European format: 01.03.2026
df = df.withColumn('date_eu', date_format(col('date'), 'dd.MM.yyyy'))

# Month name: March 2026
df = df.withColumn('date_label', date_format(col('date'), 'MMMM yyyy'))

df.select('date', 'date_iso', 'date_eu', 'date_label').show(3)

blocks.title: Keep dates as DateType, format only at the output boundary
blocks.content: Store and process dates as DateType or TimestampType throughout your pipeline. Only convert to formatted strings at the very last step, when writing to a system that requires a specific format. DateType columns support arithmetic, comparison, and extraction functions. String columns do not. Formatting too early means losing the ability to use date functions on that column.

Energy data is inherently numeric, and NordGrid's pipelines perform arithmetic constantly: converting kilowatts to megawatts, computing percentage changes between periods, calculating consumption per customer, and rounding values for billing. PySpark supports arithmetic directly on Column objects using Python's standard operators, plus a library of mathematical functions for rounding, absolute values, and more. These operations execute on the JVM at native speed and are automatically parallelized across all executors.
Column Arithmetic

You can use Python's arithmetic operators — +, -, *, / — directly on Column objects. Each operation creates a new Column expression that Spark evaluates for every row when an action triggers execution. When the expression is used in withColumn() or select(), Spark computes it across all partitions in parallel. If either operand is null, the result is null, following the same three-valued logic you learned in Module 4, lesson 6.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, lit

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# Unit conversion: kW to MW
df = df.withColumn('daily_mw', col('daily_kw') / 1000)

# Unit conversion: kW to watts
df = df.withColumn('daily_watts', col('daily_kw') * 1000)

# Compute cost at $0.12 per kW
df = df.withColumn('daily_cost', col('daily_kw') * lit(0.12))

# Percentage of a 5000 kW maximum
df = df.withColumn('pct_max', (col('daily_kw') / 5000) * 100)

df.select('meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'daily_mw', 'daily_cost', 'pct_max').show(3)
Mathematical Functions

PySpark's functions module includes mathematical functions for rounding, absolute values, powers, square roots, and more. The most frequently used at NordGrid are round() for billing calculations where amounts must be rounded to two decimal places, abs() for computing the magnitude of deviations from expected consumption regardless of direction, and greatest() and least() for finding the maximum or minimum across multiple columns in the same row.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, round as spark_round, abs as spark_abs, pow, sqrt, greatest, least

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# Round to 2 decimal places (for billing)
df = df.withColumn('daily_kw_rounded', spark_round(col('daily_kw'), 2))

# Absolute value (useful when computing deviations from a baseline)
baseline = 1200
df = df.withColumn('deviation', spark_abs(col('daily_kw') - baseline))

# Square root and power
df = df.withColumn('sqrt_kw', sqrt(col('daily_kw')))
df = df.withColumn('kw_squared', pow(col('daily_kw'), 2))
The Modulo Operator

The modulo operator (%) returns the remainder after division. NordGrid uses it for partitioning meters into processing buckets for load testing: meter IDs are hashed to an integer, and modulo determines which bucket a meter belongs to. It is also useful for identifying even versus odd values, for cycling through categories in round-robin fashion, and for implementing simple hash-based sampling strategies on large datasets.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, hash as spark_hash

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# Assign each meter to one of 4 test buckets using hash + modulo
df = df.withColumn('bucket', spark_hash(col('meter_id')) % 4)

# Identify even vs odd day-of-month
from pyspark.sql.functions import dayofmonth
df = df.withColumn('even_day', (dayofmonth(col('date')) % 2 == 0))

df.select('meter_id', 'bucket', 'date', 'even_day').show(4)

blocks.title: Arithmetic propagates nulls
blocks.content: Any arithmetic involving a null value produces null. If daily_kw is null, then daily_kw / 1000 is null, daily_kw * 0.12 is null, and daily_kw + 100 is null. This is consistent with Spark's three-valued logic. If you need nulls to be treated as zero for a calculation, use coalesce(col('daily_kw'), lit(0)) before the arithmetic. You will learn coalesce and other null-handling functions in Module 9.

Not every transformation applies uniformly to all rows. NordGrid categorizes meter readings into consumption tiers: readings below 500 kW are 'low', between 500 and 2000 kW are 'medium', between 2000 and 4000 kW are 'high', and above 4000 kW are 'critical'. This categorization depends on the value of daily_kw — different rows get different labels. PySpark's when() and otherwise() functions provide the equivalent of SQL's CASE WHEN: they evaluate conditions in order and return the value for the first condition that matches. This is the standard pattern for conditional column values in PySpark.
The when().otherwise() Pattern

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, when

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# Categorize readings into consumption tiers
df = df.withColumn('tier',
    when(col('daily_kw') < 500, 'low')
    .when(col('daily_kw') < 2000, 'medium')
    .when(col('daily_kw') < 4000, 'high')
    .otherwise('critical')
)

df.select('meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'tier').show(5)
How Conditions Are Evaluated

The when() chain evaluates conditions from top to bottom and returns the value for the first condition that is true. Once a condition matches, the remaining conditions are not evaluated. This means order matters: if you put when(col('daily_kw') < 2000, 'medium') before when(col('daily_kw') < 500, 'low'), every reading below 500 kW would be classified as 'medium' because < 2000 matches first. Always order conditions from most specific to least specific, or from smallest threshold to largest.

If no condition matches and you omitted otherwise(), the result is null for that row. NordGrid's coding convention requires that every when() chain includes an otherwise() clause, even if the default value is just 'unknown' or lit(None). This explicit default makes the logic complete, communicates intent clearly to code reviewers, and prevents unexpected nulls from propagating silently into downstream aggregations and joins where they would cause incorrect results.
Combining when() with Other Expressions

The when().otherwise() chain is a Column expression, which means it can be used anywhere Column expressions are accepted: inside withColumn(), inside select(), inside filter(), and as an argument to other functions. You can also use when() to return computed values rather than string literals — for example, applying different pricing formulas depending on the consumption tier, where each branch multiplies daily_kw by a different rate.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, when, lit

# Tiered pricing: different rate per kW depending on consumption level
df = df.withColumn('daily_cost',
    when(col('daily_kw') < 500,  col('daily_kw') * 0.08)
    .when(col('daily_kw') < 2000, col('daily_kw') * 0.12)
    .when(col('daily_kw') < 4000, col('daily_kw') * 0.18)
    .otherwise(col('daily_kw') * 0.25)
)

df.select('meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'tier', 'daily_cost').show(4)

blocks.title: when() is the PySpark equivalent of CASE WHEN
blocks.content: If you know SQL, when().when().otherwise() maps directly to CASE WHEN condition1 THEN value1 WHEN condition2 THEN value2 ELSE default END. The logic is identical. The difference is syntax: PySpark chains method calls, SQL uses keywords. When reviewing NordGrid pipelines, you can mentally translate any when() chain to a CASE WHEN statement and vice versa.

Boolean columns hold true or false values and serve as reusable flags in data pipelines. Instead of repeating a complex filter condition in multiple places, you can compute it once as a boolean column and then filter on it, join on it, or expose it to downstream consumers. NordGrid's Silver layer includes boolean flag columns like is_valid, is_peak_hour, and is_weekend that encode data quality and business logic. These flags are computed during the Silver transformation and consumed by every Gold pipeline that reads the Silver data, eliminating the need to recompute the same conditions repeatedly.
Creating Boolean Columns

A boolean column is created by assigning a comparison or logical expression to a new column with withColumn(). The expression evaluates to true, false, or null for each row. NordGrid's is_valid flag combines three conditions: the reading must be non-null, positive, and below the physical maximum of 10,000 kW. Computing this flag once in the Silver pipeline means every downstream consumer can simply filter on is_valid without knowing the underlying rules.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, dayofweek, hour

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# Data quality flag: is this a valid reading?
df = df.withColumn('is_valid',
    col('daily_kw').isNotNull() &
    (col('daily_kw') > 0) &
    (col('daily_kw') < 10000)
)

# Business logic flag: is this a weekend reading?
# dayofweek: 1=Sunday, 7=Saturday
df = df.withColumn('is_weekend',
    (dayofweek(col('date')) == 1) | (dayofweek(col('date')) == 7)
)

df.select('meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'date', 'is_valid', 'is_weekend').show(5)
Filtering on Boolean Columns

Once a boolean column exists, filtering on it is simple and expressive. Instead of repeating the entire condition, you filter on the flag name. This makes pipelines more readable and ensures consistency — every consumer uses the same definition of 'valid' because they all filter on the same column. NordGrid's Gold pipelines always start with .filter(col('is_valid')) as their first transformation.

# Filter using the boolean column directly — clean and readable
df_valid = df.filter(col('is_valid'))
df_invalid = df.filter(~col('is_valid'))
df_weekday_valid = df.filter(col('is_valid') & ~col('is_weekend'))

print(f"Valid readings:          {df_valid.count()}")
print(f"Invalid readings:        {df_invalid.count()}")
print(f"Valid weekday readings:  {df_weekday_valid.count()}")

Valid readings:          498750
Invalid readings:        1250
Valid weekday readings:  356250

blocks.title: Flags encode business rules
blocks.content: Boolean flag columns turn complex business logic into simple, reusable labels. The definition of 'valid' (non-null, positive, below 10K) lives in one place — the Silver pipeline that creates the is_valid column. Every downstream consumer trusts the flag without needing to know the rules behind it. If the definition of 'valid' changes (say, the maximum is lowered to 8,000 kW), you change it in one pipeline and every consumer automatically uses the new definition.

In Module 4, lesson 7 you saw that expr() converts SQL syntax into Column expressions. This lesson expands on that introduction because expr() becomes increasingly useful as your transformations grow more complex. Some operations that require several lines of chained Column methods can be written as a single concise expr() call. NordGrid's coding standard allows expr() for complex derived columns where SQL syntax is genuinely more readable, while using the Column API for simple transformations. Understanding both approaches and when each is appropriate gives you flexibility in writing pipelines that your team can maintain.
expr() for Complex Derived Columns

The expr() function parses a SQL expression string and returns a Column object. This Column can be used in withColumn(), select(), filter(), and any other method that accepts columns. The power of expr() is that it supports the full SQL expression syntax: CASE WHEN, BETWEEN, IN, CAST, arithmetic, function calls, and nested expressions — all in a single string that reads like SQL.

from pyspark.sql.functions import expr

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# Complex derived column: much cleaner in expr() than chained Column methods
df = df.withColumn('billing_category', expr("""
    CASE
        WHEN daily_kw IS NULL THEN 'missing'
        WHEN daily_kw <= 0 THEN 'invalid'
        WHEN daily_kw < 500 THEN 'residential_low'
        WHEN daily_kw < 2000 THEN 'residential_standard'
        WHEN daily_kw < 5000 THEN 'commercial'
        ELSE 'industrial'
    END
"""))

df.select('meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'billing_category').show(5)
expr() in select()

Using expr() inside select() lets you compute, rename, and cast columns in a compact SQL-like syntax that reads naturally to engineers with a SQL background. This is particularly useful when defining the output of a transformation pipeline where you might be selecting 10 or more columns with various transformations applied to each. NordGrid's Silver pipeline uses this pattern for the final select statement that defines the output schema contract.

from pyspark.sql.functions import expr

df = spark.read.csv('bronze/meters/', header=True)

# Compact transformation using expr() in select()
df_silver = df.select(
    expr('meter_id'),
    expr('CAST(daily_kw AS DOUBLE) AS daily_kw'),
    expr('CAST(date AS DATE) AS date'),
    expr('UPPER(TRIM(region)) AS region'),
    expr('CAST(daily_kw AS DOUBLE) / 1000 AS daily_mw'),
    expr('YEAR(CAST(date AS DATE)) AS year'),
)

df_silver.printSchema()
df_silver.show(3)

blocks.title: Use the right tool for the readability
blocks.content: The Column API and expr() produce identical execution plans. Choose based on which makes the code clearer. For simple operations like col('daily_kw') > 0 or col('daily_kw') / 1000, the Column API is more explicit and benefits from IDE support. For complex multi-branch CASE WHEN statements, nested function calls, or transformations that read naturally in SQL, expr() is cleaner. NordGrid allows both in the same pipeline — consistency within a pipeline matters more than a blanket choice across all pipelines.

This lesson brings together every technique from the module into a complete NordGrid Bronze-to-Silver transformation pipeline. The pipeline reads raw CSV data, casts string columns to proper types, trims and standardizes text columns, adds derived temporal fields, computes data quality flags, categorizes readings into consumption tiers, and selects the final Silver schema. Each step uses a technique you learned in this module. Walking through the complete pipeline shows how the individual operations compose into a real-world transformation.
The Complete Bronze-to-Silver Pipeline

from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import (
    col, year, month, quarter, dayofweek,
    upper, trim, round as spark_round,
    when, lit, current_timestamp
)
from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StructField, StringType, DoubleType, DateType

spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .appName('nordgrid-daily-meters-bronze-to-silver') \
    .config('spark.sql.shuffle.partitions', '16') \
    .getOrCreate()

# Explicit schema for the Bronze CSV
bronze_schema = StructType([
    StructField('mtr_id',      StringType(), True),
    StructField('kwh_reading', StringType(), True),  # arrives as string in CSV
    StructField('reading_date',StringType(), True),  # arrives as string in CSV
    StructField('rgn_code',    StringType(), True),
    StructField('batch_num',   StringType(), True),
])

try:
    # READ
    df = spark.read.csv('bronze/meters/', header=True, schema=bronze_schema)

    # TYPE CASTING (lesson 3)
    df = df.withColumn('daily_kw', col('kwh_reading').cast('double'))
    df = df.withColumn('date', col('reading_date').cast('date'))

    # STRING CLEANING (lesson 4)
    df = df.withColumn('region', upper(trim(col('rgn_code'))))

    # DATE DERIVATIONS (lesson 5)
    df = df.withColumn('year', year(col('date')))
    df = df.withColumn('month', month(col('date')))
    df = df.withColumn('quarter', quarter(col('date')))

    # ARITHMETIC (lesson 6)
    df = df.withColumn('daily_kw', spark_round(col('daily_kw'), 2))

    # CONDITIONAL LOGIC (lesson 7)
    df = df.withColumn('tier',
        when(col('daily_kw') < 500, 'low')
        .when(col('daily_kw') < 2000, 'medium')
        .when(col('daily_kw') < 4000, 'high')
        .otherwise('critical')
    )

    # BOOLEAN FLAGS (lesson 8)
    df = df.withColumn('is_valid',
        col('daily_kw').isNotNull() &
        (col('daily_kw') > 0) &
        (col('daily_kw') < 10000)
    )
    df = df.withColumn('is_weekend',
        (dayofweek(col('date')) == 1) | (dayofweek(col('date')) == 7)
    )

    # METADATA (lesson 1 — lit())
    df = df.withColumn('processed_at', current_timestamp())

    # OUTPUT SCHEMA CONTRACT (select with alias)
    df_silver = df.select(
        col('mtr_id').alias('meter_id'),
        'daily_kw', 'date', 'region',
        'year', 'month', 'quarter',
        'tier', 'is_valid', 'is_weekend',
        'processed_at',
    )

    # WRITE
    df_silver.write.mode('overwrite').parquet('silver/meters/')
    print('Bronze-to-Silver pipeline complete.')

finally:
    spark.stop()
How the Techniques Compose

The pipeline is 50 lines of transformation code, but it executes in a single optimized pass. Catalyst sees the entire chain before any data moves: it pushes the relevant column reads into the CSV parser, fuses the filter conditions, and writes the output in one sweep. Each withColumn() created a new immutable DataFrame in the plan. Each technique from this module contributed one step. And the final select() defined the exact schema that every downstream consumer depends on.

blocks.title: Module 5 complete — you can transform data
blocks.content: You now have the full column transformation toolkit: withColumn() for adding and replacing columns, cast() for type conversion, string functions for text cleaning, date functions for temporal operations, arithmetic for numeric computations, when().otherwise() for conditional logic, boolean flags for reusable data quality rules, and expr() for SQL-style expressions. Combined with Module 4's selection and filtering, you can build the complete read-transform-write pipeline that forms the core of every NordGrid data engineering workflow. In Module 6, you will learn aggregations — the operations that summarize data across groups.

chapters.title: Aggregations and Grouping
