chapters.description: Understand how Spark computes summaries across distributed data, master groupBy and agg patterns, and learn how aggregation triggers the shuffle that moves data between executors.

blocks.content: Modules 4 and 5 taught you to shape individual rows and columns: filtering, selecting, adding, transforming. This module introduces a fundamentally different operation: aggregation. Aggregation takes many rows and produces a summary — a count, a sum, an average, a minimum, or a maximum. Instead of working row by row, aggregation works across rows to answer questions like 'How much total energy did the NORTH region consume in March?' or 'What is the average daily reading across all 500,000 meters?' Every dashboard, billing report, and regulatory submission at NordGrid depends on aggregations computed from the Silver-layer meter readings.
Many Rows Become Few Rows

The defining characteristic of aggregation is that it reduces the number of rows. A DataFrame with 500,000 meter readings becomes a DataFrame with 4 rows when you aggregate total consumption by region. It becomes 1 row when you compute the overall average. It becomes 48 rows when you aggregate by region and month (4 regions × 12 months). This reduction is what makes aggregation powerful: it compresses millions of individual data points into the compact summaries that business users need to make decisions.

blocks.title: Aggregation reduces rows
blocks.caption: Aggregation compresses many rows into a summary. The 500,000 individual meter readings become 4 regional totals. The detail is gone, but the insight is clear.

blocks.content: Where Aggregation Sits in the Pipeline

In NordGrid's medallion architecture, aggregation is primarily a Gold-layer operation. The Silver layer holds clean, row-level data: one row per meter per day. The Gold layer holds aggregated summaries: total consumption by region, average daily reading by month, meter counts by customer type. The Gold pipeline reads the Silver data, groups it by the relevant dimensions, computes the summary metrics, and writes the result as a compact Gold table. Dashboards and reports read from Gold because the pre-computed aggregates are fast to query — the expensive summarization already happened in the pipeline.

rows: Preserve raw data exactly as received
One row per meter per day
One row per group per period
4 rows per day (one per region), or 48 rows per year (region × month)

blocks.title: Aggregation is the bridge from data to decisions
blocks.content: Raw meter readings are data. Regional consumption totals are information. The difference is aggregation. A business user cannot act on 500,000 individual readings, but they can act on 'NORTH region consumed 12% more than SOUTH this month.' Every Gold-layer table at NordGrid exists because someone asked a question that required summarizing millions of rows into a handful of numbers.

sections.title: Simple Aggregations — count, sum, avg, min, max

blocks.content: The simplest aggregations compute a single summary value from an entire DataFrame without any grouping. How many meter readings are there in total? What is the sum of all daily_kw values? What is the average? The minimum? The maximum? These whole-DataFrame aggregations are your first encounter with the aggregation functions, and they establish the syntax pattern that groupBy builds on. PySpark provides these as both DataFrame methods (df.count()) and column functions (F.count(), F.sum(), F.avg()) that you pass to the agg() method.
DataFrame-Level Aggregation Methods

The count() method is the simplest aggregation — it returns the total number of rows as a Python integer. Unlike most DataFrame methods, count() is an action: it triggers execution of the entire plan and returns a value to the driver. The describe() method, which you learned in Module 3, is also an aggregation — it computes count, mean, stddev, min, and max for every numeric column.

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# count() is an action — returns a Python integer
total_rows = df.count()
print(f"Total readings: {total_rows:,}")

# describe() is an action — returns a summary DataFrame
df.describe('daily_kw').show()
Column Aggregation Functions

For more control, you use the aggregation functions from pyspark.sql.functions: count(), sum(), avg() (or mean()), min(), and max(). These are Column functions — they take a column name and return a Column expression. You pass them to the agg() method on a DataFrame or a GroupedData object. When used without groupBy, they aggregate the entire DataFrame into a single row.

from pyspark.sql.functions import count, sum as spark_sum, avg, min as spark_min, max as spark_max

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# Aggregate the entire DataFrame into one row
df.agg(
    count('meter_id').alias('total_readings'),
    spark_sum('daily_kw').alias('total_kw'),
    avg('daily_kw').alias('avg_kw'),
    spark_min('daily_kw').alias('min_kw'),
    spark_max('daily_kw').alias('max_kw'),
).show()

blocks.title: Import aliases for sum, min, max
blocks.content: Python has built-in functions called sum, min, and max. PySpark also has functions with the same names. To avoid shadowing the Python built-ins, import PySpark's versions with aliases: from pyspark.sql.functions import sum as spark_sum, min as spark_min, max as spark_max. NordGrid's import convention uses the spark_ prefix for these three functions. If you forget the alias, calling sum() in filter or column logic may invoke Python's built-in instead of Spark's, producing confusing errors.

Whole-DataFrame aggregation gives you a single summary for the entire dataset. But most business questions require summaries per group: total consumption per region, average reading per month, meter count per customer type. The groupBy() method divides a DataFrame into groups based on column values, and the subsequent aggregation computes a separate result for each group. This is the PySpark equivalent of SQL's GROUP BY clause and is the most important aggregation pattern in data engineering. Every Gold-layer table at NordGrid is produced by a groupBy followed by one or more aggregation functions.
Single-Column Grouping

from pyspark.sql.functions import count, sum as spark_sum, avg

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# Group by region, compute total consumption per region
df_by_region = (df
    .groupBy('region')
    .agg(spark_sum('daily_kw').alias('total_kw'))
)

df_by_region.show()

The result has one row per unique value in the groupBy column. Since NordGrid has four regions, the output has four rows. Each row contains the region name and the aggregated value for that group. The 500,000 individual readings have been compressed into four summary numbers. This is the fundamental groupBy pattern: specify the grouping column, then specify the aggregation function.
Multi-Column Grouping

You can group by multiple columns to create finer-grained summaries. Passing two columns to groupBy() creates groups for each unique combination of those columns. NordGrid's monthly regional report groups by both region and month to produce one row per region per month — 48 rows for a full year (4 regions × 12 months). Each row contains the consumption total, average, and meter count for that specific region-month combination.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, month, count, sum as spark_sum, avg

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# Add month column for grouping
df = df.withColumn('month', month(col('date')))

# Group by region AND month
df_monthly = (df
    .groupBy('region', 'month')
    .agg(
        spark_sum('daily_kw').alias('total_kw'),
        avg('daily_kw').alias('avg_kw'),
        count('meter_id').alias('reading_count'),
    )
    .orderBy('region', 'month')
)

df_monthly.show(8)

blocks.title: groupBy() is a transformation, not an action
blocks.content: As you learned in Module 3, lesson 5, groupBy().agg() is a transformation — it builds a plan but does not execute anything. The aggregation only runs when you call an action like show(), count(), or write(). This means you can chain additional transformations after the aggregation (like filter() to keep only groups that meet a threshold) without triggering intermediate execution.

In the previous lesson, you passed one or more aggregation functions to agg() inside a groupBy chain. This lesson focuses on the agg() method itself: its syntax for computing multiple aggregations simultaneously, the alias() pattern for naming output columns, and the NordGrid reporting template that produces all five standard metrics in one operation. Computing multiple aggregations in a single agg() call is more efficient than computing them separately because Spark processes the data in one pass instead of multiple passes.
The agg() Method

The agg() method accepts one or more Column expressions, each of which is an aggregation function applied to a column. Every expression should include an alias() to give the output column a meaningful name that downstream code can reference cleanly. Without an alias, Spark auto-generates names like sum(daily_kw), which contain parentheses and are awkward to use in subsequent filter(), select(), or join() calls that reference the column by name.

from pyspark.sql.functions import count, sum as spark_sum, avg, min as spark_min, max as spark_max, round as spark_round

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# NordGrid standard report: all five metrics per region
df_report = (df
    .groupBy('region')
    .agg(
        count('meter_id').alias('reading_count'),
        spark_round(spark_sum('daily_kw'), 0).alias('total_kw'),
        spark_round(avg('daily_kw'), 1).alias('avg_kw'),
        spark_round(spark_min('daily_kw'), 1).alias('min_kw'),
        spark_round(spark_max('daily_kw'), 1).alias('max_kw'),
    )
)

df_report.show()
Aggregation Without groupBy

You can call agg() directly on a DataFrame without groupBy() to compute whole-DataFrame aggregations that collapse the entire dataset into a single row. This produces a one-row result with one column per aggregation function. NordGrid uses this pattern for pipeline validation: after writing the Silver table, the pipeline computes total row count, distinct meter count, and total consumption to compare against expected values, catching data loss or duplication errors early.

from pyspark.sql.functions import count, sum as spark_sum, countDistinct

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# Whole-DataFrame aggregation for pipeline validation
validation = df.agg(
    count('*').alias('total_rows'),
    countDistinct('meter_id').alias('unique_meters'),
    countDistinct('date').alias('unique_dates'),
    spark_sum('daily_kw').alias('total_kw'),
)

validation.show()

blocks.title: Always alias your aggregations
blocks.content: Without alias(), an aggregation like spark_sum('daily_kw') produces a column named sum(daily_kw). This name contains parentheses, which makes it awkward to reference in downstream code: col('sum(daily_kw)') is valid but ugly and error-prone. Adding .alias('total_kw') gives the column a clean, readable name that downstream transformations can reference naturally. NordGrid's coding standard requires an alias on every aggregation expression.

After grouping and aggregating, you often need to keep only the groups that meet a certain condition. NordGrid's operations team wants to see only regions where average daily consumption exceeds 1,300 kW — a threshold that indicates higher-than-expected demand. In SQL, this is done with the HAVING clause. In PySpark, there is no separate HAVING method: you simply chain a filter() call after the groupBy().agg() chain. Because groupBy().agg() produces a regular DataFrame, all the filtering techniques from Module 4 apply directly to the aggregated results.
Filtering After Aggregation

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, avg, count, sum as spark_sum

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# Group, aggregate, then filter groups
high_consumption_regions = (df
    .groupBy('region')
    .agg(
        avg('daily_kw').alias('avg_kw'),
        count('meter_id').alias('reading_count'),
        spark_sum('daily_kw').alias('total_kw'),
    )
    .filter(col('avg_kw') > 1300)   # SQL HAVING equivalent
)

high_consumption_regions.show()
Filtering Before vs After Aggregation

There is an important distinction between filtering before and after aggregation. Filtering before groupBy() reduces the number of rows that enter the aggregation — this is the WHERE clause in SQL. Filtering after agg() reduces the number of groups in the result — this is the HAVING clause. Both are filter() calls in PySpark, but their placement in the chain determines which behavior you get. NordGrid's pipeline often uses both: a pre-aggregation filter to keep only valid readings, and a post-aggregation filter to keep only groups that meet business thresholds.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, avg, count, sum as spark_sum

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

result = (df
    # WHERE: filter rows BEFORE aggregation (reduces input data)
    .filter(col('is_valid'))                     # only valid readings
    .filter(col('daily_kw') > 100)               # exclude very small readings

    # GROUP BY + aggregation
    .groupBy('region')
    .agg(
        avg('daily_kw').alias('avg_kw'),
        count('meter_id').alias('reading_count'),
    )

    # HAVING: filter groups AFTER aggregation (reduces output groups)
    .filter(col('avg_kw') > 1300)                # only high-consumption regions
    .filter(col('reading_count') > 10000)         # only statistically significant groups
)

result.show()

left.label: Filter Before groupBy (WHERE)
points: Reduces the rows entering the aggregation
Operates on individual row values
Improves performance by processing fewer rows
Example: filter(col('daily_kw') > 0)

right.label: Filter After agg (HAVING)
points: Reduces the groups in the aggregated result
Operates on aggregated values (avg, sum, count)
Does not affect aggregation performance
Example: filter(col('avg_kw') > 1300)

blocks.title: Pre-filter to reduce aggregation cost
blocks.content: Always filter out unwanted rows before the groupBy, not after. If you aggregate 500,000 rows and then discard groups, you wasted compute on groups you did not need. If you filter to 300,000 rows first, the aggregation processes 40% less data. Catalyst does some of this optimization automatically (like pushing simple filters before shuffles), but explicit pre-filtering makes your intent clear and ensures the optimization happens regardless of plan complexity.

A standard groupBy produces a long-format result: one row per group, with the group key in a column and the metric in another column. Pivot tables transform this into wide format: the group key's values become column headers, and the metric values fill the cells. NordGrid's Power BI dashboards expect a region-by-month matrix where each row is a month and each column is a region's total consumption. This wide format is natural for spreadsheets and dashboards but requires an explicit pivot operation in PySpark. The pivot() method sits between groupBy() and agg() and transforms one column's values into output columns.
The groupBy().pivot().agg() Pattern

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, month, sum as spark_sum, round as spark_round

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')
df = df.withColumn('month', month(col('date')))

# Pivot: region values become column headers
df_pivot = (df
    .groupBy('month')
    .pivot('region')                  # values of 'region' become columns
    .agg(spark_round(spark_sum('daily_kw'), 0))
    .orderBy('month')
)

df_pivot.show()
Specifying Pivot Values

By default, pivot() scans the data to discover all unique values in the pivot column, which requires an extra pass over the data. If you know the values in advance — and you usually do — you can pass them explicitly to avoid the extra scan. NordGrid always specifies the four region values because they are known and stable. This is both a performance optimization and a safety measure: if an unexpected region value appears in the data, it is excluded from the pivot rather than silently creating a new column.

# Specify pivot values explicitly — faster and safer
df_pivot = (df
    .groupBy('month')
    .pivot('region', ['NORTH', 'SOUTH', 'EAST', 'WEST'])  # explicit values
    .agg(spark_round(spark_sum('daily_kw'), 0))
    .orderBy('month')
)
When to Use Pivot

Pivot is useful when the output is consumed by a system that expects wide format: spreadsheets, dashboard tools, and some reporting frameworks. It is not useful when the output feeds another PySpark pipeline, because long format is more natural for further groupBy, join, and filter operations. NordGrid uses pivot exclusively at the Gold layer boundary, when the next consumer is Power BI or a regulatory report template. All intermediate pipeline stages use long format.

blocks.title: Pivot columns must have low cardinality
blocks.content: If the pivot column has 10,000 unique values, pivot creates 10,000 output columns. This produces a DataFrame that is extremely wide, difficult to work with, and expensive to compute. Only pivot on columns with a small number of distinct values: region (4 values), month (12 values), status (3–5 values). If a column has high cardinality, keep it as a groupBy column in long format instead of pivoting it.

Every aggregation lesson so far focused on what aggregation computes. This lesson focuses on how Spark physically executes it across a cluster. When you write groupBy('region').agg(sum('daily_kw')), Spark must bring all NORTH rows to one executor, all SOUTH rows to another, and so on — because you cannot compute a regional total unless all that region's rows are in the same place. This physical movement of data between executors is called a shuffle. It is the most expensive operation in Spark, and aggregation is one of the primary operations that triggers it.
Why Aggregation Requires Shuffle

Before the groupBy, meter readings are distributed across partitions without regard to region. Partition 0 might contain a mix of NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, and WEST readings. Partition 1 has a similar mix. To compute sum(daily_kw) for NORTH, Spark needs all NORTH readings on the same executor. The shuffle rearranges the data: it reads every partition, groups the rows by the region key, and sends each group to a specific executor. After the shuffle, all NORTH rows are in partition 0, all SOUTH rows are in partition 1, and so on. Now the aggregation can compute each group's total independently.

blocks.title: The shuffle during groupBy aggregation
blocks.content:
Before Shuffle                          After Shuffle
(mixed regions per partition)            (one region per partition)

┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐          ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐
│ Part 0  │  │ Part 1  │          │ Part 0  │  │ Part 1  │
│ N,S,E,W │  │ N,S,E,W │   ─►     │ N only  │  │ S only  │
└─────────┘  └─────────┘  shuffle  └─────────┘  └─────────┘
┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐          ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐
│ Part 2  │  │ Part 3  │          │ Part 2  │  │ Part 3  │
│ N,S,E,W │  │ N,S,E,W │          │ E only  │  │ W only  │
└─────────┘  └─────────┘          └─────────┘  └─────────┘

Data moves over the network.          Now each partition can compute
Every row is sent to the              its group total independently.
executor responsible for its key.
blocks.caption: The shuffle physically moves data between executors so that all rows with the same groupBy key end up on the same executor. This network transfer is expensive.

blocks.content: Narrow vs Wide Transformations

Spark classifies every transformation as either narrow or wide. Narrow transformations — filter, select, withColumn — process each partition independently. No data moves between executors. Wide transformations — groupBy, join, distinct, orderBy — require data to move between executors via shuffle. Wide transformations create stage boundaries in the Spark UI: every shuffle starts a new stage. In the NordGrid pipeline, the filter and withColumn steps are narrow (one stage), and the groupBy triggers a shuffle that starts a second stage.

rows: None — each partition is processed independently
Shuffle — data moves between executors over the network

blocks.content: The Cost of Shuffle

Shuffle is expensive because it involves three costly steps. First, each executor serializes its rows by the groupBy key and writes them to local disk (the shuffle write). Second, the data is transferred over the network to the destination executors. Third, the destination executors read the incoming data from the network and deserialize it (the shuffle read). For NordGrid's 500,000 daily readings grouped by region, the shuffle moves approximately 120MB of data across the network. For the full three-year archive of 500GB, the shuffle can move tens of gigabytes. The Spark UI's Stages tab shows shuffle read and shuffle write metrics that let you see exactly how much data moved.

blocks.title: Shuffle is not bad — it is necessary
blocks.content: Shuffle is the mechanism that makes groupBy, join, and distinct possible on distributed data. Without shuffle, you could never aggregate rows from different partitions by the same key. The goal is not to eliminate shuffle but to minimize the amount of data that shuffles. Pre-filter rows you do not need, prune columns you do not need, and the shuffle moves less data. You will learn more advanced shuffle optimization techniques in the mid-level track.

Counting unique values is a fundamental operation in data engineering: how many unique meters reported readings today, how many distinct dates are in the archive, how many different customer types exist. PySpark provides three approaches with different trade-offs: distinct() returns all unique rows, countDistinct() returns an exact count of unique values, and approx_count_distinct() returns a fast approximate count. Understanding these three options and when to use each is important because exact distinct counting is surprisingly expensive at scale — it requires a shuffle to compare every row against every other row with the same value.
countDistinct() — Exact Unique Count

The countDistinct() function counts the number of unique non-null values in a column. It is used inside agg() and returns a single integer. NordGrid uses countDistinct() in its daily validation step to verify that all 500,000 meters reported at least one reading. If the distinct meter count is lower than expected, it signals a communication failure in one or more meter groups.

from pyspark.sql.functions import countDistinct, count

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# Exact distinct counts per region
df.groupBy('region').agg(
    count('meter_id').alias('total_readings'),
    countDistinct('meter_id').alias('unique_meters'),
    countDistinct('date').alias('unique_dates'),
).show()
approx_count_distinct() — Fast Approximate Count

Exact distinct counting requires a shuffle to collect all values of a column on one executor for comparison. For columns with millions of unique values, this is expensive. The approx_count_distinct() function uses the HyperLogLog algorithm to estimate the distinct count without an exact shuffle. The result is approximate — typically within 2–3% of the true value — but the computation is significantly faster and cheaper. NordGrid uses the approximate count for exploratory queries where the exact number is not critical, such as estimating how many unique meters exist across the entire three-year archive.

from pyspark.sql.functions import countDistinct, approx_count_distinct

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# Compare exact vs approximate counts
df.agg(
    countDistinct('meter_id').alias('exact_count'),
    approx_count_distinct('meter_id').alias('approx_count'),
    approx_count_distinct('meter_id', 0.01).alias('approx_1pct'),  # 1% accuracy
).show()
When to Use Each Approach

rows: Validation, billing, regulatory reports — where the exact number matters
Exploration, dashboards, monitoring — where an estimate is sufficient
When you need a specific accuracy guarantee (rsd = relative standard deviation)

blocks.title: Exact vs approximate is a cost decision
blocks.content: The choice between countDistinct() and approx_count_distinct() is not about correctness — both are valid. It is about cost. If the exact number is required for compliance or billing, use countDistinct() and accept the shuffle cost. If an estimate within 2% is sufficient for a dashboard or monitoring alert, use approx_count_distinct() and save the compute time. NordGrid uses exact counts for regulatory reports and approximate counts for the operations dashboard.

This lesson walks through three complete NordGrid aggregation pipelines that produce Gold-layer tables. Each pipeline reads from the Silver layer, applies the techniques from this module, and writes a summary table consumed by a specific business system. The first pipeline produces daily regional summaries for the operations dashboard. The second produces monthly billing aggregates. The third produces the quarterly regulatory report. Each demonstrates a different combination of groupBy, agg, filter, and pivot.
Pipeline 1: Daily Regional Summary

The operations dashboard refreshes every morning and shows yesterday's total consumption, average reading, and meter count per region. This is a simple groupBy on region with three aggregation functions, but it demonstrates the importance of pre-filtering: the pipeline filters to yesterday's date before aggregating, keeping the shuffle small because only one day's data enters the groupBy instead of the full Silver archive.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, count, sum as spark_sum, avg, round as spark_round, lit
from datetime import date, timedelta

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')
yesterday = date.today() - timedelta(days=1)

# Gold: daily regional summary for operations dashboard
df_daily = (df
    .filter(col('date') == lit(yesterday))         # pre-filter to one day
    .filter(col('is_valid'))                       # only valid readings
    .groupBy('region')
    .agg(
        count('meter_id').alias('reading_count'),
        spark_round(spark_sum('daily_kw'), 0).alias('total_kw'),
        spark_round(avg('daily_kw'), 1).alias('avg_kw'),
    )
    .orderBy('region')
)

df_daily.write.mode('overwrite').parquet('gold/daily_regional_summary/')
Pipeline 2: Monthly Billing Aggregates

The billing system needs total consumption per meter per month. This requires grouping by meter_id and month, which produces one row per meter per month. With 500,000 meters and 12 months, the result has up to 6 million rows — still far smaller than the 182 million rows of daily readings. The pipeline adds the tiered pricing calculation from Module 5, lesson 7 after the aggregation.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, month, sum as spark_sum, round as spark_round, when

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# Gold: monthly billing aggregates per meter
df_billing = (df
    .filter(col('is_valid'))
    .withColumn('month', month(col('date')))
    .groupBy('meter_id', 'region', 'month')
    .agg(spark_round(spark_sum('daily_kw'), 2).alias('monthly_kw'))
    .withColumn('monthly_cost',
        when(col('monthly_kw') < 15000, col('monthly_kw') * 0.08)
        .when(col('monthly_kw') < 60000, col('monthly_kw') * 0.12)
        .otherwise(col('monthly_kw') * 0.18)
    )
    .select('meter_id', 'region', 'month', 'monthly_kw', 'monthly_cost')
)

df_billing.write.mode('overwrite').parquet('gold/monthly_billing/')
Pipeline 3: Quarterly Regulatory Report

The energy regulator requires a quarterly matrix showing total consumption per region per month in wide format. This uses the pivot pattern from lesson 6: groupBy month, pivot on region, aggregate with sum. The pipeline filters to the target quarter, pivots the four regions into columns, and writes the result as a small table that the compliance team exports to the regulator's template.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, month, quarter, sum as spark_sum, round as spark_round

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')
target_quarter = 1  # Q1

# Gold: quarterly regulatory report (pivoted by region)
df_regulatory = (df
    .filter(col('is_valid'))
    .withColumn('month', month(col('date')))
    .withColumn('quarter', quarter(col('date')))
    .filter(col('quarter') == target_quarter)
    .groupBy('month')
    .pivot('region', ['NORTH', 'SOUTH', 'EAST', 'WEST'])
    .agg(spark_round(spark_sum('daily_kw'), 0))
    .orderBy('month')
)

df_regulatory.write.mode('overwrite').parquet('gold/quarterly_regulatory/')

blocks.title: Three patterns, one toolkit
blocks.content: All three pipelines use the same building blocks: filter() for pre-aggregation row reduction, groupBy() for defining groups, agg() for computing metrics, pivot() for reshaping, and when() for post-aggregation derived columns. The differences are in which columns they group by, which metrics they compute, and which output format they produce. Mastering these building blocks means you can construct any Gold-layer aggregation table NordGrid needs.

Aggregation is conceptually straightforward but operationally treacherous. NordGrid's data engineering team has debugged more production issues caused by aggregation mistakes than by any other category of PySpark error. The mistakes are subtle: the pipeline runs without errors, produces output, and the output looks reasonable — but the numbers are wrong. This lesson covers the three most common aggregation mistakes, how to detect them, and the defensive patterns NordGrid uses to prevent them.
Mistake 1: Aggregating Before Filtering

The most expensive aggregation mistake is processing rows you do not need. If your Gold table needs only valid readings from Q1, but you aggregate the entire three-year Silver table and then filter to Q1 valid readings, Spark shuffles and processes 20x more data than necessary. The fix is simple: filter as early as possible, before the groupBy. This reduces the number of rows entering the shuffle and can turn a 10-minute pipeline into a 30-second pipeline.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, quarter, sum as spark_sum

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# WRONG: aggregate everything, then filter (shuffles 3 years of data)
df_slow = (df
    .groupBy('region', 'quarter')
    .agg(spark_sum('daily_kw').alias('total_kw'))
    .filter(col('quarter') == 1)                   # too late — already shuffled
)

# RIGHT: filter first, then aggregate (shuffles only Q1 data)
df_fast = (df
    .filter(col('is_valid'))                        # eliminate invalid rows
    .filter(quarter(col('date')) == 1)              # eliminate non-Q1 rows
    .groupBy('region')
    .agg(spark_sum('daily_kw').alias('total_kw'))
)
Mistake 2: Nulls in Aggregation Columns

Aggregation functions handle nulls differently than you might expect. count('*') counts all rows including those with nulls. count('daily_kw') counts only non-null values. sum('daily_kw') ignores nulls and sums the non-null values. avg('daily_kw') computes the average of non-null values, which means the denominator is smaller than the total row count. If your Silver data has null readings and you compute avg('daily_kw'), the result is the average of valid readings, not the average across all meters. This is usually what you want, but if you expected the average to include a zero for missing meters, you need to replace nulls with zero before aggregating.

from pyspark.sql.functions import count, avg, sum as spark_sum

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# Demonstration: null behavior in aggregations
df.agg(
    count('*').alias('count_all'),                # counts ALL rows (including nulls)
    count('daily_kw').alias('count_non_null'),     # counts only non-null values
    avg('daily_kw').alias('avg_non_null'),          # average of non-null values only
    spark_sum('daily_kw').alias('sum_non_null'),    # sum of non-null values
).show()
Mistake 3: Accidental Cartesian in groupBy

Including a high-cardinality column in groupBy() that you did not intend can produce a result with far more groups than expected. If you accidentally include meter_id in a groupBy meant to aggregate by region, you get 500,000 groups (one per meter) instead of 4 groups (one per region). The pipeline does not fail — it just produces a 500,000-row Gold table instead of a 4-row one. NordGrid prevents this by always verifying the output row count against expectations: if a regional summary has more than 4 rows, something is wrong.

from pyspark.sql.functions import sum as spark_sum

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# WRONG: meter_id accidentally included — produces 500,000 groups
df_wrong = df.groupBy('region', 'meter_id').agg(spark_sum('daily_kw').alias('total_kw'))
print(f"Wrong: {df_wrong.count()} groups")  # 500,000 — not what we wanted

# RIGHT: only the intended grouping column
df_right = df.groupBy('region').agg(spark_sum('daily_kw').alias('total_kw'))
print(f"Right: {df_right.count()} groups")  # 4 — one per region

Wrong: 500,000 groups
Right: 4 groups

blocks.title: Module 6 complete — you can summarize data
blocks.content: You now understand aggregation as the operation that compresses many rows into summaries, the groupBy().agg() pattern for per-group metrics, pivot for reshaping into wide format, and the shuffle as the physical cost of aggregation. You know how to filter before and after aggregation, when to use exact vs approximate counts, and the three most common mistakes to avoid. In Module 7, you will learn joins — the operations that combine data from multiple DataFrames.

chapters.title: Joins — Combining DataFrames
chapters.description: Understand every join type Spark supports, learn to choose the right join for each data engineering scenario, and handle the column ambiguity problem that joins create.

sections.title: Why Joins Matter in Data Engineering

blocks.content: Every module until now has worked with a single DataFrame at a time: reading it, filtering it, transforming it, aggregating it. But real-world data is rarely stored in one table. NordGrid's meter readings live in one table. The metadata about each meter — installation date, customer type, substation assignment — lives in another. The substation reference data — name, location, capacity — lives in a third. When the billing pipeline needs to compute charges per customer type per region, it must combine data from all three tables. The operation that combines rows from two DataFrames based on a shared key is called a join. Joins are the mechanism that reassembles data that was split across tables, and they are one of the most important operations in data engineering.
Why Data Is Split Across Tables

Data is stored in separate tables for good reasons. Meter readings change every day — 500,000 new rows arrive daily. Meter metadata changes rarely — installation dates and customer types are updated a few times per year. Storing them together would mean rewriting the metadata for every meter every day, wasting storage and creating data quality risks. By keeping readings in one table and metadata in another, each table changes at its natural frequency. But when a pipeline needs both readings and metadata together, it must join them. This separation-and-recombination pattern is fundamental to data engineering and appears in every NordGrid pipeline that enriches raw readings with context from reference tables.
NordGrid’s Tables

NordGrid's Silver layer contains four core tables that the join examples in this module use. The readings table has one row per meter per day with columns meter_id, daily_kw, date, and region. The meters table is a reference table with columns meter_id, customer_type, install_date, and substation_id. The substations table has columns substation_id, substation_name, and capacity_mw. The weather table has columns station_id, date, and temp_c. Each table connects to the others through shared key columns: meter_id links readings to meters, substation_id links meters to substations.

blocks.title: NordGrid’s table relationships
blocks.content:
readings                 meters                  substations
┌────────────┐          ┌───────────────┐          ┌───────────────┐
│ meter_id   │────────│ meter_id      │          │ substation_id │
│ daily_kw   │          │ customer_type │          │ substation_name│
│ date       │          │ install_date  │          │ capacity_mw   │
│ region     │          │ substation_id │────────│               │
└────────────┘          └───────────────┘          └───────────────┘
500K rows/day             500K rows (static)        200 rows (static)

    readings.meter_id = meters.meter_id
    meters.substation_id = substations.substation_id
blocks.caption: Three tables connected by shared key columns. Joins follow the arrows: readings join to meters on meter_id, then meters join to substations on substation_id.

blocks.content: The Join Operation

A join takes two DataFrames and a condition that defines how their rows relate. For each row in the left DataFrame, Spark finds the matching rows in the right DataFrame based on the condition, and combines them into a single wider row. The most common condition is equality on a shared key column: readings.meter_id == meters.meter_id. When a reading's meter_id matches a meter's meter_id, the columns from both tables are combined into one row. The result DataFrame has columns from both sides, enriching the readings with the metadata that was stored separately.

# The basic join pattern: left_df.join(right_df, condition, join_type)
df_readings = spark.read.parquet('silver/readings/')
df_meters = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters_ref/')

# Join readings with meter metadata on meter_id
df_enriched = df_readings.join(
    df_meters,
    on='meter_id',        # join key — column name shared by both DataFrames
    how='inner'           # join type — keep only matching rows
)

print(f"Readings columns: {df_readings.columns}")
print(f"Meters columns:   {df_meters.columns}")
print(f"Enriched columns: {df_enriched.columns}")

Readings columns: ['meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'date', 'region']
Meters columns:   ['meter_id', 'customer_type', 'install_date', 'substation_id']
Enriched columns: ['meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'date', 'region', 'customer_type', 'install_date', 'substation_id']

blocks.title: Joins are the most complex operation in PySpark
blocks.content: Joins are more complex than filtering, transforming, or aggregating because they involve two DataFrames, a matching condition, a join type that controls which non-matching rows survive, and a column ambiguity problem when both sides share column names. This module dedicates 10 lessons to joins because mastering them thoroughly prevents the subtle bugs — silently lost rows, duplicated columns, inflated counts — that plague data engineering pipelines.

An inner join keeps only the rows where the join key matches in both DataFrames. If a reading's meter_id has a corresponding row in the meters reference table, the reading is included in the result with the metadata columns attached. If a reading's meter_id has no match — perhaps the meter was recently installed and the reference table has not been updated — the reading is silently excluded from the result. This silent exclusion is both the defining characteristic and the primary danger of inner joins: rows disappear without errors, warnings, or log messages.
How Inner Joins Work

blocks.title: Inner join: only matching rows survive
blocks.caption: An inner join keeps only rows with matching keys in both DataFrames. M-003 (no metadata) and M-004 (no reading) both disappear from the result.

blocks.content: Inner Join in Code

df_readings = spark.read.parquet('silver/readings/')
df_meters = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters_ref/')

# Inner join: keep only readings that have metadata
df_inner = df_readings.join(df_meters, on='meter_id', how='inner')

print(f"Readings:  {df_readings.count():,} rows")
print(f"Meters:    {df_meters.count():,} rows")
print(f"Inner join: {df_inner.count():,} rows")

df_inner.show(3)
The Silent Data Loss Problem

The inner join result has 498,200 rows — 1,650 fewer than the readings table. Those 1,650 readings had meter IDs that did not appear in the meters reference table, so they were dropped. This is correct behavior if you only want enriched readings. But if you expected all readings to have metadata, the drop is a silent data quality issue. NordGrid's pipeline monitors for this: after every inner join, the pipeline compares the input and output row counts. If more than 0.1% of rows were lost, it raises an alert. If you need to detect which specific rows were lost, you use an anti join — which you will learn in lesson 6.

blocks.title: Inner joins silently lose rows
blocks.content: Every inner join can drop rows from both sides. If you start with 500,000 readings and the inner join produces 498,200, you lost 1,800 readings without any error message. Always compare input and output counts after an inner join. If the count drops unexpectedly, investigate which keys did not match. This is the single most common source of 'where did my data go?' bugs in PySpark pipelines.

An inner join drops rows from both sides when the key does not match. A left join keeps all rows from the left DataFrame regardless of whether they match, and fills in null for the right side's columns when there is no match. This guarantees that the left DataFrame's row count is preserved — no data is silently lost from the primary table. A right join does the same but preserves the right side instead. Left joins are far more common than right joins in practice because you typically want to keep all rows from your primary fact table (readings) and enrich with optional reference data (metadata).
Left Join

blocks.title: Left join: all left rows survive, nulls fill missing right columns
blocks.caption: A left join preserves all rows from the left side. Where the right side has no match, its columns are filled with null.

blocks.content:
df_readings = spark.read.parquet('silver/readings/')
df_meters = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters_ref/')

# Left join: keep ALL readings, enrich with metadata where available
df_left = df_readings.join(df_meters, on='meter_id', how='left')

print(f"Readings:   {df_readings.count():,} rows")
print(f"Left join:  {df_left.count():,} rows")  # same as readings count!

# Readings without metadata have null in the metadata columns
df_left.filter(df_left['customer_type'].isNull()).show(3)
The Data Quality Audit Pattern

NordGrid uses left joins for data quality auditing. By left-joining readings to metadata, the team preserves every reading and can then filter for rows where the metadata columns are null. These null-metadata rows are readings from meters that are missing from the reference table — a data quality issue that needs investigation. An inner join would hide these rows. A left join makes them visible. This is why NordGrid's Silver pipeline uses a left join for enrichment and then splits the result into two paths: rows with metadata go to the main Silver table, and rows without metadata go to a data quality exception table for the ops team to review.
Right Joins

A right join is the mirror of a left join: it keeps all rows from the right DataFrame and fills null for unmatched left columns. In practice, right joins are rare because you can always swap the DataFrame order and use a left join instead. df_a.join(df_b, ..., how='right') is identical to df_b.join(df_a, ..., how='left'). NordGrid's coding standard prefers left joins because the primary table (the one whose rows must be preserved) is always on the left side, making the intent clearer to code reviewers.

blocks.title: Left join preserves your row count
blocks.content: Use a left join when you want to guarantee that no rows from your primary table are lost. The result always has the same number of rows as the left DataFrame (or more, if the right side has duplicate keys). Where the right side has no match, the right columns are null. This null-based signaling is valuable: after the join, you can filter for nulls to find exactly which rows lacked a match, turning a potential data loss into a detectable data quality event.

A full outer join keeps all rows from both DataFrames, filling in null wherever a match does not exist. If a reading has no metadata, the metadata columns are null but the reading is kept. If a meter exists in the reference table but has no reading, the reading columns are null but the meter row is kept. No data from either side is lost. This is the most permissive join type: every row from every table appears in the result. Full outer joins are less common than inner or left joins in production pipelines, but they are the right tool for reconciliation scenarios where you need to compare two datasets and find the differences.
The Reconciliation Pattern

NordGrid uses full outer joins to reconcile meter inventories. Every week, the asset management team exports the official meter inventory. The data engineering team compares it against the meters that actually reported readings. A full outer join on meter_id reveals three categories: meters in both lists (active and reporting), meters in the inventory but not in the readings (installed but not yet reporting or communication failure), and meters in the readings but not in the inventory (potentially decommissioned meters still sending data or data quality issues in the inventory).

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, when, lit

# Two datasets to reconcile
df_inventory = spark.read.parquet('reference/meter_inventory/')   # official list
df_reported = (spark.read.parquet('silver/readings/')
    .select('meter_id').distinct()                                # meters that reported
)

# Full outer join: keep everything from both sides
df_reconciled = df_inventory.join(df_reported, on='meter_id', how='full')

# Categorize the results
df_reconciled = df_reconciled.withColumn('status',
    when(col('install_date').isNotNull() & col('meter_id').isNotNull(), 'active')
    .when(col('install_date').isNotNull(), 'inventory_only')
    .otherwise('readings_only')
)

df_reconciled.groupBy('status').count().show()

The result reveals 1,800 meters in the inventory that did not report readings — possible communication failures that the field maintenance team needs to investigate. It also reveals 1,650 meters that reported readings but are not in the official inventory — possibly decommissioned meters still sending data, or data quality issues in the asset management system. Neither discrepancy would be visible with an inner join, which would silently drop both categories of unmatched rows.

blocks.title: Full outer joins are for finding differences
blocks.content: Use a full outer join when you need to see everything from both sides and identify what does not match. The nulls in the result are the signal: a null on the left side means the right-side row had no match, and a null on the right side means the left-side row had no match. This pattern is ideal for reconciliation, auditing, and change detection between two snapshots of the same dataset.

A cross join combines every row from the left DataFrame with every row from the right DataFrame, producing a Cartesian product. If the left has 10 rows and the right has 5, the result has 50 rows — every possible combination. Cross joins are the rarest join type in production pipelines because the result can be enormous: crossing two 500,000-row tables produces 250 billion rows, which would crash most clusters. But cross joins are the right tool for a small set of genuine use cases where you need every possible combination, and understanding them helps you recognize accidental cross joins in your own code.
The Gap Detection Pattern

NordGrid's meter reading completeness check needs to know which meter-date combinations are missing. If 500,000 meters should each report one reading per day for 31 days in March, there should be 15.5 million readings. If the actual count is 15.3 million, 200,000 readings are missing. To find which specific meter-date combinations are absent, the team generates all possible combinations (500,000 meters × 31 dates = 15.5 million combinations) using a cross join, and then anti-joins against the actual readings to find the gaps.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, explode, sequence, to_date, lit

# Small DataFrames for cross join: unique meters and unique dates
df_meters = spark.read.parquet('silver/readings/').select('meter_id').distinct()
df_dates = spark.createDataFrame(
    [(d,) for d in ['2026-03-01', '2026-03-02', '2026-03-03']],
    schema=['date']
)

print(f"Meters: {df_meters.count()}")
print(f"Dates:  {df_dates.count()}")

# Cross join: every meter × every date
df_expected = df_meters.crossJoin(df_dates)
print(f"Expected combinations: {df_expected.count():,}")

df_expected.show(4)

blocks.title: Cross joins are output multipliers
blocks.content: A cross join multiplies row counts: left_rows × right_rows = result_rows. Crossing a 500,000-row table with a 31-row table produces 15.5 million rows — manageable. Crossing two 500,000-row tables produces 250 billion rows — catastrophic. Only use cross joins when at least one side is small. PySpark requires you to use crossJoin() explicitly (not join with how='cross') to signal that the Cartesian product is intentional, not accidental.
Accidental Cross Joins

The most dangerous cross join is the accidental one. If you join two DataFrames on a condition that is always true (like lit(True) or omitting the condition entirely), Spark produces a Cartesian product instead of a key-based match. This can happen when a join condition has a typo, when you forget to specify the join key, or when the join key has only one unique value (making every row match every other row). NordGrid's code review checklist requires every join to have an explicit key, and the team's CI pipeline flags any use of crossJoin() for manual review.

If you see a join result with far more rows than either input, suspect an accidental cross join. A reading table with 500,000 rows joined to a metadata table with 500,000 rows should produce at most 500,000 rows (if keys are unique). If the result has 250 billion rows, the join condition is wrong. Check the join key, verify that it has unique values on at least one side, and ensure the condition is an equality comparison on the correct columns.

Inner, left, right, and full outer joins all produce a wider DataFrame by combining columns from both sides. Semi joins and anti joins are different: they use the right DataFrame only for matching, and the result contains only the left DataFrame's columns. A semi join keeps left rows that have a match in the right side but does not add any right-side columns. An anti join keeps left rows that have no match in the right side. These two join types are filtering operations that use another DataFrame as the filter criterion, and they are essential tools in NordGrid's data quality and fleet management pipelines.
Semi Join — Keep Rows That Match

A semi join answers the question: 'Which rows in the left DataFrame have a matching key in the right DataFrame?' The result has the same columns as the left DataFrame — no columns from the right side are added. NordGrid uses semi joins to filter meter readings to only those meters in the active fleet. The active fleet list is maintained by the asset management team as a separate table. A semi join keeps readings from active meters and discards readings from decommissioned meters, without adding any fleet columns to the readings.

df_readings = spark.read.parquet('silver/readings/')
df_active_fleet = spark.read.parquet('reference/active_meters/')  # just meter_id

# Semi join: keep readings where meter_id exists in the active fleet
df_active_readings = df_readings.join(df_active_fleet, on='meter_id', how='semi')

print(f"All readings:    {df_readings.count():,} rows")
print(f"Active readings: {df_active_readings.count():,} rows")
print(f"Columns:         {df_active_readings.columns}")  # same as readings — no extra columns

All readings:    499,850 rows
Active readings: 498,200 rows
Columns:         ['meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'date', 'region']
Anti Join — Keep Rows That Do Not Match

An anti join answers the opposite question: 'Which rows in the left DataFrame have no matching key in the right DataFrame?' The result contains left-side rows that are absent from the right side. NordGrid uses anti joins to find meters that stopped reporting: an anti join from the expected meter list against the actual readings reveals which meters did not send data. This is the companion to the semi join and the tool that detects the rows an inner join would silently drop.

# Anti join: find meters that SHOULD have reported but did NOT
df_expected = spark.read.parquet('reference/active_meters/')
df_reported = spark.read.parquet('silver/readings/').select('meter_id').distinct()

# Meters in the expected list but NOT in the reported list
df_missing = df_expected.join(df_reported, on='meter_id', how='anti')

print(f"Expected meters: {df_expected.count():,}")
print(f"Reported meters: {df_reported.count():,}")
print(f"Missing meters:  {df_missing.count():,}")

df_missing.show(3)

Expected meters: 500,000
Reported meters: 498,200
Missing meters:  1,800

+--------+
|meter_id|
+--------+
|   M-AAA|
|   M-BBB|
|   M-CCC|
+--------+

left.label: Semi Join (how='semi')
points: Keeps left rows WITH a match
Result has left-side columns only
Like a filter that uses another table as criteria
Use case: keep readings from active meters

right.label: Anti Join (how='anti')
points: Keeps left rows WITHOUT a match
Result has left-side columns only
Like a NOT IN filter that uses another table
Use case: find meters that stopped reporting

blocks.title: Semi and anti joins are filters, not enrichments
blocks.content: Unlike inner, left, and full joins, semi and anti joins do not add columns from the right DataFrame. They use the right DataFrame purely as a lookup: does this key exist there or not? This makes them the right tool when you want to filter one DataFrame based on the presence or absence of keys in another, without changing the left DataFrame's schema.

When you join two DataFrames that share a column name, the result contains two columns with the same name. If both the readings table and the meters table have a column called region, the joined DataFrame has two region columns — and Spark cannot tell which one you mean when you write col('region'). This is the column ambiguity problem, and it is the most common source of join-related errors in PySpark. Spark raises an AnalysisException with the message 'Reference region is ambiguous' whenever you try to use an ambiguous column in a subsequent transformation. This lesson covers three strategies for resolving ambiguity.
When Ambiguity Occurs

Ambiguity occurs when you join on a Column expression (like df_a['meter_id'] == df_b['meter_id']) rather than on a string (on='meter_id'). When you use the string form, Spark automatically merges the two key columns into one — no ambiguity. When you use the expression form, Spark keeps both key columns in the result. This distinction catches many beginners: the string form is safer for simple equi-joins, but the expression form is required for non-equi joins and multi-condition joins.

df_readings = spark.read.parquet('silver/readings/')     # has 'meter_id', 'region'
df_meters = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters_ref/')     # has 'meter_id', 'region'

# String form: Spark merges the key column — no ambiguity
df_safe = df_readings.join(df_meters, on='meter_id', how='inner')
print(f"Safe columns: {df_safe.columns}")  # one 'meter_id', but TWO 'region' columns!

# Expression form: Spark keeps both key columns — ambiguity!
df_ambiguous = df_readings.join(
    df_meters,
    df_readings['meter_id'] == df_meters['meter_id'],
    how='inner'
)
print(f"Ambiguous columns: {df_ambiguous.columns}")  # two 'meter_id', two 'region'

Safe columns: ['meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'date', 'region', 'customer_type', 'install_date', 'substation_id', 'region']
Ambiguous columns: ['meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'date', 'region', 'meter_id', 'customer_type', 'install_date', 'substation_id', 'region']
Strategy 1: Join on a String or List

The simplest resolution is to join on a string (on='meter_id') or a list of strings (on=['meter_id', 'date']). This tells Spark that the key columns are the same on both sides, and Spark merges them into a single column in the result. However, this only prevents ambiguity for the key columns. If both DataFrames have a non-key column with the same name (like region), that column is still duplicated and ambiguous.
Strategy 2: Alias the DataFrames

You can give each DataFrame an alias using the alias() method and then reference columns with the alias prefix in subsequent transformations. This lets you specify which DataFrame’s region column you mean: col('readings.region') vs col('meters.region'). NordGrid uses this strategy when both DataFrames have overlapping non-key column names and the expression form of the join condition is required for complex matching logic.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col

# Alias both DataFrames
df_r = df_readings.alias('readings')
df_m = df_meters.alias('meters')

# Join using expression form (needed for complex conditions)
df_joined = df_r.join(
    df_m,
    col('readings.meter_id') == col('meters.meter_id'),
    how='inner'
)

# Now you can reference columns unambiguously
df_result = df_joined.select(
    col('readings.meter_id'),
    col('readings.daily_kw'),
    col('readings.region').alias('reading_region'),
    col('meters.customer_type'),
    col('meters.region').alias('meter_region'),
)
Strategy 3: Drop Duplicate Columns After Join

The most pragmatic strategy is to drop the duplicate columns immediately after the join. If both sides have a region column but you only want the left side’s version, drop the right side’s column using drop(df_meters['region']). This approach is simple, explicit, and works well when the duplicated columns contain the same values on both sides, which is typically the case for key columns and for columns that were derived from the same source.

# Join on string to merge the key, then drop the duplicate non-key column
df_joined = df_readings.join(df_meters, on='meter_id', how='inner')

# Drop the meters table's 'region' column (keep readings' version)
df_clean = df_joined.drop(df_meters['region'])

blocks.title: Always handle ambiguity immediately
blocks.content: Resolve column ambiguity in the same line or the next line after the join. If you postpone it, every subsequent transformation on the joined DataFrame risks an ambiguous reference error. NordGrid's pattern: join, then immediately select or drop to produce a clean, unambiguous DataFrame that the rest of the pipeline can use safely.

Every join so far used a simple equality condition on a single column: on='meter_id'. But real-world joins often require more complex conditions: joining on multiple columns simultaneously, joining on a range of values, or joining where the key columns have different names on each side. PySpark supports all of these through join expressions — Column conditions passed as the second argument to join(). This lesson covers multi-column joins, non-equi joins, and the NordGrid patterns that require them.
Multi-Column Joins

When a single column is not sufficient to uniquely identify the relationship between two DataFrames, you join on multiple columns. NordGrid's hourly SCADA data has readings split across multiple tables: one per region per month. To join March NORTH readings from table A with March NORTH aggregates from table B, the join must match on both region AND month. You pass a list of column names to the on parameter: on=['region', 'month'].

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, month

df_readings = spark.read.parquet('silver/readings/')
df_readings = df_readings.withColumn('month', month(col('date')))

df_targets = spark.read.parquet('reference/regional_targets/')  # region, month, target_kw

# Multi-column join: match on both region AND month
df_with_targets = df_readings.join(
    df_targets,
    on=['region', 'month'],   # list of shared key columns
    how='left'
)

df_with_targets.show(3)
Joining on Different Column Names

When the key columns have different names on each side — for example, meter_id in the readings table and device_id in a partner's table — you cannot use the on parameter with a string. You must use a Column expression that explicitly equates the two differently named columns. This requires the expression form of the join condition and the ambiguity handling strategies from the previous lesson.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col

df_readings = spark.read.parquet('silver/readings/')         # has 'meter_id'
df_partner = spark.read.parquet('partner/device_readings/')  # has 'device_id'

# Join on differently named columns using a Column expression
df_combined = df_readings.join(
    df_partner,
    df_readings['meter_id'] == df_partner['device_id'],  # explicit condition
    how='inner'
)

# Drop the duplicate key column
df_combined = df_combined.drop(df_partner['device_id'])
Non-Equi Joins

A non-equi join uses a comparison other than equality — greater than, less than, between — in the join condition. These are rare in data engineering but appear in range-based lookups. NordGrid uses a non-equi join to assign each meter reading to a pricing tier based on the reading's value falling within a range defined in a tier table. The join condition checks that daily_kw is between the tier's lower and upper bounds.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col

df_readings = spark.read.parquet('silver/readings/')

# Pricing tiers with lower and upper bounds
df_tiers = spark.createDataFrame([
    ('low',      0.0,  500.0, 0.08),
    ('medium', 500.0, 2000.0, 0.12),
    ('high',  2000.0, 4000.0, 0.18),
    ('critical', 4000.0, 99999.0, 0.25),
], schema=['tier', 'lower_bound', 'upper_bound', 'rate'])

# Non-equi join: reading falls within the tier's range
df_priced = df_readings.join(
    df_tiers,
    (col('daily_kw') >= col('lower_bound')) & (col('daily_kw') < col('upper_bound')),
    how='inner'
)

df_priced.select('meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'tier', 'rate').show(4)

blocks.title: Match the join condition to the relationship
blocks.content: Simple equi-joins on a single key handle 90% of data engineering join scenarios. Multi-column joins handle cases where the relationship requires a composite key. Non-equi joins handle range-based lookups. Knowing all three patterns gives you the flexibility to express any relationship between two DataFrames. Start with the simplest form that fits, and escalate to expressions only when the relationship requires it.

sections.title: Joins at NordGrid — Meters and Metadata

blocks.content: This lesson walks through a complete NordGrid join pipeline that reads meter readings and three reference tables, joins them in sequence, handles column ambiguity at each step, and produces an enriched Silver table with all the context downstream consumers need. This is the most complex pipeline in the course so far: it chains three joins, each with its own ambiguity resolution and validation step. Every technique from this module appears in the pipeline.
The Three-Table Join Pipeline

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, count

# Read the four tables
df_readings = spark.read.parquet('silver/readings/')          # meter_id, daily_kw, date, region
df_meters = spark.read.parquet('reference/meters/')           # meter_id, customer_type, substation_id
df_substations = spark.read.parquet('reference/substations/') # substation_id, substation_name, capacity_mw
df_weather = spark.read.parquet('reference/weather/')         # region, date, temp_c

print(f"Readings:     {df_readings.count():,} rows")
print(f"Meters:       {df_meters.count():,} rows")
print(f"Substations:  {df_substations.count():,} rows")
print(f"Weather:      {df_weather.count():,} rows")

Readings:     499,850 rows
Meters:       500,000 rows
Substations:  200 rows
Weather:      1,460 rows
Join 1: Readings + Meter Metadata

# Join 1: Left join readings to meter metadata
# Left join preserves all readings, even those without metadata
df = df_readings.join(df_meters, on='meter_id', how='left')

# Validate: no readings were lost
assert df.count() == df_readings.count(), "Join 1 changed the row count!"
print(f"After join 1: {df.count():,} rows, columns: {df.columns}")
Join 2: + Substation Reference

# Join 2: Left join to substations on substation_id
df = df.join(df_substations, on='substation_id', how='left')

# Validate: row count unchanged (left join preserves left side)
assert df.count() == df_readings.count(), "Join 2 changed the row count!"
print(f"After join 2: {df.count():,} rows")
Join 3: + Weather Data

# Join 3: Left join to weather on composite key (region + date)
df = df.join(df_weather, on=['region', 'date'], how='left')

# Validate: row count unchanged
assert df.count() == df_readings.count(), "Join 3 changed the row count!"
print(f"After join 3: {df.count():,} rows")

# Final select: define the enriched Silver schema
df_enriched = df.select(
    'meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'date', 'region',
    'customer_type', 'substation_id', 'substation_name', 'capacity_mw',
    'temp_c',
)

df_enriched.show(3)
df_enriched.write.mode('overwrite').parquet('silver/readings_enriched/')

The pipeline chains three left joins, each preserving the original reading count. The assert statements after each join are NordGrid's defensive pattern: if a join unexpectedly changes the row count (which would indicate a duplicate key in a reference table or an unintended cross join), the pipeline fails immediately with a clear error rather than producing incorrect output. The final select defines the enriched schema contract, and the write produces the Parquet files that Gold pipelines and dashboards consume.

blocks.title: Assert after every join
blocks.content: NordGrid's most important join discipline: validate the row count after every join. A left join should not change the left side's row count unless the right side has duplicate keys (which causes row multiplication). An inner join should drop rows only when expected. Inserting an assert or a count comparison after each join catches bugs at the exact point they occur, rather than discovering wrong numbers in a dashboard days later.

sections.title: How Joins Work Under the Hood

blocks.content: In Module 6, lesson 7 you learned that aggregation triggers a shuffle — data moves between executors so that rows with the same groupBy key end up on the same machine. Joins trigger the same mechanism but for a different reason: rows with the same join key from both DataFrames must land on the same executor so that Spark can compare them. This lesson provides a light introduction to how Spark physically executes joins, introduces the broadcast join optimization for small tables, and establishes the vocabulary you will revisit in the mid-level track where join performance becomes a central concern.
Shuffle Hash Join

The default join strategy is the shuffle hash join (or sort-merge join, depending on the data size). Spark shuffles both DataFrames by the join key, placing all rows with key M-001 from both tables on the same executor. Once matching rows are co-located, the executor performs the actual comparison and combination locally. The cost is proportional to the total size of both DataFrames because every row from both sides must be shuffled across the network.

blocks.title: Shuffle join: both sides are shuffled by the join key
blocks.caption: Both DataFrames are shuffled by the join key so that matching rows from both sides land on the same executor. The comparison then happens locally without further network transfer.

blocks.content: Broadcast Join — The Small-Table Optimization

When one side of the join is small enough to fit in each executor's memory, Spark can avoid shuffling the large side entirely. Instead, it broadcasts the small DataFrame — sends a complete copy to every executor. Each executor then joins its local partition of the large DataFrame against the in-memory copy of the small table. No shuffle of the large side occurs, which eliminates the most expensive part of the operation. Spark automatically broadcasts tables smaller than 10MB by default (configurable via spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold), or you can force it with the broadcast() hint.

from pyspark.sql.functions import broadcast

df_readings = spark.read.parquet('silver/readings/')          # 500K rows, ~120 MB
df_substations = spark.read.parquet('reference/substations/') # 200 rows, ~5 KB

# Spark broadcasts substations automatically (it is tiny)
# You can also force it explicitly:
df_enriched = df_readings.join(
    broadcast(df_substations),     # broadcast hint: send full copy to every executor
    on='substation_id',
    how='left'
)

# The readings side was NOT shuffled — only the tiny substations table was broadcast
# This is much faster than shuffling 120 MB of readings
When to Expect Each Strategy

rows: One side is small (< 10MB default threshold)
Low — no shuffle of the large side
High — both sides shuffled over the network
You use broadcast() hint on a small-ish table
Low — but risks OOM if the table is too large to broadcast

blocks.content: At the junior level, you do not need to choose join strategies manually — Spark's Catalyst optimizer selects the best strategy based on table sizes. But knowing that broadcast joins exist explains why joining a 500,000-row readings table with a 200-row substations table is almost instant, while joining two 500,000-row tables takes noticeably longer. The substations join is broadcast; the readings-to-meters join is shuffled. You will learn to tune join strategies, diagnose join performance, and handle skewed joins in the mid-level track.

blocks.title: Module 7 complete — you can combine data
blocks.content: You now understand every join type: inner (matching rows only), left (preserve the left side), full outer (preserve everything), cross (Cartesian product), semi (filter by match), and anti (filter by non-match). You know how to resolve column ambiguity, join on multiple columns and different column names, and validate row counts after every join. You understand that joins trigger shuffles and that broadcast joins optimize small-table lookups. In Module 8, you will learn to read and write data in different file formats — completing the read-transform-write pipeline pattern.

chapters.title: Reading and Writing Data
chapters.description: Master the DataFrameReader and DataFrameWriter APIs, understand file format characteristics, and build complete read-transform-write pipelines following the NordGrid Bronze-Silver-Gold pattern.

blocks.content: In Module 3, lesson 3 you got a brief introduction to reading files with spark.read. This module gives you the complete picture. Every PySpark pipeline starts by reading data from an external source — a file, a directory of files, or a database — and the DataFrameReader is the object that handles this. It determines how files are parsed, which schema to apply, how to handle malformed records, and how to split the data into partitions. Understanding the reader's full capabilities means you can confidently load any data NordGrid's pipelines encounter, from clean Parquet archives to messy CSV exports with encoding issues.
The Builder Pattern

The DataFrameReader uses the same builder pattern as SparkSession: you chain method calls to configure the reader, then call a final method to produce the DataFrame. The spark.read property returns a DataFrameReader object. From there, you call format() to specify the file type, option() or options() to set format-specific parameters, schema() to provide an explicit schema, and load() to execute the read. Alternatively, convenience methods like csv(), json(), and parquet() combine format and load into a single call, accepting options as keyword arguments.

from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StructField, StringType, DoubleType, DateType

# Generic builder pattern: format() + option() + schema() + load()
df = (spark.read
    .format('csv')
    .option('header', 'true')
    .option('sep', ',')
    .option('nullValue', 'NA')
    .schema(meter_schema)
    .load('bronze/meters/')
)

# Convenience method: csv() combines format + load, options as kwargs
df = spark.read.csv(
    'bronze/meters/',
    header=True,
    sep=',',
    nullValue='NA',
    schema=meter_schema,
)

# Both produce the same DataFrame
Reading Directories

When you pass a directory path instead of a file path, Spark reads all files in the directory that match the expected format. If bronze/meters/ contains 365 CSV files (one per day), spark.read.csv('bronze/meters/') reads all 365 files and combines them into a single DataFrame. This is how NordGrid's pipelines process historical data: the Bronze directory accumulates daily files, and the Silver pipeline reads the entire directory in one call. Spark automatically parallelizes this — different executors read different files simultaneously.

# Read a single file
df_one = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/part-00000.parquet')

# Read all files in a directory
df_all = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')  # reads every .parquet file in the dir

# Read from multiple directories using glob patterns
df_march = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/date=2026-03-*/')  # all March partitions

# Read from an explicit list of paths
df_specific = spark.read.parquet(
    'silver/meters/date=2026-03-01/',
    'silver/meters/date=2026-03-15/',
)

blocks.title: The reader is lazy
blocks.content: Like all DataFrame operations, spark.read does not immediately load data into memory. It creates a DataFrame that references the files but does not scan them. The actual reading happens when an action (show, count, write) triggers execution. This means you can configure a reader, chain transformations, and let Catalyst optimize the entire sequence — including pushing filters and column selections into the file scan itself.

CSV is the most common format for raw data ingestion. NordGrid's meter readings, partner data exports, and regulatory submission templates all arrive as CSV. The format is simple — values separated by a delimiter, one row per line — but its simplicity hides complexity: there is no schema metadata, no standard for null representation, no guarantee of consistent encoding, and no type information. Every CSV read requires you to make decisions about headers, delimiters, null handling, and schema. Getting these decisions wrong produces a DataFrame that looks correct but contains wrong types, missing values, or misaligned columns.
Essential CSV Options

rows: Whether the first row contains column names
true — NordGrid CSVs always have headers
Whether Spark scans data to guess types
Whether values can span multiple lines

blocks.content: Error Handling Modes

The mode option controls what happens when Spark encounters a row that cannot be parsed — perhaps it has too many columns, too few columns, or a value that does not match the schema. PERMISSIVE mode (the default) keeps the row and puts null in fields it could not parse. DROPMALFORMED mode silently drops the row. FAILFAST mode throws an exception and stops the pipeline. NordGrid uses DROPMALFORMED for production loads because malformed rows are rare (typically less than 0.01% of data) and should not crash the pipeline, but the team monitors the row count to detect if the drop rate increases.

from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StructField, StringType, DoubleType, DateType

meter_schema = StructType([
    StructField('meter_id',  StringType(), False),
    StructField('daily_kw',  DoubleType(), True),
    StructField('date',      DateType(),   False),
    StructField('region',    StringType(), False),
])

# Production CSV read: explicit schema, known options, malformed rows dropped
df = spark.read.csv(
    'bronze/meters/',
    header=True,
    schema=meter_schema,
    sep=',',
    nullValue='NA',
    mode='DROPMALFORMED',
    encoding='UTF-8',
)

print(f"Rows loaded: {df.count():,}")
df.printSchema()

Rows loaded: 499,850
root
 |-- meter_id: string (nullable = false)
 |-- daily_kw: double (nullable = true)
 |-- date: date (nullable = false)
 |-- region: string (nullable = false)

blocks.title: Never use inferSchema in production
blocks.content: inferSchema requires Spark to scan the entire file twice: once to guess types, once to read data. On a 500GB archive, this doubles the read time. Worse, inferred types can change if the data changes — a column that was all integers today might contain a decimal tomorrow, silently changing from LongType to DoubleType and breaking downstream pipelines. Always provide an explicit schema for production CSV reads.

JSON is the standard format for web APIs, configuration files, and semi-structured data. NordGrid's weather data arrives as JSON from an external weather service API, and SCADA telemetry events are logged as JSON objects. Unlike CSV, JSON carries its structure within the data: field names are embedded in every record, nested objects and arrays are natively supported, and data types are partially preserved (strings, numbers, booleans, nulls). PySpark's JSON reader handles both single-line JSON (one JSON object per line, the most common format for data processing) and multi-line JSON (a single JSON array spanning the entire file).
Single-Line JSON

The most common JSON format for data engineering is JSON Lines (also called JSONL or newline-delimited JSON): each line is a complete JSON object. Spark reads this format by default. Each line becomes one row in the DataFrame, and the JSON keys become column names. Spark infers the schema by scanning the data, and unlike CSV inference, JSON inference is generally reliable because JSON preserves the distinction between strings, numbers, and booleans.

# Single-line JSON (default): one JSON object per line
# Example file content:
# {"station_id": "WS-01", "temp_c": -2.3, "date": "2026-03-01"}
# {"station_id": "WS-02", "temp_c": 4.1, "date": "2026-03-01"}

df_weather = spark.read.json('bronze/weather/daily_temps.json')
df_weather.printSchema()
df_weather.show(3)
Nested JSON and Struct Columns

JSON supports nested objects and arrays, which Spark reads as StructType and ArrayType columns. NordGrid's weather API returns forecast data with a nested structure: each record has a station_id at the top level and a forecasts array containing objects with timestamp, predicted_temp, and confidence fields. Spark preserves this nesting in the DataFrame schema. You can access nested fields using dot notation in column references: col('forecasts.predicted_temp').

# Nested JSON example
# {"station_id": "WS-01", "location": {"lat": 59.3, "lon": 18.1}, "readings": [1.2, 1.5, 1.8]}

df_nested = spark.read.json('bronze/weather/stations.json')
df_nested.printSchema()

root
 |-- station_id: string (nullable = true)
 |-- location: struct (nullable = true)
 |    |-- lat: double (nullable = true)
 |    |-- lon: double (nullable = true)
 |-- readings: array (nullable = true)
 |    |-- element: double (containsNull = true)

from pyspark.sql.functions import col

# Access nested struct fields with dot notation
df_flat = df_nested.select(
    'station_id',
    col('location.lat').alias('latitude'),
    col('location.lon').alias('longitude'),
)

df_flat.show(3)
