blocks.title: Flatten nested JSON at the Silver boundary
blocks.content: NordGrid's convention is to read nested JSON as-is in the Bronze layer, preserving the original structure. The Bronze-to-Silver pipeline then flattens the nested fields into simple columns that are easier to filter, join, and aggregate. This separation means Bronze faithfully captures the source format, and Silver provides the clean, flat schema that downstream consumers expect.

Parquet is the dominant file format in data engineering. It stores data in a columnar layout where each column's values are grouped together on disk, compressed independently, and annotated with statistics (min, max, null count). This design makes Parquet dramatically more efficient than CSV or JSON for analytical workloads: Spark can skip entire columns your query does not need (column pruning), skip entire row groups whose statistics show they cannot match your filter (predicate pushdown), and decompress only the data it actually reads. NordGrid's Silver and Gold layers use Parquet exclusively, and every pipeline you have written in this course reads from or writes to Parquet.
Self-Describing Schema

Unlike CSV, Parquet files embed their schema in the file metadata. Column names, data types, and nullability are stored alongside the data. When Spark reads a Parquet file, it gets the schema for free — no inference needed, no header parsing, no type guessing. This is why spark.read.parquet() takes no options in most cases: the file already contains everything Spark needs to know. This self-describing property also means that if the schema changes between file versions, Spark can detect the mismatch, which is the foundation for schema evolution strategies you will learn in the mid-level track.

# Reading Parquet: no options needed
df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

df.printSchema()  # schema from Parquet metadata, not inference
print(f"Rows: {df.count():,}")
print(f"Partitions: {df.rdd.getNumPartitions()}")

root
 |-- meter_id: string (nullable = false)
 |-- daily_kw: double (nullable = true)
 |-- date: date (nullable = false)
 |-- region: string (nullable = false)

Rows: 499,850
Partitions: 8
Column Pruning and Predicate Pushdown

When you chain a select() or filter() after reading Parquet, Spark's Catalyst optimizer pushes these operations into the Parquet reader. If your query selects only meter_id and daily_kw, the reader skips the date and region columns entirely — those bytes are never read from disk. If your query filters for region == 'NORTH' and the Parquet files are organized with region as a partition column, Spark skips the SOUTH, EAST, and WEST directories at the filesystem level. These optimizations happen automatically because of lazy evaluation: Catalyst sees the full plan before reading any data.

# Spark pushes the select and filter into the Parquet reader
df = (spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')
    .select('meter_id', 'daily_kw')        # column pruning: skip date, region
    .filter(col('daily_kw') > 2000)         # predicate pushdown: skip row groups
)

# Verify with explain()
df.explain(True)
# Look for: ReadSchema shows only meter_id, daily_kw
# Look for: PushedFilters shows GreaterThan(daily_kw, 2000.0)

blocks.title: Parquet turns your code into hardware optimization
blocks.content: Every select() you write becomes a column skip at the disk level. Every filter() on a partition column becomes a directory skip at the filesystem level. Every filter() on a data column becomes a row-group skip using Parquet's embedded statistics. You do not need to do anything special to get these optimizations — they happen automatically when you read Parquet and chain transformations. This is the reason the industry standardized on Parquet for analytical data: the format and the optimizer work together to minimize I/O.

In Module 3, lesson 4 you learned that every DataFrame has a schema and that explicit schemas are safer than inference. This lesson expands on that foundation with the full comparison between inference and explicit schemas for file reading, the concrete risks of inference in production, and the NordGrid patterns for managing schemas across pipeline versions. The schema decision is one of the most consequential choices in a pipeline because it determines whether your pipeline is deterministic (same input always produces same output) or fragile (output depends on what Spark infers from today's data).
How Inference Works

When you set inferSchema=True for CSV or let Spark auto-infer for JSON, Spark scans a sample of the data to determine the data type of each column. For CSV, this requires reading the file twice: once for inference, once for the actual load. Spark examines the values and chooses the narrowest type that fits: if all values are integers, it picks LongType; if any value has a decimal, it picks DoubleType; if any value contains letters, it picks StringType. This usually works, but 'usually' is not sufficient for a production pipeline that must behave identically every day.
When Inference Breaks

Inference breaks when the data changes in ways that affect type detection. NordGrid experienced this: the daily_kw column contained only whole numbers for weeks (1250, 870, 2100), so Spark inferred LongType. One day, a meter reported 1250.5, and Spark switched the inference to DoubleType. The downstream Silver pipeline expected LongType, applied integer arithmetic, and produced wrong results. The pipeline did not crash — it ran successfully and wrote incorrect data. This silent type change is the primary risk of schema inference in production.

left.label: Schema Inference
points: Spark scans data to guess types
CSV reads file twice (slow on large files)
Types can change if data changes
Good for quick exploration and prototyping
No code maintenance for schema
Dates often inferred as strings in CSV

right.label: Explicit Schema
points: Types are defined in code — no scanning
CSV reads file once (explicit schema applied directly)
Types are locked — pipeline is deterministic
Required for all production pipelines
Schema must be updated when source format changes
Dates are DateType if defined that way

blocks.content: The NordGrid Schema Management Pattern

NordGrid defines each pipeline's schema as a StructType constant at the top of the script or in a shared schema module. The schema is versioned alongside the pipeline code. When the source system adds or removes a column, the schema is updated in a code change that goes through code review. This means schema changes are tracked in version control, reviewed by a second engineer, and deployed intentionally — not discovered accidentally when Spark's inference produces a different result.

from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StructField, StringType, DoubleType, DateType

# Schema defined as a constant — version-controlled, code-reviewed
METER_BRONZE_SCHEMA = StructType([
    StructField('meter_id',  StringType(), nullable=False),
    StructField('daily_kw',  DoubleType(), nullable=True),   # always DoubleType, never inferred
    StructField('date',      DateType(),   nullable=False),
    StructField('region',    StringType(), nullable=False),
])

# Used in the pipeline
df = spark.read.csv('bronze/meters/', header=True, schema=METER_BRONZE_SCHEMA)

blocks.title: Inference is for notebooks, explicit is for production
blocks.content: Use inferSchema=True in Jupyter notebooks when exploring a new dataset for the first time. Once you understand the schema, define it explicitly and use the explicit version in the pipeline script. NordGrid's code review checklist flags any production script that uses inferSchema=True as a required fix. The rule is simple: if the script runs on a schedule or produces data that other systems consume, the schema must be explicit.

sections.title: Write Modes — append, overwrite, error, ignore

blocks.content: When a pipeline writes output to a directory that already contains data, Spark needs to know what to do with the existing files. Should it add the new data alongside the existing data? Replace everything? Fail to prevent accidental overwrites? The mode parameter on the DataFrameWriter controls this behavior, and choosing the wrong mode can cause data loss, data duplication, or pipeline failures. NordGrid's pipelines use different modes for different scenarios, and understanding when each is appropriate prevents the kind of mistakes that are easy to make and hard to detect.
The Four Write Modes

rows: Fails if the output path already exists
First-time writes, safety when path should be empty
Pipeline crashes if path exists — must handle or clean up
Deletes existing data and writes new data
Full rebuilds, daily pipelines that recompute everything
Accidentally destroys data if pointed at the wrong path
Adds new data alongside existing data
Incremental pipelines that add each day's data
Creates duplicates if the pipeline runs twice for the same date
Does nothing if the output path already exists
Idempotent writes where re-running should not change the output
Silently skips the write — you may not realize it did nothing

blocks.content: NordGrid’s Mode Choices

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# OVERWRITE: NordGrid's Silver pipeline rebuilds the entire output each run
df.write.mode('overwrite').parquet('silver/meters_clean/')

# APPEND: NordGrid's Bronze ingestion adds each day's data to the archive
df_today.write.mode('append').parquet('bronze/meters/')

# ERROR: safety check for a one-time export that should not overwrite
df_export.write.mode('error').parquet('exports/regulatory_q1/')

# IGNORE: idempotent pipeline — if output exists, skip silently
df_gold.write.mode('ignore').parquet('gold/daily_summary/')
The Append Duplication Problem

Append mode's biggest risk is duplicate data. If NordGrid's daily pipeline uses append mode and the pipeline runs twice for March 1st (perhaps due to a retry after a transient failure), the Bronze directory contains March 1st's data twice. Every downstream aggregate — total consumption, average readings, billing totals — is inflated by the duplicate. NordGrid prevents this with two defenses: the Silver pipeline uses overwrite mode (so duplicates in Bronze are eliminated during the rebuild), and the Bronze ingestion pipeline checks whether today's data already exists before appending.

blocks.title: Mode protects your data contract
blocks.content: The write mode is not just a technical setting — it encodes your pipeline's data management strategy. Overwrite means 'I recompute everything from scratch, and the output is always correct.' Append means 'I add incremental data, and the output accumulates over time.' Error means 'This path should be empty, and if it is not, something is wrong.' Choose the mode that matches your pipeline's purpose, not the mode that makes the code run without errors.

Writing Parquet is the complement of reading it: the DataFrameWriter takes a DataFrame, compresses it into columnar Parquet format, and writes one file per partition. The writer is simpler than the reader because Parquet's self-describing format means you do not need to specify headers, delimiters, or null representations — the schema, types, and metadata are all embedded automatically. This lesson covers the writer API, compression options, the relationship between DataFrame partitions and output files, and NordGrid's writing conventions.
The DataFrameWriter API

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# Basic Parquet write
df.write.parquet('gold/meters_summary/')

# With mode and compression
df.write \
    .mode('overwrite') \
    .option('compression', 'snappy') \
    .parquet('gold/meters_summary/')

# Builder pattern (equivalent)
(df.write
    .format('parquet')
    .mode('overwrite')
    .option('compression', 'snappy')
    .save('gold/meters_summary/')
)
Compression Codecs

Parquet supports multiple compression codecs that trade off between file size and read/write speed. Snappy is the default and the most common choice: it provides fast compression and decompression with moderate file size reduction. GZIP compresses more aggressively (smaller files) but is slower to read and write. Uncompressed produces the largest files but the fastest reads. NordGrid uses snappy for all pipeline output because the cluster's bottleneck is typically network and disk I/O, and snappy's balance of speed and compression minimizes total processing time.

rows: All Silver and Gold layer output
Long-term archive where storage cost matters
Newer alternative to gzip with better speed
Temporary staging data that will be rewritten soon

blocks.content: Partitions and Output Files

When Spark writes a DataFrame, each partition produces one output file. If the DataFrame has 8 partitions, the output directory contains 8 Parquet files named part-00000 through part-00007. The number of output files equals the number of partitions at the time of the write. This is important because too many small files (thousands of tiny Parquet files) degrades read performance, and too few large files (one enormous file) prevents parallelism on the read side. NordGrid aims for output files between 128MB and 256MB, adjusting the partition count before writing if needed.

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

print(f"Partitions before write: {df.rdd.getNumPartitions()}")

# If you have too many partitions (many small files), coalesce first
df.coalesce(4).write.mode('overwrite').parquet('gold/meters_compact/')

# If you have too few partitions (few huge files), repartition first
df.repartition(16).write.mode('overwrite').parquet('gold/meters_distributed/')

blocks.title: Control your output file count
blocks.content: The number of output files is determined by the number of DataFrame partitions at write time. Use coalesce(n) to reduce partitions without a shuffle (merges adjacent partitions) or repartition(n) to redistribute evenly with a shuffle. NordGrid's convention: coalesce before writing Gold tables (which are small) to produce a handful of reasonably sized files, and let Silver writes use the natural partition count from the read.

Partitioned writes organize output files into a directory hierarchy based on column values. Instead of writing all rows into one flat directory, partitionBy() creates subdirectories for each unique value of the partition columns: date=2026-03-01/, date=2026-03-02/, and so on. When Spark later reads from this partitioned directory, it can skip entire subdirectories that do not match a filter condition — reading only date=2026-03-01/ instead of scanning all 365 daily directories. This technique is called partition pruning, and it is one of the most powerful performance optimizations in data engineering.
Writing with partitionBy()

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# Write partitioned by date
df.write \
    .mode('overwrite') \
    .partitionBy('date') \
    .parquet('silver/meters_partitioned/')

# Output directory structure:
# silver/meters_partitioned/
#   date=2026-03-01/
#     part-00000.parquet
#     part-00001.parquet
#   date=2026-03-02/
#     part-00000.parquet
#     part-00001.parquet
#   ...
Multi-Column Partitioning

You can partition by multiple columns, creating a nested directory hierarchy. NordGrid partitions the Silver meter table by region and date: the first level has four directories (one per region), and each region directory has subdirectories for each date. This enables two levels of partition pruning: a query for NORTH meters in March skips three region directories and 334 date directories, reading only the 31 relevant date folders under the NORTH directory.

# Multi-column partitioning: region first, then date
df.write \
    .mode('overwrite') \
    .partitionBy('region', 'date') \
    .parquet('silver/meters_partitioned/')

# Output directory structure:
# silver/meters_partitioned/
#   region=NORTH/
#     date=2026-03-01/
#       part-00000.parquet
#     date=2026-03-02/
#       part-00000.parquet
#   region=SOUTH/
#     date=2026-03-01/
#       part-00000.parquet
#     ...
The Small Files Problem

Partitioned writes can produce many small files. If you partition by date and region, and you have 365 dates and 4 regions, you create 1,460 directories. If each directory has 8 files (one per DataFrame partition), you produce 11,680 files. Each file might be only a few megabytes. Reading thousands of small files is slower than reading a few large files because each file incurs metadata overhead: Spark must open the file, read the Parquet footer, check statistics, and close it. NordGrid mitigates this by coalescing within each partition before writing, and by choosing partition columns with low cardinality.

blocks.title: Choose partition columns carefully
blocks.content: Partition columns should have low cardinality (few unique values). Region (4 values) is a good partition column. Meter_id (500,000 values) is a terrible partition column — it would create 500,000 directories. Date is acceptable for daily data but produces 365 directories per year. NordGrid's rule of thumb: the total number of partition directories should stay under 10,000. Beyond that, the small files problem dominates any partition pruning benefit.

NordGrid's data platform uses multiple file formats: CSV for raw ingestion, JSON for API data, and Parquet for all processed layers. Each format has strengths and weaknesses that determine where it fits in the pipeline architecture. Understanding these trade-offs helps you choose the right format for each stage of your pipeline and explains why the industry converged on Parquet as the standard for analytical storage. This lesson compares five formats across the dimensions that matter most to data engineers: schema support, compression, read speed, write speed, and ecosystem compatibility.
The Five Formats

rows: Raw data ingestion from source systems
Partial — types for strings, numbers, booleans
Full — embedded column names, types, nullability
Production tables needing updates, deletes, and time travel

blocks.content: Row-Oriented vs Column-Oriented

The fundamental distinction is between row-oriented formats (CSV, JSON) and column-oriented formats (Parquet, ORC). Row-oriented formats store all columns of one row together on disk: meter_id, daily_kw, date, region for row 1, then all columns for row 2. Column-oriented formats store all values of one column together: all 500,000 meter_id values, then all 500,000 daily_kw values. Column orientation is dramatically better for analytical queries that read a few columns from many rows, which is the dominant pattern in data engineering pipelines.

blocks.title: Row-oriented vs column-oriented storage
blocks.content:
Row-Oriented (CSV, JSON)              Column-Oriented (Parquet, ORC)

[M-001, 1250.5, 2026-03-01, NORTH]    [M-001, M-002, M-003, ... M-500K]
[M-002,  870.2, 2026-03-01, SOUTH]    [1250.5, 870.2, 2100.0, ... 440.7]
[M-003, 2100.0, 2026-03-01, EAST]     [2026-03-01, 2026-03-01, ... ]
[  ...                           ]     [NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, ... WEST]

Reading daily_kw:                      Reading daily_kw:
Must scan ALL columns of ALL rows      Reads ONLY the daily_kw column
→ reads 100% of data                   → reads ~25% of data (1 of 4 columns)
blocks.caption: Column-oriented formats let Spark read only the columns needed for the query. For a query that uses one column out of four, Parquet reads 75% less data than CSV.

blocks.content: Where Each Format Fits at NordGrid

NordGrid's pipeline architecture maps each format to a specific purpose. CSV is the input format for the Bronze layer because source systems produce it. JSON is the input format for weather and SCADA API data. Parquet is the output format for Silver and Gold layers because it provides the best read performance for the analytical queries and pipeline chains that consume processed data. Delta Lake is used for a small number of Gold tables that require update and delete operations (like a slowly changing dimension table), but the team plans to expand Delta Lake usage as the platform matures.

blocks.title: Format choice is a pipeline architecture decision
blocks.content: Do not choose a file format based on familiarity. Choose it based on where the data sits in the pipeline and how it will be consumed. Data entering the platform: use whatever the source produces (CSV, JSON). Data being processed: always Parquet. Data being served to dashboards: Parquet or Delta. Data being exported to external systems: whatever the recipient requires (CSV, JSON, Parquet).

This lesson brings together every technique from the course so far into a complete NordGrid pipeline that reads raw CSV from Bronze, transforms it into clean Parquet in Silver, aggregates it into Gold, and writes the final output. Every module contributes: SparkSession creation (Module 2), DataFrame operations (Module 3), selection and filtering (Module 4), column transformations (Module 5), aggregation (Module 6), joins (Module 7), and file I/O (this module). This is the pipeline pattern that NordGrid's team runs every morning to process the previous day's meter readings.
The Complete Pipeline

from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import (
    col, year, month, quarter, dayofweek, upper, trim,
    round as spark_round, when, count, sum as spark_sum, avg,
    current_timestamp, lit
)
from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StructField, StringType, DoubleType, DateType

spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .appName('nordgrid-daily-meters-full-pipeline') \
    .config('spark.sql.shuffle.partitions', '16') \
    .getOrCreate()

# ── SCHEMAS ──
bronze_schema = StructType([
    StructField('mtr_id',      StringType(), True),
    StructField('kwh_reading', StringType(), True),
    StructField('reading_date',StringType(), True),
    StructField('rgn_code',    StringType(), True),
])

try:
    # ── BRONZE READ ──
    df = spark.read.csv('bronze/meters/', header=True, schema=bronze_schema)

    # ── BRONZE → SILVER ──
    df = (df
        .withColumn('daily_kw', col('kwh_reading').cast('double'))
        .withColumn('date', col('reading_date').cast('date'))
        .withColumn('region', upper(trim(col('rgn_code'))))
        .filter(col('daily_kw').isNotNull())
        .filter(col('daily_kw') > 0)
        .filter(col('daily_kw') < 10000)
        .dropDuplicates(['mtr_id', 'date'])
        .withColumn('year', year(col('date')))
        .withColumn('month', month(col('date')))
        .withColumn('is_valid', lit(True))
        .withColumn('tier',
            when(col('daily_kw') < 500, 'low')
            .when(col('daily_kw') < 2000, 'medium')
            .when(col('daily_kw') < 4000, 'high')
            .otherwise('critical')
        )
        .select(
            col('mtr_id').alias('meter_id'),
            'daily_kw', 'date', 'region',
            'year', 'month', 'tier', 'is_valid',
        )
    )

    # ── SILVER WRITE ──
    df.write.mode('overwrite').partitionBy('region', 'date').parquet('silver/meters/')

    # ── SILVER → GOLD ──
    df_silver = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

    df_gold = (df_silver
        .filter(col('is_valid'))
        .groupBy('region', 'month')
        .agg(
            count('meter_id').alias('reading_count'),
            spark_round(spark_sum('daily_kw'), 0).alias('total_kw'),
            spark_round(avg('daily_kw'), 1).alias('avg_kw'),
        )
    )

    # ── GOLD WRITE ──
    df_gold.coalesce(1).write.mode('overwrite').parquet('gold/monthly_regional_summary/')

    print('Pipeline complete: Bronze → Silver → Gold.')

finally:
    spark.stop()
How Each Module Contributed

blocks.title: Module 8 complete — you can build full pipelines
blocks.content: You now know how to read CSV, JSON, and Parquet files with the DataFrameReader, how to choose between schema inference and explicit schemas, how to write data with the four write modes, how to compress and partition Parquet output, and how to evaluate format trade-offs. Combined with everything from Modules 1–7, you have the complete toolkit to build production-grade read-transform-write pipelines. In Module 9, you will expand your function vocabulary with Spark's built-in function library, and in Module 10, you will learn the SQL interface that many teams use alongside the DataFrame API.

chapters.title: Built-in Functions and Expressions
chapters.description: Gain working knowledge of Spark’s built-in function library, learn to compose functions into complex expressions, and understand when to use a built-in function versus a user-defined function.

blocks.content: Throughout this course you have used functions like col(), year(), upper(), when(), and count() without thinking much about where they come from. They all live in pyspark.sql.functions — Spark's standard library of column operations. This module is a systematic tour of the function library, expanding beyond the functions you met in Modules 4–6 and introducing entirely new categories: collection functions for arrays and maps, a comprehensive view of null-handling functions, window functions that aggregate without collapsing rows, and user-defined functions for custom logic. Understanding the breadth of the built-in library is important because every built-in function runs on the JVM at native speed, while custom Python code runs in a separate process with serialization overhead.
Why Built-in Functions Are Fast

When you write col('daily_kw') * 1000 or upper(col('region')), Spark compiles the expression into JVM bytecode and executes it directly on the executors. The Python layer is only involved on the driver, where it builds the plan. The data never leaves the JVM. In contrast, a Python UDF (user-defined function) must serialize each row from the JVM to Python, execute the Python function, serialize the result back, and return it to the JVM. This serialization round-trip is 10–100x slower than a built-in function for the same operation. This performance gap is why NordGrid's coding standard requires checking for a built-in function before writing any custom Python logic.

blocks.title: Built-in function vs Python UDF execution path
blocks.content:
Built-in Function (fast)              Python UDF (slow)
──────────────────────              ──────────────────

Python driver:                        Python driver:
  plan: upper(col('region'))            plan: my_udf(col('region'))
         │                                      │
         ▼                                      ▼
JVM executor:                         JVM executor:
  read row ─► upper() ─► result         read row ─► serialize ─►
  (all in JVM, no Python)                              │
                                      Python worker process:
                                        ─► deserialize ─► my_func()
                                        ─► serialize result ─►
                                                    │
                                      JVM executor:
                                        ─► deserialize ─► result
blocks.caption: Built-in functions execute entirely in the JVM. Python UDFs add four serialization steps per row, making them 10–100x slower for the same operation.

blocks.content: The Function Categories

The pyspark.sql.functions module contains over 300 functions organized into logical categories. You already know many of them from Module 5 — this module fills in the gaps and introduces the categories you have not seen yet. The following table maps each category to the lesson that covers it. Lessons 2–4 revisit string, date, and numeric functions with additional functions not covered in Module 5. Lessons 5–7 introduce new categories. Lessons 8–9 cover window functions and UDFs. Lesson 10 provides the decision framework for choosing between approaches.

rows: lpad, rpad, split, initcap, translate, repeat, reverse, plus Module 5 review
to_date, to_timestamp, unix_timestamp, from_unixtime, last_day, next_day, trunc
ceil, floor, log, log10, greatest, least, signum, factorial
array, array_contains, explode, split, size, map_keys, map_values, element_at
isNull, isNotNull, coalesce, na.drop, na.fill, na.replace
row_number, rank, dense_rank, lag, lead, running totals
udf(), type annotations, pandas UDF overview

blocks.title: You already know more than you think
blocks.content: Module 5 taught you the most common functions in each category: upper, trim, year, month, round, abs, when, col, lit, expr. This module builds on that foundation by adding less common but equally important functions: split for parsing delimited strings, explode for flattening arrays, coalesce for null replacement, and window functions for running totals. By the end of this module, you will have working knowledge of every function category in Spark's library.

Module 5, lesson 4 introduced the core string functions: upper, lower, trim, substring, concat, concat_ws, length, regexp_replace, and regexp_extract. This lesson expands the toolkit with functions you will encounter in NordGrid's pipelines as data cleaning needs become more complex: split for breaking a string into an array, lpad and rpad for fixed-width formatting, initcap for title-casing customer names, translate for character-level replacements, and repeat for generating test data. Together with the Module 5 functions, these give you a comprehensive string manipulation vocabulary.
split() — Breaking Strings into Arrays

The split() function divides a string column into an array of substrings based on a delimiter pattern. NordGrid's composite meter IDs follow the format REGION-TYPE-NUMBER (e.g., 'NO-M-001'). Splitting on the dash produces an array of three elements that can be accessed individually. split() is one of the most commonly used string functions in data engineering because source data frequently packs multiple pieces of information into a single delimited string.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, split, element_at

df = spark.createDataFrame(
    [('NO-M-001', 1250.5), ('SO-C-002', 870.2), ('EA-R-003', 2100.0)],
    schema=['meter_id', 'daily_kw']
)

# Split meter_id into parts
df = df.withColumn('parts', split(col('meter_id'), '-'))

# Extract individual elements (1-based indexing)
df = df.withColumn('region_code', element_at(col('parts'), 1))
df = df.withColumn('meter_type', element_at(col('parts'), 2))
df = df.withColumn('meter_num', element_at(col('parts'), 3))

df.select('meter_id', 'region_code', 'meter_type', 'meter_num').show()
Padding and Formatting

lpad() and rpad() pad a string to a fixed width by adding characters on the left or right. NordGrid uses lpad() to ensure meter numbers are always three digits with leading zeros: meter 1 becomes '001', meter 42 becomes '042'. This fixed-width formatting is required by the regulatory reporting system, which expects meter numbers in a specific field width. initcap() capitalizes the first letter of each word, which NordGrid uses to standardize customer names from the CRM system.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, lpad, rpad, initcap

df = spark.createDataFrame(
    [('1', 'john smith'), ('42', 'anna JONES'), ('100', 'bob lee')],
    schema=['meter_num', 'customer_name']
)

# Pad meter numbers to 3 digits with leading zeros
df = df.withColumn('meter_num_padded', lpad(col('meter_num'), 3, '0'))

# Title-case customer names
df = df.withColumn('customer_name_clean', initcap(col('customer_name')))

df.show()
translate() and repeat()

translate() performs character-level replacement: each character in the source string is replaced by the corresponding character in the replacement string. NordGrid uses it to sanitize file paths by replacing slashes with underscores. repeat() duplicates a string a specified number of times — useful for generating test data or building fixed-length filler strings. These are specialized functions that appear infrequently in pipelines but are essential when you need them.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, translate, repeat

df = spark.createDataFrame(
    [('bronze/meters/2026-03-01',), ('silver/readings/north',)],
    schema=['path']
)

# Replace / with _ for use as a filename
df = df.withColumn('safe_name', translate(col('path'), '/-', '__'))

df.show(truncate=False)

blocks.title: split() + element_at() replaces most regex parsing
blocks.content: When source data packs multiple values into a single delimited string, the split() and element_at() combination is cleaner and more readable than regex. Use split() when the delimiter is consistent and the position of each field is known. Use regexp_extract() when the pattern is more complex or the position varies. NordGrid's convention: prefer split() for simple delimiters, regex for complex patterns.

Module 5, lesson 5 covered the core date functions: year, month, dayofmonth, dayofweek, date_add, date_sub, datediff, months_between, and date_format. This lesson adds the conversion functions (to_date, to_timestamp, unix_timestamp, from_unixtime) that handle the messy reality of date parsing, plus boundary functions (last_day, next_day, trunc) that simplify period-based aggregations. NordGrid's pipelines encounter dates in many formats — ISO strings from internal systems, Unix timestamps from SCADA sensors, and European-format strings from partner exports — and these conversion functions handle all of them.
Parsing Dates with to_date() and to_timestamp()

When dates arrive as strings in a non-standard format, cast('date') may not parse them correctly because cast expects the ISO format YYYY-MM-DD. The to_date() function accepts a format string that tells Spark how the date is structured. to_timestamp() does the same for timestamps that include a time component. NordGrid's partner exports use the European date format DD.MM.YYYY, which requires to_date with the format 'dd.MM.yyyy'. Without the format string, Spark would either produce null (failed parse) or misinterpret the values.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, to_date, to_timestamp

# Partner data uses European date format: DD.MM.YYYY
df = spark.createDataFrame(
    [('M-001', '01.03.2026'), ('M-002', '15.03.2026')],
    schema=['meter_id', 'date_str']
)

# Parse with explicit format
df = df.withColumn('date', to_date(col('date_str'), 'dd.MM.yyyy'))

# Timestamp parsing with time component
df_ts = spark.createDataFrame(
    [('T-001', '2026-03-01 14:30:00'),],
    schema=['turbine_id', 'ts_str']
)
df_ts = df_ts.withColumn('ts', to_timestamp(col('ts_str'), 'yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss'))

df.show()
df_ts.show()
Unix Timestamps

SCADA telemetry from NordGrid's wind turbines often uses Unix timestamps — the number of seconds since January 1, 1970. The unix_timestamp() function converts a date or timestamp string to a Unix timestamp integer. The from_unixtime() function does the reverse: it converts a Unix timestamp integer to a formatted timestamp string. These functions bridge the gap between systems that use human-readable dates and systems that use epoch-based integers.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, unix_timestamp, from_unixtime

# SCADA data arrives with Unix timestamps
df_scada = spark.createDataFrame(
    [('T-001', 1772553000), ('T-002', 1772556600)],
    schema=['turbine_id', 'epoch']
)

# Convert Unix timestamp to readable timestamp
df_scada = df_scada.withColumn('ts', from_unixtime(col('epoch')))

# Reverse: convert a date string to Unix timestamp
df_scada = df_scada.withColumn('epoch_check', unix_timestamp(col('ts')))

df_scada.show(truncate=False)
Date Boundary Functions

Boundary functions help align dates to period boundaries for aggregation. last_day() returns the last day of the month for a given date. trunc() truncates a date to a specified unit (month, year, week), returning the first day of that period. next_day() returns the next occurrence of a specified day of the week. NordGrid uses trunc(col('date'), 'month') to align daily readings to their month boundary before aggregating monthly totals, ensuring that each reading is attributed to the correct month regardless of when the aggregation pipeline runs.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, last_day, trunc, next_day

df = spark.createDataFrame(
    [('2026-03-15',), ('2026-03-28',), ('2026-04-02',)],
    schema=['date']
).withColumn('date', col('date').cast('date'))

df = (df
    .withColumn('month_start', trunc(col('date'), 'month'))   # first day of month
    .withColumn('month_end', last_day(col('date')))           # last day of month
    .withColumn('next_monday', next_day(col('date'), 'Mon'))  # next Monday
)

df.show()

blocks.title: Parse once at Bronze, use DateType everywhere after
blocks.content: NordGrid's convention: convert every date string to DateType or TimestampType in the Bronze-to-Silver pipeline using to_date() or to_timestamp() with the correct format string. Once the date is a proper DateType, all date functions (year, month, datediff, trunc, last_day) work natively without format strings. Parsing the date once eliminates repeated format guessing in downstream pipelines.

Module 5, lesson 6 covered basic arithmetic (addition, subtraction, multiplication, division) and the most common math functions (round, abs). This lesson adds the remaining mathematical functions that NordGrid's pipelines use: ceil and floor for directional rounding, log and log10 for logarithmic scaling, greatest and least for row-level comparisons, and signum for sign detection. These functions appear less frequently than round and abs, but each solves a specific problem that would otherwise require verbose Column expressions or, worse, a UDF.
Directional Rounding: ceil() and floor()

round() rounds to the nearest value. ceil() always rounds up to the next integer. floor() always rounds down. NordGrid's billing system uses ceil() to ensure that fractional kilowatts are always rounded up to the next whole unit for billing purposes — a reading of 1250.1 kW is billed as 1251 kW. The regulatory system uses floor() to report conservative consumption estimates where rounding down is the policy for compliance calculations.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, ceil, floor, round as spark_round

df = spark.createDataFrame(
    [('M-001', 1250.3), ('M-002', 870.7), ('M-003', 2100.0)],
    schema=['meter_id', 'daily_kw']
)

df = (df
    .withColumn('rounded', spark_round(col('daily_kw'), 0))  # nearest: 1250, 871, 2100
    .withColumn('ceiling', ceil(col('daily_kw')))             # up:      1251, 871, 2100
    .withColumn('floored', floor(col('daily_kw')))            # down:    1250, 870, 2100
)

df.show()
Logarithmic Functions

log() computes the natural logarithm and log10() computes the base-10 logarithm. NordGrid uses log10 to create logarithmic bins for consumption analysis: instead of grouping meters into fixed-width bins (0–500, 500–1000, 1000–1500), the team groups by order of magnitude (1–10, 10–100, 100–1000, 1000–10000). Logarithmic binning reveals the distribution of consumption across a wide range of values that fixed-width bins would compress into a single bucket or spread across too many buckets.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, log10, floor

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# Create logarithmic consumption bins: order of magnitude
df = df.withColumn('log_bin', floor(log10(col('daily_kw'))))

# Count meters in each log bin
df.groupBy('log_bin').count().orderBy('log_bin').show()
Row-Level Comparisons: greatest() and least()

greatest() returns the largest value among multiple columns in the same row. least() returns the smallest. These are different from max() and min(), which aggregate across rows. NordGrid uses greatest() to determine the peak reading across multiple sampling periods within a single day when a meter reports multiple readings, and least() to find the minimum temperature across multiple weather stations in a region for cold-weather alerts.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, greatest, least

# Multiple readings per meter per day (morning, afternoon, evening)
df = spark.createDataFrame([
    ('M-001', 800.0, 1250.5, 950.0),
    ('M-002', 600.0,  870.2, 720.0),
], schema=['meter_id', 'morning_kw', 'afternoon_kw', 'evening_kw'])

# Peak reading across the three periods (row-level max)
df = df.withColumn('peak_kw', greatest(col('morning_kw'), col('afternoon_kw'), col('evening_kw')))
df = df.withColumn('min_kw', least(col('morning_kw'), col('afternoon_kw'), col('evening_kw')))

df.show()

blocks.title: greatest/least compare across columns, max/min aggregate across rows
blocks.content: This distinction trips up many engineers. greatest(col('a'), col('b'), col('c')) compares three columns within the same row and returns the largest value. max('a') compares all values of column 'a' across all rows and returns the single largest. They solve different problems: greatest() for row-level comparisons, max() for column-level aggregations.

Most columns in NordGrid's data contain scalar values: a single string, number, or date per cell. But some data naturally contains collections: a meter that reports multiple readings per day produces an array of values, and a telemetry event with variable attributes produces a map of key-value pairs. PySpark supports two collection types — ArrayType and MapType — and provides functions for creating, querying, and transforming them. The most important of these is explode(), which converts an array column into multiple rows, one per array element. This lesson covers the collection types and the functions you are most likely to encounter.
Array Columns

An array column holds a list of values of the same type. You can create arrays from existing columns using the array() function, from a split() operation on a delimited string, or by reading data that already contains arrays (like nested JSON). NordGrid's sub-hourly meter data reports multiple samples per reading period as an array: [1250.0, 1255.3, 1248.7] represents three samples taken during one hour.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, array, array_contains, size, element_at

# Create an array column from individual columns
df = spark.createDataFrame([
    ('M-001', 1250.0, 1255.3, 1248.7),
    ('M-002',  870.2,  875.0,  868.5),
], schema=['meter_id', 'sample_1', 'sample_2', 'sample_3'])

df = df.withColumn('samples', array(col('sample_1'), col('sample_2'), col('sample_3')))

# Array operations
df = df.withColumn('num_samples', size(col('samples')))
df = df.withColumn('first_sample', element_at(col('samples'), 1))  # 1-based
df = df.withColumn('has_high', array_contains(col('samples'), 1255.3))

df.select('meter_id', 'samples', 'num_samples', 'first_sample', 'has_high').show(truncate=False)
explode() — Arrays to Rows

The explode() function is the most important collection function in data engineering. It takes an array column and produces one row per array element, duplicating the other columns for each element. A DataFrame with 2 rows and an array of 3 samples becomes a DataFrame with 6 rows, each containing one sample. NordGrid uses explode() to normalize sub-hourly meter samples into individual rows for per-sample analysis and quality checking.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, explode

df = spark.createDataFrame([
    ('M-001', [1250.0, 1255.3, 1248.7]),
    ('M-002', [870.2, 875.0, 868.5]),
], schema=['meter_id', 'samples'])

# Explode: each array element becomes its own row
df_exploded = df.select('meter_id', explode(col('samples')).alias('sample'))
df_exploded.show()
Map Columns

A map column holds key-value pairs, similar to a Python dictionary. Maps are less common than arrays but appear in telemetry data where events carry variable attributes. NordGrid's SCADA events include a properties map with keys like 'firmware_version', 'signal_strength', and 'error_code' — attributes that vary between turbine models. map_keys(), map_values(), and element_at() (with a string key) extract data from map columns.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, map_keys, map_values, element_at, create_map, lit

# Create a map column
df = spark.createDataFrame([
    ('T-001', {'firmware': '3.2.1', 'signal': '92'}),
    ('T-002', {'firmware': '3.1.0', 'signal': '78', 'error': 'E04'}),
], schema=['turbine_id', 'properties'])

# Extract from map
df = df.withColumn('firmware', element_at(col('properties'), 'firmware'))
df = df.withColumn('all_keys', map_keys(col('properties')))

df.select('turbine_id', 'firmware', 'all_keys').show(truncate=False)

blocks.title: explode() multiplies your row count
blocks.content: After exploding, the DataFrame has more rows than before — potentially many more. If 500,000 meters each have 24 hourly samples, exploding produces 12 million rows. This is correct and intentional, but be aware of the row count change when validating pipeline output. NordGrid's convention: add a comment noting the expected row multiplication factor whenever explode() is used.

Module 5, lesson 7 introduced the when().otherwise() pattern for conditional column values. This lesson expands the conditional toolkit with coalesce(), which returns the first non-null value from a list of columns, and the less common ifnull(), nullif(), and nanvl() functions. These functions handle specific conditional patterns more concisely than when() chains. NordGrid's pipelines use coalesce() extensively for null replacement and fallback values — it is arguably the most important function in this lesson because it solves the ubiquitous problem of providing default values for missing data.
coalesce() — First Non-Null Value

coalesce() takes two or more columns and returns the value from the first column that is not null. If all columns are null, the result is null. This is the standard pattern for replacing null values with a fallback. NordGrid uses coalesce() to fill missing meter readings with the average for that meter's region, or to fall back to a default customer type when the CRM lookup returns null.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, coalesce, lit

df = spark.createDataFrame([
    ('M-001', 1250.5, 'residential'),
    ('M-002', None,    'commercial'),
    ('M-003', 2100.0, None),
    ('M-004', None,    None),
], schema=['meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'customer_type'])

# Replace null daily_kw with 0.0
df = df.withColumn('daily_kw_safe', coalesce(col('daily_kw'), lit(0.0)))

# Replace null customer_type with 'unknown'
df = df.withColumn('type_safe', coalesce(col('customer_type'), lit('unknown')))

df.show()
Multi-Column Fallback with coalesce()

coalesce() accepts more than two arguments, creating a fallback chain. NordGrid uses this when meter readings come from multiple sources: the primary SCADA feed, a backup cellular connection, and a manual reading. coalesce(col('scada_kw'), col('cellular_kw'), col('manual_kw'), lit(0.0)) takes the first available reading from the most reliable source, falling through to less reliable sources, and finally to zero if no reading exists.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, coalesce, lit

df = spark.createDataFrame([
    ('M-001', 1250.5, None,    None),     # SCADA available
    ('M-002', None,   870.2,   None),     # SCADA down, cellular available
    ('M-003', None,   None,    2100.0),   # both down, manual entry
    ('M-004', None,   None,    None),     # no reading from any source
], schema=['meter_id', 'scada_kw', 'cellular_kw', 'manual_kw'])

# Fallback chain: best available reading
df = df.withColumn('daily_kw',
    coalesce(col('scada_kw'), col('cellular_kw'), col('manual_kw'), lit(0.0))
)

df.select('meter_id', 'daily_kw').show()

blocks.title: coalesce() vs fillna()
blocks.content: Both replace nulls, but they work differently. fillna({'daily_kw': 0.0}) replaces nulls in one column with a fixed literal value. coalesce(col('a'), col('b'), lit(0)) creates a fallback chain across multiple columns. Use fillna() when you just need a default value. Use coalesce() when you need to choose between multiple candidate columns. NordGrid uses fillna() for simple defaults and coalesce() for multi-source fallback logic.

Null handling has appeared in multiple modules: Module 4 lesson 6 covered three-valued logic and isNull/isNotNull for filtering, Module 6 lesson 10 covered how nulls affect aggregations, and the previous lesson introduced coalesce for null replacement. This lesson brings all null-handling strategies together in one place and adds the na accessor methods (na.drop, na.fill, na.replace) that provide DataFrame-level null operations. NordGrid's data quality layer depends on careful null handling because sensor malfunctions, communication failures, and missing reference data all manifest as null values in different columns.
The Complete Null-Handling Toolkit

rows: Check if a column value is null
Check if a column value is not null
Return first non-null from a list of columns
Replace specific values with other values

blocks.content: na.fill() — Column-Specific Defaults

The na.fill() method replaces null values with a default, and you can specify different defaults for different columns by passing a dictionary. NordGrid uses this to fill missing meter readings with zero (the meter was offline) and missing customer types with 'unknown' (the CRM lookup failed). Unlike coalesce(), which creates a Column expression for use in withColumn(), na.fill() operates on the entire DataFrame at once and produces a new DataFrame with nulls replaced.

df = spark.createDataFrame([
    ('M-001', 1250.5, 'residential'),
    ('M-002', None,    'commercial'),
    ('M-003', 2100.0, None),
], schema=['meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'customer_type'])

# Fill nulls with column-specific defaults
df_filled = df.na.fill({
    'daily_kw': 0.0,
    'customer_type': 'unknown',
})

df_filled.show()
na.replace() — Value Substitution

The na.replace() method replaces specific non-null values with other values. This is useful when source data uses sentinel values like 'NA', 'N/A', '-999', or 'MISSING' instead of proper nulls. NordGrid's partner exports sometimes use '-1' to indicate a missing reading instead of leaving the field empty. na.replace() converts these sentinel values to proper nulls (or to a different value) so that downstream null-handling logic works correctly.

df = spark.createDataFrame([
    ('M-001', 1250.5),
    ('M-002', -1.0),     # sentinel for missing
    ('M-003', -999.0),   # another sentinel
], schema=['meter_id', 'daily_kw'])

# Replace sentinel values with null
df_clean = df.na.replace([-1.0, -999.0], None, subset=['daily_kw'])
df_clean.show()

blocks.title: Handle nulls at the Silver boundary
blocks.content: NordGrid's convention: all null handling happens during the Bronze-to-Silver transformation. By the time data reaches the Silver layer, every column's null policy is defined: daily_kw nulls are filled with zero or dropped (depending on the pipeline), customer_type nulls are filled with 'unknown', and sentinel values have been converted to proper nulls. Gold pipelines can trust that Silver data has consistent null behavior and do not need their own null-handling logic.

Every aggregation you have written so far collapses rows: groupBy('region').agg(sum('daily_kw')) reduces 500,000 rows to 4 rows. But sometimes you need to compute a summary value and attach it to each row without losing the individual row detail. Window functions solve this problem. They compute an aggregation over a group of rows (called a window) and assign the result to each row in the group, preserving the original row count. NordGrid uses window functions for ranking meters by consumption within each region, computing running totals of daily consumption, and finding each meter's previous day's reading for day-over-day comparisons.
The Window Specification

A window function needs two pieces of information: which rows belong to the same window (partitionBy), and in what order those rows should be processed (orderBy). The Window object from pyspark.sql.window defines these. Window.partitionBy('region') creates a window for each region. Adding .orderBy('date') sorts the rows within each window by date. The window function then computes its result for each row based on the other rows in the same window.

from pyspark.sql.window import Window
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, row_number, rank, sum as spark_sum, lag, lead

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# Define the window: partition by region, order by daily_kw descending
window_rank = Window.partitionBy('region').orderBy(col('daily_kw').desc())

# row_number: unique sequential number within each window
df = df.withColumn('rank_in_region', row_number().over(window_rank))

# Show the top 3 meters per region
df.filter(col('rank_in_region') <= 3) \
    .select('region', 'meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'rank_in_region') \
    .orderBy('region', 'rank_in_region').show(12)
lag() and lead() — Previous and Next Row Values

lag() returns the value from a previous row in the window, and lead() returns the value from a following row. NordGrid uses lag() to compute day-over-day consumption change: for each meter, the previous day's daily_kw is brought alongside today's reading, and the difference reveals whether consumption increased or decreased. This is the window function pattern that replaces the self-join approach many engineers use when they first encounter this requirement.

from pyspark.sql.window import Window
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, lag

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# Window: same meter, ordered by date
window_meter = Window.partitionBy('meter_id').orderBy('date')

# Previous day's reading
df = df.withColumn('prev_day_kw', lag('daily_kw', 1).over(window_meter))

# Day-over-day change
df = df.withColumn('daily_change', col('daily_kw') - col('prev_day_kw'))

df.filter(col('meter_id') == 'M-001') \
    .select('meter_id', 'date', 'daily_kw', 'prev_day_kw', 'daily_change') \
    .orderBy('date').show(5)

blocks.title: Window functions preserve your row count
blocks.content: Unlike groupBy().agg(), which collapses rows into groups, window functions add a new column to every row without reducing the row count. The input DataFrame has 500,000 rows, and after a window function, it still has 500,000 rows — each enriched with the window computation. This is what makes window functions essential for analytics: you get the summary alongside the detail, not instead of it.

Built-in functions cover the vast majority of transformation needs, but occasionally you need logic that no built-in function provides. Perhaps NordGrid's billing formula involves a complex lookup that changes by customer contract, or a data quality check requires calling a Python validation library. In these cases, you can write a user-defined function (UDF) — a Python function that Spark applies to each row. UDFs are powerful and flexible, but they come with a critical performance cost: every row must be serialized from the JVM to Python, processed, and serialized back. This lesson covers how to create and use UDFs, and why they should be your last resort rather than your first choice.
Creating a UDF

A UDF is a regular Python function wrapped with the udf() decorator or function from pyspark.sql.functions. The decorator specifies the return type so that Spark knows how to integrate the result back into the DataFrame's schema. Once registered, the UDF can be used in withColumn(), select(), and filter() like any other Column expression. But unlike built-in functions, the code runs in Python on the executors, not in the JVM.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, udf
from pyspark.sql.types import StringType

# Define a Python function
def classify_meter(meter_id):
    """Custom classification logic that no built-in function provides."""
    if meter_id is None:
        return 'unknown'
    prefix = meter_id.split('-')[0]
    return {'NO': 'nordic', 'SO': 'southern', 'EA': 'eastern', 'WE': 'western'}.get(prefix, 'other')

# Register it as a UDF with return type
classify_meter_udf = udf(classify_meter, StringType())

# Use it in a transformation
df = spark.createDataFrame(
    [('NO-M-001',), ('SO-C-002',), ('EA-R-003',), ('XX-Z-999',)],
    schema=['meter_id']
)

df = df.withColumn('zone', classify_meter_udf(col('meter_id')))
df.show()
The Performance Cost

The classify_meter function above could be rewritten as a when().otherwise() chain using built-in functions — and the built-in version would be 10–100x faster. The UDF version forces Spark to serialize each meter_id string from JVM to Python (one per row), execute the Python function, and serialize the result back. For 500,000 rows, this means 1 million serialization operations. For the full three-year archive of 182 million rows, the UDF adds minutes of overhead that the built-in version avoids entirely. NordGrid's coding standard flags every UDF for review and requires a comment explaining why a built-in function was not sufficient.

left.label: Built-in Functions
points: Execute in the JVM at native speed
Catalyst can optimize them (push into readers, fuse)
No additional process needed
Cover 90%+ of transformation needs
Always the first choice

right.label: Python UDFs
points: Execute in Python with serialization overhead
Catalyst treats UDFs as black boxes — no optimization
Require a Python worker process on each executor
Needed for custom logic not available in built-ins
Last resort when no built-in exists

blocks.title: UDFs are a last resort
blocks.content: Before writing a UDF, search the pyspark.sql.functions documentation for a built-in that does what you need. If no exact match exists, try composing built-in functions (when/otherwise, coalesce, regexp_extract). If the logic genuinely cannot be expressed with built-ins — for example, calling an external validation library or applying a proprietary algorithm — then a UDF is justified. NordGrid's rule: every UDF in production code must have a comment explaining why built-in functions were insufficient.

You now have three ways to express column transformations in PySpark: built-in functions from pyspark.sql.functions, SQL expressions via expr(), and Python UDFs for custom logic. Each approach produces correct results, but they differ dramatically in performance, readability, and optimizability. This lesson provides a decision framework for choosing between them, covering the performance hierarchy, the readability trade-offs, and NordGrid's coding standards. The goal is not to memorize rules but to develop an instinct for reaching for the right tool first.
The Performance Hierarchy

Performance ranks in a clear hierarchy from fastest to slowest. Built-in Column functions are fastest because they execute entirely in the JVM with no serialization. SQL expressions via expr() are equally fast because Spark compiles them to the same JVM operations. Pandas UDFs (also called vectorized UDFs) are the next tier — they batch rows into Pandas DataFrames and process them in Python, avoiding per-row serialization but still requiring JVM-to-Python transfer. Standard Python UDFs are the slowest because they serialize one row at a time between the JVM and Python.

rows: When SQL syntax is clearer (CASE WHEN, BETWEEN)
When you need Python libraries (NumPy, scikit-learn) on batches
Last resort: logic impossible with built-ins

blocks.content: The Decision Framework

NordGrid's coding standard defines a three-step decision process. Step 1: can the transformation be expressed with built-in functions? Check pyspark.sql.functions first — the answer is 'yes' for over 90% of transformations. Step 2: is SQL syntax clearer for this expression? If the logic involves CASE WHEN with many branches, BETWEEN, or nested conditions, use expr(). Step 3: does the transformation require custom Python logic that no built-in can express? Only then use a UDF, and prefer a Pandas UDF over a standard UDF for better performance.

blocks.title: NordGrid's function selection decision tree
blocks.content:
Can a built-in function do it?
│
├─ YES ─► Use the built-in function. Done.
│
└─ NO
    │
    ├─ Can a combination of built-ins do it?
    │   ├─ YES ─► Compose built-ins (when, coalesce, etc.). Done.
    │   └─ NO
    │       │
    │       ├─ Is SQL syntax clearer? ─► Use expr(). Done.
    │       │
    │       └─ Does it need Python libraries or custom logic?
    │           ├─ Batch processing OK? ─► Pandas UDF. Done.
    │           └─ Row-by-row needed? ─► Python UDF. Done.
    │                                    (add comment explaining why)
blocks.caption: Start at the top and follow the first 'YES' path. Most transformations exit at the first decision point. UDFs are the last branch for a reason.

blocks.title: Module 9 complete — your function vocabulary is comprehensive
blocks.content: You now have working knowledge of every major function category in Spark's library: string functions for text processing, date functions for temporal operations, numeric functions for calculations, collection functions for arrays and maps, conditional functions for branching logic, null-handling functions for data quality, and window functions for row-level analytics. You understand why built-in functions are fast, when expr() is clearer, and when a UDF is justified. In Module 10 — the final module — you will learn the Spark SQL interface, giving you a second way to express everything you have learned in Modules 1–9.

chapters.title: Spark SQL — The SQL Interface
chapters.description: Learn to use SQL alongside the DataFrame API, understand when SQL is the clearer choice, and build the hybrid SQL-plus-DataFrame pipelines that most production teams use.

blocks.content: Every module in this course has used the DataFrame API: col(), filter(), groupBy(), join(). But Spark has a second interface for the same operations: SQL. You can write SELECT, WHERE, GROUP BY, and JOIN using standard SQL syntax, and Spark executes the query across the cluster with the same Catalyst optimizer that powers the DataFrame API. The bridge between these two worlds is a view — a named reference that lets SQL queries access a DataFrame as if it were a database table. This lesson covers how to register DataFrames as views, why registration is necessary, and the NordGrid pattern for analytical SQL queries on Silver-layer data.
Why Registration Is Necessary

SQL queries reference tables by name: SELECT * FROM meter_readings WHERE region = 'NORTH'. But a PySpark DataFrame does not have a name that SQL can reference — it is a Python variable. To make a DataFrame visible to SQL, you register it as a temporary view using createOrReplaceTempView(). This gives the DataFrame a name in Spark's catalog, and from that point on, spark.sql() queries can reference it by that name. The view is a lightweight alias — it does not copy or materialize the data. It simply tells Spark's SQL engine 'when someone asks for meter_readings, use this DataFrame.'

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# Register the DataFrame as a temporary view
df.createOrReplaceTempView('meter_readings')

# Now SQL can reference it by name
result = spark.sql("SELECT * FROM meter_readings WHERE region = 'NORTH' LIMIT 5")
result.show()
createOrReplaceTempView()

The createOrReplaceTempView() method registers the DataFrame under the given name. If a view with that name already exists (from an earlier registration in the same session), it is replaced. The 'Temp' in the name means the view is tied to the current SparkSession — it disappears when the session stops. NordGrid's analytical notebooks register Silver DataFrames as views at the top of the notebook, then use SQL cells for exploration. The registration is fast because no data is moved or copied — it is purely a metadata operation that takes milliseconds.

# Register multiple views for a multi-table analysis
df_readings = spark.read.parquet('silver/readings/')
df_meters = spark.read.parquet('reference/meters/')
df_substations = spark.read.parquet('reference/substations/')

df_readings.createOrReplaceTempView('readings')
df_meters.createOrReplaceTempView('meters')
df_substations.createOrReplaceTempView('substations')

# All three are now available to SQL queries
result = spark.sql("""
    SELECT r.meter_id, r.daily_kw, m.customer_type, s.substation_name
    FROM readings r
    JOIN meters m ON r.meter_id = m.meter_id
    JOIN substations s ON m.substation_id = s.substation_id
    WHERE r.daily_kw > 2000
    LIMIT 5
""")
result.show()

+--------+--------+-------------+----------------+
|meter_id|daily_kw|customer_type|substation_name |
+--------+--------+-------------+----------------+
|   M-003|  2100.0|  residential| East Industrial|
|   M-027|  2450.3|   commercial| North Central  |
|   M-041|  3200.0|  residential| South Harbor   |
|   M-055|  2800.7|   commercial| West Campus    |
|   M-069|  2150.0|  residential| East Industrial|
+--------+--------+-------------+----------------+

blocks.title: Views are the bridge, not the destination
blocks.content: Registering a view does not change how you read or write data. You still use spark.read to load DataFrames and df.write to save them. Views exist solely to give SQL queries access to DataFrames. Think of createOrReplaceTempView() as pinning a name tag on a DataFrame so that SQL can find it. NordGrid registers views at the start of analytical sessions and uses SQL for complex queries, but the underlying data loading and writing always uses the DataFrame API.

The spark.sql() method takes a SQL query string, executes it against the registered views, and returns the result as a DataFrame. This is the core of Spark SQL: you write standard SQL, and Spark handles the distributed execution. The returned DataFrame is identical in every way to a DataFrame produced by the DataFrame API — you can filter it, transform it, join it, write it, or register it as another view. This means SQL queries are not a dead end; they are a starting point for further operations. NordGrid's analysts write SQL queries for exploration, and the data engineering team sometimes wraps those SQL queries into production pipelines when the SQL version is clearer.
The Full SQL Toolkit

Spark SQL supports the standard SQL clauses: SELECT for choosing columns, FROM for specifying tables, WHERE for filtering rows, GROUP BY for aggregation, HAVING for filtering groups, ORDER BY for sorting, and LIMIT for restricting output. It also supports subqueries, CTEs, window functions, and most SQL functions. If you have used SQL in a database like PostgreSQL, MySQL, or SQL Server, Spark SQL will feel familiar. The syntax is ANSI SQL-compatible with Spark-specific extensions for features like partition pruning and broadcast hints.

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')
df.createOrReplaceTempView('readings')

# SELECT, WHERE, ORDER BY, LIMIT
result = spark.sql("""
    SELECT meter_id, daily_kw, date, region
    FROM readings
    WHERE daily_kw > 2000 AND region = 'NORTH'
    ORDER BY daily_kw DESC
    LIMIT 10
""")

result.show()
GROUP BY and Aggregation

# GROUP BY with multiple aggregation functions
result = spark.sql("""
    SELECT 
        region,
        COUNT(*) AS reading_count,
        ROUND(SUM(daily_kw), 0) AS total_kw,
        ROUND(AVG(daily_kw), 1) AS avg_kw,
        ROUND(MIN(daily_kw), 1) AS min_kw,
        ROUND(MAX(daily_kw), 1) AS max_kw
    FROM readings
    WHERE daily_kw > 0
    GROUP BY region
    HAVING AVG(daily_kw) > 1200
    ORDER BY total_kw DESC
""")

result.show()
SQL Results Are DataFrames

The result of spark.sql() is a standard PySpark DataFrame. You can chain DataFrame operations on it, register it as another view, or write it to Parquet. This interoperability is one of Spark SQL's most powerful features: you are not locked into SQL or the DataFrame API. You can switch between them at any point in your pipeline, using whichever syntax is clearer for each step. NordGrid's Gold pipelines sometimes start with a complex SQL aggregation and then use the DataFrame API to write the result with specific partitioning.

# SQL query returns a DataFrame — use DataFrame API to write it
df_gold = spark.sql("""
    SELECT region, ROUND(SUM(daily_kw), 0) AS total_kw
    FROM readings
    WHERE daily_kw > 0
    GROUP BY region
""")

# Continue with DataFrame API
df_gold.write.mode('overwrite').parquet('gold/regional_totals/')

blocks.title: spark.sql() is an action trigger
blocks.content: Like show() and write(), spark.sql() triggers the execution of the query plan when an action is called on the resulting DataFrame. The SQL string itself is parsed and optimized lazily — calling spark.sql() builds the plan, and the plan executes when you call show(), write(), count(), or another action on the result. The same lazy evaluation principles from Module 3 apply to SQL queries.

A natural question at this point is: if SQL and the DataFrame API both produce the same results with the same performance, why learn both? The answer is that each has situations where it is the clearer, more natural choice. SQL excels at expressing complex queries that involve multiple joins, subqueries, and aggregations in a single statement — the kind of queries analysts write daily. The DataFrame API excels at composable, step-by-step transformations that are easy to test, debug, and maintain — the kind of pipelines engineers build for production. Most teams use both, and understanding the trade-offs helps you choose the right tool for each situation.
Same Query, Two Syntaxes

The following example shows the same operation — total consumption per region for readings above 1,000 kW — expressed in both SQL and the DataFrame API. Both produce identical execution plans and identical results. The difference is purely syntactic: SQL reads as a single declarative statement, while the DataFrame API reads as a chain of method calls. Neither is objectively better — the right choice depends on context, team preferences, and the complexity of the operation.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, sum as spark_sum, round as spark_round

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')
df.createOrReplaceTempView('readings')

# SQL approach
df_sql = spark.sql("""
    SELECT region, ROUND(SUM(daily_kw), 0) AS total_kw
    FROM readings
    WHERE daily_kw > 1000
    GROUP BY region
    ORDER BY total_kw DESC
""")

# DataFrame API approach — same result, same execution plan
df_api = (df
    .filter(col('daily_kw') > 1000)
    .groupBy('region')
    .agg(spark_round(spark_sum('daily_kw'), 0).alias('total_kw'))
    .orderBy(col('total_kw').desc())
)

# Prove they are identical
df_sql.show()
df_api.show()
# Both produce the same 4 rows in the same order
When SQL Is Clearer

rows: SQL's FROM/JOIN/WHERE/GROUP BY reads as a single declarative intent, while chained joins in the DataFrame API require careful ordering and ambiguity handling
SQL's WHERE x IN (SELECT ...) is concise; the DataFrame equivalent requires semi joins or intermediate variables
Analysts can write and iterate on SQL queries faster than method chains
SQL's CASE WHEN reads more naturally than a long when().when().otherwise() chain

blocks.content: When the DataFrame API Is Clearer

rows: Each transformation is a separate, testable, commentable line
Column expressions compose programmatically; SQL strings require concatenation
A Python function that accepts and returns DataFrames is composable; SQL strings are not
Column expressions benefit from autocompletion; SQL strings are opaque to the IDE

blocks.title: Same engine, different syntax
blocks.content: Spark's Catalyst optimizer compiles both SQL queries and DataFrame operations into the same internal representation. The execution plan, the parallelism, the shuffle behavior, and the performance are identical. Choosing between SQL and the DataFrame API is a readability decision, not a performance decision. NordGrid uses the DataFrame API for ETL pipelines and SQL for analytical queries — a hybrid approach that leverages the strengths of both.

Lesson 1 introduced createOrReplaceTempView(), which creates a view scoped to the current SparkSession. PySpark also provides createOrReplaceGlobalTempView(), which creates a view visible across all SparkSessions in the same Spark application. The difference is scope: temporary views are private to one session, global views are shared. At the junior level, you will almost exclusively use temporary views because most pipelines have a single SparkSession. But understanding the distinction prevents confusion when you encounter global views in production code or in Databricks notebooks where multiple sessions can coexist.
Temporary Views

A temporary view is created with createOrReplaceTempView() and is visible only within the SparkSession that created it. When the session stops, the view disappears. If two notebooks run simultaneously in the same Spark application but with different sessions, their temporary views are invisible to each other. This isolation is a safety feature: two analysts working in parallel cannot accidentally overwrite each other's views. NordGrid's standard pattern uses temporary views for all analytical queries because each notebook runs in its own session.

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# Temporary view: visible only in this session
df.createOrReplaceTempView('meter_readings')

# This works in the same session
spark.sql("SELECT COUNT(*) FROM meter_readings").show()

# A different SparkSession CANNOT see this view
Global Temporary Views

A global temporary view is created with createOrReplaceGlobalTempView() and is visible to all sessions in the same application. Global views live in a special schema called global_temp, so you must prefix the view name when querying: SELECT * FROM global_temp.meter_readings. Global views persist until the Spark application terminates (not just the session). They are used in rare scenarios where multiple sessions need to share data without re-reading from storage, such as when a main pipeline writes intermediate results that a monitoring session reads.

df = spark.read.parquet('silver/meters/')

# Global temporary view: visible to all sessions
df.createOrReplaceGlobalTempView('meter_readings')

# Must use the global_temp schema prefix
spark.sql("SELECT COUNT(*) FROM global_temp.meter_readings").show()

# Other sessions in the same application can also query it
# spark2.sql("SELECT * FROM global_temp.meter_readings")  # works

left.label: Temporary View
points: createOrReplaceTempView(name)
Visible only in the creating session
Query as: SELECT * FROM name
Disappears when the session stops
Default choice for all NordGrid pipelines

right.label: Global Temporary View
points: createOrReplaceGlobalTempView(name)
Visible to all sessions in the application
Query as: SELECT * FROM global_temp.name
Disappears when the application terminates
Rare — only for cross-session sharing

blocks.title: Use temporary views unless you have a specific cross-session need
blocks.content: NordGrid's coding standard: always use createOrReplaceTempView() unless you have a documented reason to share data between sessions. Global views introduce shared mutable state, which makes debugging harder and creates the risk of one session's view overwriting another's. In NordGrid's five years of PySpark pipelines, global views have been used in exactly three situations, all involving cross-notebook monitoring in Databricks.

The most productive way to use Spark SQL is not as a replacement for the DataFrame API but as a complement. NordGrid's production pipelines follow a hybrid pattern: the DataFrame API handles data loading, schema enforcement, and column transformations (where step-by-step composability matters), and SQL handles complex analytical queries with multi-table joins and aggregations (where declarative syntax is clearer). The key enabler is interoperability: a DataFrame can be registered as a view for SQL, and a SQL result is a DataFrame that the API can transform further. This lesson shows the hybrid workflow in practice.
The Hybrid Pipeline Pattern

The pattern has four steps. Step 1: use the DataFrame API to load data and apply transformations that are naturally step-by-step (type casting, filtering, column renaming). Step 2: register the transformed DataFrames as views. Step 3: use SQL for the complex analytical query (multi-table join with aggregation). Step 4: take the SQL result as a DataFrame and use the DataFrame API to write it. This pattern lets each tool handle the task it is best at.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, upper, trim

# STEP 1: DataFrame API for loading and cleaning
df_readings = (spark.read.parquet('silver/readings/')
    .filter(col('daily_kw') > 0)
    .filter(col('daily_kw').isNotNull())
)
df_meters = spark.read.parquet('reference/meters/')
df_substations = spark.read.parquet('reference/substations/')

# STEP 2: Register as views for SQL
df_readings.createOrReplaceTempView('readings')
df_meters.createOrReplaceTempView('meters')
df_substations.createOrReplaceTempView('substations')

# STEP 3: SQL for the complex analytical query
df_gold = spark.sql("""
    SELECT 
        s.substation_name,
        m.customer_type,
        COUNT(*) AS reading_count,
        ROUND(SUM(r.daily_kw), 0) AS total_kw,
        ROUND(AVG(r.daily_kw), 1) AS avg_kw
    FROM readings r
    JOIN meters m ON r.meter_id = m.meter_id
    JOIN substations s ON m.substation_id = s.substation_id
    GROUP BY s.substation_name, m.customer_type
    HAVING COUNT(*) > 100
    ORDER BY total_kw DESC
""")

# STEP 4: DataFrame API for writing
df_gold.coalesce(1).write.mode('overwrite').parquet('gold/substation_customer_summary/')
Chaining SQL and DataFrame Operations

You can freely alternate between SQL and DataFrame operations in the same pipeline. A SQL query returns a DataFrame that you can filter, transform, register as another view, and query again with SQL. NordGrid's regulatory reporting pipeline uses this pattern: a SQL query computes the base aggregation, the DataFrame API adds computed columns (tiered pricing, compliance flags), and a second SQL query pivots the result into the regulator's required format.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, when

# SQL for the base aggregation
df_base = spark.sql("""
    SELECT region, MONTH(date) AS month, SUM(daily_kw) AS total_kw
    FROM readings
    GROUP BY region, MONTH(date)
""")

# DataFrame API for computed columns
df_enriched = df_base.withColumn('tier',
    when(col('total_kw') > 15000000, 'high').otherwise('normal')
)

# Register the enriched result for another SQL query
df_enriched.createOrReplaceTempView('monthly_summary')

# SQL for the final pivot
df_pivot = spark.sql("""
    SELECT month, 
        SUM(CASE WHEN region = 'NORTH' THEN total_kw END) AS north,
        SUM(CASE WHEN region = 'SOUTH' THEN total_kw END) AS south,
        SUM(CASE WHEN region = 'EAST' THEN total_kw END) AS east,
        SUM(CASE WHEN region = 'WEST' THEN total_kw END) AS west
    FROM monthly_summary
    GROUP BY month
    ORDER BY month
""")

df_pivot.show(3)

blocks.title: The best pipeline uses both tools
blocks.content: Do not force yourself into SQL-only or DataFrame-only. The hybrid approach uses each tool where it shines: the DataFrame API for step-by-step ETL (loading, cleaning, column work) and SQL for complex analytical queries (multi-table joins with aggregation and pivots). The interoperability is seamless because views and DataFrames are just different names for the same underlying plans.

When a SQL query grows complex — multiple joins, nested aggregations, intermediate calculations — it becomes hard to read and maintain. Common Table Expressions (CTEs) solve this by breaking a complex query into named, sequential steps using WITH clauses. Each CTE defines a temporary result that subsequent CTEs and the final query can reference by name.
